unit.name: Aseptic Filling Machine
unit.cfj: Dispense measured volumes of sterile drug product into primary containers under aseptic conditions

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of filled product becoming microbially contaminated during the aseptic filling run
rows.plain: Prevent germs from getting into the product while it is being filled under clean conditions. This protects the product from becoming unsafe or unusable.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial contamination of filled product during the run is the definitive aseptic-job failure (relevance 10); catastrophic to patients and to release integrity (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Modern isolators, media fills and EM programs make contamination uncommon but sporadic failures still occur, so certainty is high yet not absolute. Established aseptic run protocols and RABS/isolator practice keep contamination-control routines running without adding delay, but investigation of any breach still costs the overseer significant time. Preventing contamination depends heavily on highly trained aseptic operators and validated gowning, so the outcome remains skill-dependent. A single contamination event can force a full batch rejection representing large sterile-product and rework losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a sterile product pathway breach during filling
rows.plain: Prevent any break in the route that carries the clean product during filling. A breach could allow germs into the product.
rows.imp_rat: A sterile pathway breach during filling is a direct rupture of the aseptic barrier central to the CFJ (relevance 10); consequences are non-sterile product with catastrophic impact (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Single-use assemblies and integrity testing have markedly improved pathway certainty, though undetected micro-breaches remain a residual risk. Sealed sterile pathways and pre-run integrity checks add little runtime delay for the overseer. Correct assembly and connection of the sterile path relies on trained technicians and remains error-prone at manual steps. A pathway breach wastes the whole sterile fill and any product already dispensed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recording a passing result when a safety device fails its intended protective response
rows.plain: Make sure a safety device is not marked as passing when it fails to provide the protection it should. Incorrect results could leave people exposed to danger.
rows.imp_rat: A passing result recorded when a safety device actually failed undermines the trust layer around filling operation (relevance 8); this latent falsehood can allow catastrophic protective failures to go unaddressed, risking severe injury (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Manual verification, generic pass/fail checklists, and the difficulty of simulating true fault conditions leave a real gap where devices are logged as good but would not actually trip. Safety-device functional testing is scheduled during maintenance windows but adds meaningful time, and false-pass results only surface later when duration is irrelevant. Correctly provoking and observing a protective response demands trained technicians who understand each interlock's intended behavior, a scarce and error-prone skill. A recorded false pass carries enormous downstream cost in incident liability and recalls, though the test itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterility loss in the drug-product contact path during filling
rows.plain: Keep every part of the equipment that touches the medicine free of germs while the medicine is being filled. This helps prevent the finished medicine from becoming unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: Sterility loss in the product-contact path defeats the core aseptic dispensing job (relevance 10); consequences are non-sterile parenterals with catastrophic patient and compliance impact (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Closed sterilized product paths with isolator technology make contact-path sterility loss uncommon in modern lines though never fully eliminated. RABS/isolator setup and sterilized product paths take significant validated time up front but run steadily once qualified. Maintaining sterile-path integrity depends heavily on validated CIP/SIP procedures and trained aseptic operators, leaving residual judgement dependence. Single-use assemblies and pre-sterilized paths add material cost but avoid catastrophic batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering open containers during sterile-product dispensing
rows.plain: Prevent germs or other unwanted material from getting into containers that are open while the medicine is being put into them. This helps keep the medicine safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering open containers during dispensing directly voids sterility of the delivered dose (relevance 10); severity is catastrophic given injectable patient exposure (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Isolators and RABS with barrier technology have driven contamination rates very low where properly specified, making this a mature, widely deployed practice with only residual failures. Media fills and smoke studies take weeks to plan and execute, but that time is well-accepted as part of standard qualification cadence. Specifying contamination-control capability draws on mature aseptic-processing expertise and Annex , though it still leans heavily on experienced microbiology and validation staff. The cost of media fills, environmental monitoring, and RABS/isolator specification is substantial but routinely budgeted as a known line item.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the sterile field becomes contaminated during a required filling intervention
rows.plain: Keep the clean work area free of germs while a necessary filling task is being performed. This helps prevent the medicine from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-field contamination during a required intervention is a frequent, central aseptic failure mode (relevance 9); severity high given resulting non-sterile product (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Despite training and media fills, manual interventions remain a top source of variable contamination risk. Interventions must be performed quickly to limit exposure, adding time pressure. Aseptic intervention technique is highly skill-dependent and a leading cause of contamination. A contaminated field during intervention can force batch rejection, a large potential cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that viable contamination remains in the product-contact pathway when filling begins
rows.plain: The product’s path through the equipment should be free of living germs when filling starts. This helps prevent the product from becoming contaminated during filling.
rows.imp_rat: Residual viable contamination in the pathway at filling start means the whole run is compromised from the outset (relevance 10); severity is maximal as the entire batch is non-sterile (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Validated sterilization plus routine media fills and bioburden monitoring make residual viable contamination at fill start uncommon, so this need is well served though never absolutely guaranteed. Steam-in-place and validated sterilization cycles run on fixed timers, so timing itself is controlled but adds no assurance beyond the cycle window. Cycle validation and biological indicators remove most operator judgement, though correct load configuration still depends on trained personnel. Failed sterility forces costly batch rejection and reprocessing, which validated cycles largely prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of viable contamination reaching exposed sterile product or container-contact surfaces during filling
rows.plain: Prevent living germs from reaching uncovered sterile products or surfaces that touch them during filling. This helps keep the products safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Viable contamination reaching exposed sterile product is the direct failure of the aseptic CFJ (relevance 10); a contaminated injectable can cause patient death or mass recall (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Mature barrier technology, first-air principles and validated airflow make direct viable contamination reaching product uncommon yet never zero. Barrier isolators and unidirectional airflow act continuously so there is no lag, but recovery from a breach still takes time. RABS/isolator technology plus qualified operators reduce reliance on gowning finesse, though aseptic technique still demands high skill. Isolator systems and HEPA-filtered zones are costly to install and maintain but are standard capital in this industry.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile product contamination during the form-fill-seal cycle
rows.plain: Keep the product free of germs and other unwanted material while it is being formed, filled, and sealed. This protects the product throughout the packaging process.
rows.imp_rat: For sterile form-fill-seal machinery, minimizing product contamination during the cycle is the central purpose of the job; contamination makes product unsafe and can cause patient harm or full batch recall, making both relevance and severity maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Validated aseptic FFS with isolator/RABS barriers and continuous monitoring is a mature, widely deployed practice that keeps in-cycle contamination events genuinely rare. Sterile FFS runs continuously once qualified, so the contamination-prevention measures add no meaningful cycle time during normal operation. Modern aseptic and blow-fill-seal machines encapsulate the sterile cycle so operators rely on validated automation rather than personal aseptic finesse. Isolators, RABS, HEPA-filtered zones and continuous environmental monitoring represent large ongoing cost but are standard sunk investment in this sector.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an operator intervention introduces contamination into the critical filling zone
rows.plain: Prevent a worker's hands-on action from bringing germs into the protected area where products are filled. This helps keep the products sterile.
rows.imp_rat: Operator interventions are the dominant source of aseptic-zone contamination, central to the job (relevance 9); severity is very high as human-borne microbes contaminate product (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Even validated interventions carry inherent variability because humans remain the largest contamination source. Interventions themselves take time and each one prolongs the at-risk window during a run. Contamination-free intervention hinges almost entirely on operator technique and discipline, the most skill-dependent failure mode in aseptic filling. A botched intervention can invalidate the batch, a very costly outcome.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating exposed product, containers, product-contact parts, or the sterile filling zone during jam clearance
rows.plain: Prevent germs or other contamination from reaching exposed product, containers, equipment parts, or the clean filling area while clearing a blockage. This helps keep the product safe.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during jam clearance is highly relevant since jams are routine and expose product-contact parts (relevance 8); contaminated sterile-zone product can cause recalls or patient harm (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Manual jam clearance in an open or breached sterile zone remains a chronic contamination risk that current practice only partially mitigates. Jam clearance interrupts production for extended manual intervention, and re-sterilization adds further downtime. Clearing a jam without contaminating the sterile zone demands trained, disciplined aseptic technique that is error-prone under time pressure. Contamination during clearance can scrap product and force line resanitization, a significant recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of integrity loss in the sterile product-contact path during production
rows.plain: The protective product pathway should not lose its seal while production is running. This helps keep germs out of the product.
rows.imp_rat: Integrity loss in the sterile product-contact path during production is a direct breach of the aseptic boundary (relevance 9); catastrophic since product becomes non-sterile (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: PUPSIT and post-use filter integrity testing make undetected product-path integrity loss uncommon, though assembly leaks still occur. Pre- and post-use integrity testing adds defined time to the run. Automated filter integrity testers reduce operator judgement. Single-use assemblies and integrity test equipment add material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating the sterile product pathway while clearing a material jam
rows.plain: Keep germs and other unwanted material out of the path that touches the product while removing a blockage. This protects the product during the repair.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating the sterile pathway while clearing a jam is a common intervention point central to maintaining sterility; a breach here spoils product and can trigger recall, giving very high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Manual intervention into the sterile pathway is one of the highest-risk aseptic events and remains poorly bounded by current practice. Clearing a jam usually halts the line and demands a lengthy re-sanitization or line-clearance before restart. Jam clearance inside a sterile zone is a manual, judgement-heavy intervention where technique directly determines whether the pathway stays sterile. Scrapped product around the jam plus re-sanitization consumables and downtime make each event costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of initiating filling before sterile-zone, product, container, parameter, guarding, or interlock conditions are satisfied
rows.plain: Do not start filling until the clean area, product, container, settings, protective covers, and safety checks are all ready. This prevents filling under unsafe or unsuitable conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Initiating filling before sterile-zone, guarding, or interlock conditions are satisfied strikes at the core of filling machinery's job (relevance 10); the consequences span batch loss, operator injury, and contaminated product reaching patients (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Modern filling lines embed interlock and permissive systems that reliably block start until sterile-zone, container, and parameter conditions are confirmed, though misconfiguration remains a residual gap. Automated startup interlock checks run in seconds and rarely delay production once configured. PLC-driven permissive logic and recipe management remove most operator judgement from the start decision. Preventing a bad start avoids costly aborted batches and contamination losses, so the standard practice pays for itself.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a batch with compromised sterility assurance is approved for release
rows.plain: Prevent a production run that may no longer be germ-free from being approved for release. This helps protect people from receiving a contaminated product.
rows.imp_rat: Approving a sterility-compromised batch is a disposition-side error slightly downstream of the fill act (relevance 8); severity is maximal since non-sterile product reaches patients (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Sterility tests have low sensitivity, so a compromised batch can pass, making full assurance dependent on the whole PUPSIT/EM record. Batch disposition review with sterility test incubation is slow but built into the schedule. Trained QP/QA reviewers apply defined release criteria, reducing dependence on individual judgement. Release review consumes QA labor and holding costs but is a normal cost of doing business.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of viable microbial contamination remaining on a product-contact surface after sanitation
rows.plain: Make sure no living germs remain on surfaces that touch the product after cleaning and sanitizing. This helps keep the product safe from harmful contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Viable microbial contamination remaining after sanitation is central to sterile filling integrity (relevance 8); surviving microbes on product-contact surfaces can cause life-threatening product contamination (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP/SIP and sanitation protocols are mature, but dead legs, shadowed surfaces, and imperfect coverage mean viable contamination occasionally survives, so this is only acceptably served. CIP/SIP cycles and swab-test turnaround add significant validated hold time before release to production. Effective sanitation depends heavily on correctly executed procedures and skilled hygiene staff, and residual-risk judgement is expert-dependent. Cleaning chemicals, water, energy, and any failed-swab re-clean carry real recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the sterile product-contact path loses integrity during filling
rows.plain: Keep the product’s protected path intact while the product is being filled. This prevents germs or other contamination from getting into it.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of product-contact path integrity during filling is a direct sterile-boundary breach (relevance 9); catastrophic since product becomes non-sterile (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Pre/post-use filter integrity testing and validated sterile connections make product-path integrity a mature and dependable capability with uncommon residual failures. Integrity testing of the product path is a defined step that fits standard qualification schedules. Specifying SIP, sterilizing-grade filters, and connection integrity relies on established engineering standards accessible to trained staff. Single-use assemblies and filter-integrity testing carry recurring cost that is well understood and budgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that product-contact surfaces lose sterile status during assembly
rows.plain: Keep surfaces that touch the product germ-free while the sterile pathway is being put together. This prevents contamination during assembly.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of sterile status on product-contact surfaces during assembly directly seeds later contamination (relevance 9); severity is very high though detectable before run start reduces it marginally (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Grade A/RABS environments and no-touch technique help, but manual assembly under first-air remains a recurring vulnerability that is only acceptably controlled. Aseptic assembly must be done deliberately within a limited exposure window, and rushing raises risk rather than saving meaningful time. Maintaining sterile status during assembly rests heavily on individual aseptic technique, which varies between operators despite training. A lost sterile status during assembly can scrap components and product, an expensive but not routine outcome.

rows.source_job: Inspect package seal and formation quality
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of packages with seal, dimensional, or structural defects accepted as conforming
rows.plain: Make sure fewer packages with bad seals, wrong sizes, or damaged shapes are mistakenly accepted as good. This helps prevent defective packages from being used or shipped.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting seal, dimensional, or structural defects as conforming is central because escaping defective packages defeats the seal job; a compromised seal on sterile product can allow contamination downstream, so severity is very high.
rows.sat_rat: Detection is good for gross defects but subtle seal or micro-defects still escape as false-accepted conforming units. Inline seal and vision inspection operates at line speed without adding cycle time. Automated vision and seal-integrity testing reduce dependence on operator visual judgement, though setup and sensitivity tuning need expertise. Inspection systems and false-reject scrap add cost, and marginal defects slipping through carry downstream liability.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a leak or unintended opening exposes the sterile product path during filling
rows.plain: Prevent a leak or accidental opening from exposing the product path to the surrounding air during filling. This helps keep germs out of the product.
rows.imp_rat: A leak or unintended opening exposing the sterile path during filling is a direct aseptic breach (relevance 9); severity high yielding non-sterile product (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Closed sterile paths and pressure monitoring make in-run leaks uncommon but not eliminated. Leak exposure is a continuous filling risk unrelated to task duration. Leak prevention depends on correct assembly rather than ongoing operator skill. A leak exposing the sterile path forces product disposal, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unintended operator contacts with sterile product-contact surfaces or exposed container openings
rows.plain: Reduce how often operators accidentally touch surfaces that must stay sterile or openings in containers that are exposed. This matters because such contact can contaminate the product.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended operator contacts with sterile surfaces or openings are a leading contamination vector (relevance 8); severity high as touch-transfer can non-sterilize product (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Glove-port and barrier designs reduce contacts, but human error keeps this an inconsistent risk. Careful contact-avoiding movement slows interventions somewhat. Avoiding contact with sterile surfaces depends almost entirely on trained operator technique and vigilance. An unintended contact can contaminate and cost product, but the practice itself adds little direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an aseptic-zone contamination excursion remains undetected
rows.plain: Make it less likely that harmful contamination in the sterile work area goes unnoticed. This matters because contamination can spoil the product or make it unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: An undetected aseptic-zone excursion isn't the fill act itself but governs whether contamination is caught (relevance 8); severity is severe because undetected contamination reaches patients (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Excursions can go undetected between sample points because monitoring is spatially and temporally limited despite defined schedules. Viable excursions surface only after incubation, delaying detection by days. Interpreting whether an excursion happened requires experienced microbiologists. Comprehensive EM programs add media, labor and lab cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of containers, closures, or transfer components entering filling with compromised aseptic status
rows.plain: Reduce the number of containers, caps, and transfer parts that arrive at filling without their clean, protected condition intact. This helps prevent contamination from entering the product.
rows.imp_rat: Components entering filling with compromised aseptic status directly seed contamination into the product path (relevance 9); severity high producing non-sterile units (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Validated sterilization and controlled transfer make compromised components entering rare, though transfer breaches remain a residual risk. Incoming sterilization and component prep are scheduled ahead so add little delay. Validated sterilization cycles reduce reliance on operator judgement about component status. Depyrogenation, sterilization and single-use components carry material and energy costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between measured and actual container fill quantity during accuracy checks
rows.plain: Keep the measured amount of liquid in each container as close as possible to the actual amount. This helps confirm that the filling process is giving reliable results.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between measured and actual fill quantity is central to the core job of filling machinery, since accurate dosing IS the fundamental output the machine exists to deliver (relevance 10). When this error occurs, consequences are serious: overfill wastes product and erodes margins while underfill triggers regulatory non-compliance, mislabeling penalties, and customer distrust (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Inline checkweighers reliably quantify fill discrepancy, but sampling gaps and calibration drift leave measurable uncertainty. In-process checkweighing gives near-real-time accuracy readings without stopping the line. Automated measurement reduces reliance on manual sampling and gravimetric skill. Accurate discrepancy detection limits both giveaway and rejected batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of required product-contact and film-contact surface area remaining contaminated after cleaning
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of product-touching and packaging-film-touching surface that is still contaminated after cleaning. This helps keep the next production run clean.
rows.imp_rat: Residual contamination on product- and film-contact surfaces after cleaning is highly central to sterility assurance; unremoved contamination carries forward into product with serious safety and compliance consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning protocols plus swab/ATP testing catch most residues, but shadowed and hard-to-reach contact surfaces routinely leave residual contamination gaps. CIP/COP cycles and manual cleaning follow validated timing but swab-and-verify loops add real downtime when residues persist. Effective cleaning of complex geometries and dead legs still relies on experienced technicians who know the machine's problem zones. Rework cleaning, water, chemicals and lost production accumulate when surfaces fail verification, though this is budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile product and component exposure during unplanned filling stoppages
rows.plain: Keep sterile product and parts exposed for as short a time as possible when filling unexpectedly stops. This helps reduce the chance that they become contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile product/component exposure during unplanned stoppages directly raises contamination risk (relevance 8); severity high as exposure duration enlarges at-risk product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Stoppage handling is inconsistent and product exposure during unplanned events remains poorly controlled. During unplanned stoppages, exposed product sits at risk while operators diagnose, a duration hard to bound. Managing exposure during stoppages requires quick expert decisions on hold or scrap. Prolonged stoppages often lead to product discard, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of personnel breaches of aseptic practices while accessing the critical zone
rows.plain: Have workers break the rules for keeping the product sterile as rarely as possible when they enter the most sensitive work area. This helps prevent contamination caused by people.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel aseptic-practice breaches accessing the critical zone are a top contamination cause (relevance 8); severity high as they can non-sterilize product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Personnel remain the largest contamination source, and breaches recur despite training and behavioral monitoring. Interventions happen in real time; behavioral correction is not time-bound. This need depends heavily on operator training, gowning discipline and constant vigilance which remain error-prone. Training programs and gowning consumables are ongoing costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the sterile boundary is compromised at machine-to-line interfaces
rows.plain: Keep the germ-free area sealed where the machine connects to the production line. This prevents outside contamination from entering during production.
rows.imp_rat: A compromised sterile boundary at machine-to-line interfaces is central because these junctions are frequent breach points; loss of the sterile boundary results in contaminated product with major safety and compliance harm.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practices like media fills and connection qualification help, but machine-to-line interfaces remain a recurring weak point where breaches still occur. Verifying sterile boundary integrity at line interfaces is time-consuming and often extends aseptic commissioning. It demands scarce aseptic-engineering expertise to correctly interpret interface sealing and airflow, making outcomes expert-dependent. Failures drive costly media-fill reruns and rework, and mitigation hardware is expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of primary containers or closures presented for filling with compromised sterility
rows.plain: Present only containers and closures that are still free from germs for filling. This helps keep the product safe from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Containers/closures presented with compromised sterility directly contaminate the delivered dose (relevance 9); severity high as it produces non-sterile filled units (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Validated component sterilization is robust, though occasional compromised containers still reach the feed point. Depyrogenation tunnels and closure sterilization run inline without major delay to the operator. Component sterility is engineered into upstream processes, requiring little operator finesse during filling. Pre-sterilized components and inline tunnels carry cost but are standard for the process.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surface area retaining biological, chemical, or allergen residue after decontamination
rows.plain: Remove biological material, chemicals, and allergy-causing residue from all surfaces that touch the product.
rows.imp_rat: Residual biological, chemical, or allergen residue after decontamination is central to fit-for-use filling surfaces (relevance 8); retained allergen or chemical residue can cause cross-contamination with serious health consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP/COP plus swab and rinse testing make residue control fairly consistent, though allergen and biofilm residues on complex geometries still occasionally persist. Validated cleaning cycles run to fixed durations, adding predictable but non-trivial time to maintenance. Once cleaning is validated, execution needs modest skill, though manual disassembly cleaning still depends on operator diligence. Cleaning agents, water, and swab/rinse testing consume resources but are a well-budgeted routine expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of open-container and sterile-product-path exposure during an intervention
rows.plain: Keep containers and the paths carrying germ-free product exposed for as little time as possible during an intervention. This reduces the chance that germs can enter.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of open-container and path exposure during intervention scales contamination probability (relevance 8); severity high as longer exposure enlarges at-risk product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Exposure duration varies widely by operator and situation, making this inconsistently controlled. Open-container exposure duration during interventions is directly driven by manual speed and remains a persistent concern. Minimizing exposure time depends on rehearsed, skilled intervention execution. Extended exposure risks product loss but the time-minimizing effort itself is low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of viable contamination remaining on product-contact surfaces at run start
rows.plain: Make sure no living germs remain on surfaces that touch the product when a run begins. This helps keep the product safe from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Viable contamination on product-contact surfaces at run start seeds the batch from the beginning (relevance 9); severity high given potential batch-wide non-sterility (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Validated sterilization cycles plus release checks make residual viable contamination at start rare, giving good confidence. Validated SIP/CIP cycles run to schedule and rarely delay run start. Sterilization is largely automated and validated, reducing dependence on operator judgement at start. A failed pre-run sterility check forces reprocessing with modest added cost relative to a lost batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of critical-zone airborne contamination beyond validated sterility-assurance limits during filling
rows.plain: Keep airborne contamination in the most sensitive filling area within the level shown to protect the product from germs. This helps maintain the product's cleanliness during filling.
rows.imp_rat: Critical-zone airborne contamination beyond sterility limits directly threatens exposed product (relevance 9); severity high given it can render fills non-sterile across the run (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Validated Grade A airflow with continuous non-viable monitoring keeps airborne contamination consistently within limits, though viable results lag incubation. Continuous particle counters give near-real-time readings during filling. Automated monitoring reduces the skill needed to detect airborne excursions. Particle counting and viable air sampling equipment carry recurring media and calibration costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of departures from required aseptic conditions in the critical filling zone
rows.plain: If the clean conditions in the main filling area are lost, restore them as quickly as possible. A shorter loss of protection reduces the chance of contaminating the product.
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged departures from aseptic conditions in the critical zone directly raise contamination exposure of product (relevance 9); severity high as duration scales the amount of at-risk product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Airflow monitoring and pressure alarms flag departures, but restoring conditions quickly is not consistently achieved. The core of this need is shortening how long aseptic conditions are lost, and current recovery relies on operator response times that vary considerably. Rapid restoration of grade A conditions depends on experienced operators recognizing and correcting the excursion. Every minute of excursion increases product at risk and potential discard cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical contamination control departs from established limits without escalation
rows.plain: Make sure an important contamination safeguard does not go outside its allowed limits without someone being alerted. This allows the problem to be handled before it becomes serious.
rows.imp_rat: A critical contamination control drifting out of limits without escalation lets contamination proceed unchecked (relevance 8); severity severe as it can release non-sterile product (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Critical parameters often have automated alarms, but not all contamination controls are instrumented, leaving escalation gaps. Alarm escalation is immediate where automated triggers exist, delayed where manual. Defined escalation SOPs reduce reliance on operator judgement about when to escalate. Alarm and SCADA systems are established infrastructure cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into sterile product during replenishment
rows.plain: Prevent germs or other unwanted material from entering the sterile product while more product is added.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination during replenishment directly defeats the sterile-fill job whenever refill happens; the consequence is a contaminated batch and possible recall, keeping severity very high though it is scoped to a specific operational moment.
rows.sat_rat: Replenishment breaches the barrier and is a recognized higher-risk intervention where residual contamination events still occur despite RTPs and procedures. Replenishing components or bulk into a sterile line typically forces a slow, deliberate transfer sequence that interrupts steady-state flow. Correct use of transfer ports, rapid-transfer ports and aseptic technique during replenishment still depends heavily on trained operator discipline. RTP systems and pre-sterilized consumables add cost but are the accepted means of protecting the transfer.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating the sterile product path during deviation resolution
rows.plain: Avoid introducing germs into the sterile product path while fixing a problem during filling. This protects the product while the issue is being resolved.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating the sterile path during deviation resolution is a real intervention-driven risk (relevance 8); severity high as it can non-sterilize affected product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Outcomes during deviation resolution are variable and depend heavily on the specific event and operator. Deviation resolution takes time during which sterility is at stake. Resolving deviations without contaminating requires senior aseptic expertise. A contaminated path during resolution can force batch loss, high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that aseptic conditions are compromised during disruption recovery
rows.plain: Keep the product and work area protected from germs while recovering from a disruption. Restarting the process should not weaken the clean conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Compromised aseptic conditions during disruption recovery is a high-risk contamination window (relevance 8); severity high as recovery-phase product can be non-sterile (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Intervention procedures exist but disruptions remain a high-variability risk where aseptic conditions are often compromised. Disruption recovery inherently interrupts flow and extends downtime. Recovering aseptically after a disruption demands experienced judgement about acceptable interventions. Recovery interventions frequently sacrifice exposed product, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of open-container exposure during an unplanned filling stoppage
rows.plain: Keep containers exposed to the surrounding air for as little time as possible when filling stops unexpectedly. This reduces the chance that germs reach the product.
rows.imp_rat: Open-container exposure duration during stoppage directly scales contamination probability (relevance 8); severity high as longer exposure risks more units (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance rules exist but exposure duration during unplanned stoppages varies widely. Reducing open-container exposure during stoppage is fundamentally a speed-of-response problem that current manual procedures handle inconsistently. Deciding to reject exposed units requires trained judgement under pressure. Extended exposure means discarded units and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an interlock fails to stop hazardous motion when a guard opens
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment stops moving when someone opens a safety guard. This helps prevent people from being injured by moving parts.
rows.imp_rat: An interlock failing to stop hazardous motion when a guard opens is central to safe machine operation and, when it occurs, can cause severe crush/amputation injuries or death, so severity is catastrophic and relevance high.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant, self-monitoring interlock architectures with mandated functional testing make undetected stop failures rare on properly built modern machines. Interlock verification adds modest structured time to commissioning that is well-absorbed into standard startup schedules. Certified safety-circuit design and testing per ISO 13849/IEC embedded in machine build, reducing reliance on the installer's own judgement. Rated safety components and validation labor carry cost but are standard line items rather than surprise overspend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of containers or closures entering the filling zone with compromised sterile status
rows.plain: Reduce the number of containers and closures that enter the filling area with their sterile protection already damaged. This helps prevent the product from being contaminated during filling.
rows.imp_rat: Containers/closures entering the zone with compromised sterile status directly contaminate delivered product (relevance 9); severity high yielding non-sterile units (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Validated tunnels and sealed transfer of sterile components make compromised containers entering the zone uncommon. Container and closure sterilization runs upstream in parallel so it rarely delays filling. Automated depyrogenation tunnels and component sterilizers reduce reliance on operator skill. Compromised containers cause rejects, but validated feed systems keep this loss low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile-path connections remain incompletely sealed after assembly
rows.plain: Make sure every connection in the sterile product path is fully closed after assembly. This prevents leaks or openings that could let contamination reach the product.
rows.imp_rat: Incompletely sealed sterile-path connections create leak points that breach the sterile boundary (relevance 8); severity severe as it can non-sterilize the batch (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic connectors and post-assembly checks reduce open seals, but incompletely sealed connections still occasionally reach readiness. Verifying seals adds modest assembly time within the setup window. Achieving fully seated sterile connections depends on assembler technique and torque feel. An incomplete seal can leak and scrap product, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sterile-path or critical-zone breach remains at readiness release
rows.plain: Make sure no break in the sterile or protected work area is still present when the setup is approved as ready. This helps prevent contamination from going unnoticed before work begins.
rows.imp_rat: A breach persisting at readiness release means filling starts with compromised sterility (relevance 8); severity high as it can contaminate the full batch before detection (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and second-person verification catch most breaches, but subtle integrity gaps still occasionally pass readiness release. Readiness checks add defined time before release but are built into the schedule. Detecting a residual breach at release relies on experienced inspection and checklist discipline that can miss subtle defects. A breach released to production risks whole-batch loss, so undetected ones are very costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that aseptic conditions are compromised during equipment configuration changes
rows.plain: Make sure changing the equipment setup does not expose the product to contamination. This keeps the product protected while parts are rearranged or replaced.
rows.imp_rat: Compromised aseptic conditions during equipment configuration changes is a known contamination window (relevance 8); severity high as it can non-sterilize subsequent product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Procedures and RABS help, but configuration changes remain an intervention-heavy risk that is only moderately controlled. Configuration changes stop production and take time to complete aseptically. Maintaining asepsis during configuration changes leans heavily on operator technique and discipline. A compromised change can scrap the setup and product, a notable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that exposed containers or the sterile product path become contaminated during the pause
rows.plain: Keep open containers and the path carrying germ-free product from picking up germs during a pause. This protects the product from becoming unsafe or spoiled.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of exposed containers or the sterile path during a pause is relevant because pauses are common (relevance 7); breach of sterility can compromise entire batches with patient-safety consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Barrier isolators and RABS help, but exposed containers during a pause still present a recurring contamination risk that is only moderately controlled. Pauses trigger holding procedures that consume time but are procedurally defined. Maintaining sterility during an unplanned pause relies heavily on operator vigilance and aseptic discipline. A compromised pause can force product rejection or re-sterilization, creating avoidable loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical machine surfaces with viable contamination at filling start
rows.plain: Reduce the number of important machine surfaces that have living germs on them when filling starts. This helps prevent the product from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Viable contamination on critical machine surfaces at start is a leading root cause of run contamination (relevance 9); severity high as it can contaminate the entire batch (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Validated sterilization and integrity checks make viable contamination at start rare where standard practice is followed. Sterilization cycles and pre-use assembly verification add setup time before filling can start. Confirming all critical surfaces are sterile relies on validated cycles more than operator skill, reducing dependence on expertise. Autoclaving and single-use sterile components consume resources but are routine and budgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrect fill product or batch is loaded into the filling machine supply
rows.plain: Prevent the wrong product or batch from being put into the filling machine. This helps ensure the machine fills containers with the intended product.
rows.imp_rat: Loading incorrect fill product or batch into the supply is highly central to the filling job's correctness (relevance 8); wrong product filled and shipped is a serious mix-up with recall and patient-safety exposure (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Material scanning and MES enforcement catch most wrong-product loads, but manual handling steps still leave a real residual error path. Barcode/recipe verification of incoming product adds negligible time to changeover. Scan-based material verification reduces dependence on operator memory or manual checking. Loading the wrong batch causes total batch loss, so verification investment is justified.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contacts transferring contamination from non-aseptic surfaces to critical surfaces during filling
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times workers or objects carry dirt or germs from ordinary surfaces onto surfaces that must stay sterile during filling. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contacts transferring contamination from non-aseptic to critical surfaces are a core contamination vector (relevance 8); severity high as they non-sterilize product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: No-touch technique and glove ports help, but operator-driven contact contamination during filling remains a persistent variable risk. Avoiding contaminating contacts does not change task duration materially. Preventing non-aseptic-to-critical contacts is fundamentally a continuous manual technique and vigilance challenge. Each contamination contact risks product loss, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricant entering sterile or product-contact areas during or after application
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that oil or grease gets into clean areas or areas that touch the product during or after application. This helps keep the product and production area clean.
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant entering sterile or product-contact areas is a recognized contamination route central to sterile machinery integrity; when it occurs it adulterates product and can force a recall, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Use of H1 lubricants, sealed bearings and defined lube points controls most risk, yet over-application and migration still occasionally contaminate product-contact areas. Food-grade lubrication procedures are quick but require careful metered application that adds steps to PM. Preventing migration into product zones depends on technician discipline in quantity and placement, an error-prone manual skill. Food-grade lubricants cost more and contamination events cause product scrap, but usage volumes are small.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the sterile boundary loses integrity between cycle completion and filling
rows.plain: Make sure the clean, protected liquid path stays intact from the end of the cleaning process until filling begins. This helps stop germs from getting into the product.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-boundary integrity loss between sterilization and filling means filling begins compromised (relevance 8); severity severe with batch-wide non-sterility (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Validated clean hold times and closed systems generally preserve the boundary, though extended holds introduce some risk. Minimizing the gap between cycle end and filling is scheduled but hold times add waiting. Managing sterile hold requires procedural discipline more than deep skill. A lost boundary in this window scraps sterilized assemblies, a notable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unplanned interventions that disturb sterile product-contact surfaces during filling
rows.plain: Reduce unexpected work during filling that could disturb surfaces that touch the clean product. This helps prevent the product from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned interventions disturbing product-contact surfaces are a major contamination trigger (relevance 8); severity high given resulting non-sterile product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Robotics and gloveless isolators reduce interventions, but unplanned ones still disturb sterile surfaces on conventional lines. Unplanned interventions cause immediate line stoppage delays. Automation reduces manual interventions, but line clearance judgement is skilled. Interventions cause product loss and downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile product exposure to nonaseptic conditions during filling interruptions
rows.plain: Reduce how long a germ-free product is exposed to unclean surroundings when filling is interrupted. This helps keep the product safe from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile product exposure to non-aseptic conditions during interruptions directly raises contamination odds (relevance 8); severity high as exposure duration scales risk to product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Hold-time limits exist but consistent rapid protection of exposed product during unplanned pauses is not assured. This need is fundamentally about shortening exposure duration during interruptions, and current hold-time responses are often slow and manual. Deciding how to protect exposed product during a stoppage relies on operator and supervisor judgement. Prolonged exposure typically forces discard of exposed sterile product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that airborne contamination reaches exposed product or critical surfaces during filling
rows.plain: Keep germs and particles in the air from reaching exposed products or important clean surfaces during filling. This helps keep the products free from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Airborne contamination reaching exposed product is a primary contamination route during filling (relevance 9); severity high as it non-sterilizes filled units (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Isolators and RABS with validated first-air deliver strong, consistent protection of exposed product, making this one of the better-served needs. Airflow protection is continuous during filling so it imposes no added task time. Unidirectional airflow does the work automatically, reducing dependence on operator skill once setup is correct. Airflow systems are capital-intensive but their steady operation avoids contamination losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of fill parameters set outside validated limits for the intended product, container, and production run
rows.plain: Keep filling settings within the approved range for the product, container, and production run. This helps ensure the right amount is put into each container.
rows.imp_rat: Fill parameters outside validated limits directly compromise the machine's central task of accurate, compliant filling (relevance 9); out-of-spec runs cause rejected batches and regulatory deviations, though usually detectable before release (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Modern fillers with password-protected recipe profiles and enforced parameter ceilings make out-of-limit settings uncommon on standard equipment, but manual overrides and legacy machines still let deviations through. Loading recipe limits and verifying parameters against validated ranges takes measurable setup time that HMI recipe-lock features have shortened but not eliminated. Recipe management systems with parameter bounds reduce reliance on operator judgement, though a knowledgeable technician must still author and approve the validated limits. Out-of-limit parameters mainly cost scrapped trial product and rework, which recipe-locking largely contains.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of operator glove or garment integrity breaches during filling
rows.plain: Prevent gloves or protective clothing from tearing or developing openings while the product is being filled. This helps keep people and the product from spreading contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Glove or garment integrity breaches expose the sterile field to human microflora, a central risk (relevance 8); severity high as breaches can contaminate product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Periodic glove integrity testing catches many breaches but micro-breaches during filling can go undetected. Glove integrity testing between batches consumes time but is scheduled. Detecting breaches relies on operator awareness and periodic testing rather than continuous certainty. Glove replacement and testing are modest recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an aseptic gown or glove barrier is compromised during filling
rows.plain: Prevent the protective clothing or gloves used during filling from tearing, leaking, or otherwise losing their protection. This helps keep the product safe from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: A compromised gown/glove barrier during filling exposes the sterile field to operator contamination (relevance 8); severity high given resulting product contamination (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Glove-port and isolator designs help, but gown and glove barrier compromise during extended filling remains a real recurring risk. Glove integrity is a continuous exposure across the whole fill so no time saving applies. Barrier integrity during filling depends on careful operator movement and glove-port technique, a persistent skill demand. A compromised barrier can scrap product and force gowning replacement, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between measured fill volume and the approved target after calibration
rows.plain: Make the amount put into each container as close as possible to the approved target after adjustment. This helps keep fills consistent and accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between measured fill volume and target after calibration is the literal core deliverable of filling machinery (relevance 10); consequences are wasted product, underfill complaints, or overfill loss, generally correctable per run (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Post-calibration deviation is tightly controlled on volumetric and net-weight fillers with feedback, yet product viscosity and temperature drift keep residual variance a recurring reality. Calibration against a target fill volume is a routine timed procedure with established check-weigh loops, so time cost is predictable. Achieving tight deviation still leans on an experienced setter feeling out pump/valve behavior, though checkweigher feedback reduces that dependence. Deviation from target directly drives giveaway or underfill rejects, a well-understood cost that calibration routines actively minimize.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of hazardous filling machine motion or dispensing after emergency-stop activation
rows.plain: Dangerous movement or product dispensing should stop as quickly as possible after the emergency stop is pressed. This helps reduce the chance and severity of injury.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous motion continuing after e-stop is central to safe operation of filling machinery (relevance 8); failure to halt promptly causes crush/entanglement injuries that are irreversible (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Safety-rated stop categories per ISO 13849 make rapid, deterministic stopping a mature and consistently deployed practice. Category-rated E-stop circuits cut hazardous motion within milliseconds to a fraction of a second, which is the whole point of the function. Stopping requires no skill from the operator beyond pressing the button, and standards prescribe the stopping performance. E-stop hardware is inexpensive and mandatory, so cost is not a barrier to good performance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between the onset of an aseptic breach and its recognition
rows.plain: Reduce the time it takes to notice that germs may have entered a protected process. Faster recognition helps limit the effect of the problem.
rows.imp_rat: Time to recognize an aseptic breach determines how much product is filled under compromise (relevance 7); severity high since delay expands the non-sterile quantity (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Continuous particle and pressure monitoring catch some breaches immediately, but microbial breaches are inherently delayed in detection. The need targets the detection lag itself, and many aseptic breaches are still recognized only after the fact via EM results days later. Early recognition depends on vigilant operators noticing subtle deviations, a demanding attentional load. Late recognition expands the volume of product that must be discarded.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a deteriorating mechanical, pneumatic, electrical, or dosing condition remains undetected before failure
rows.plain: Notice worsening problems with the machine or the amount being delivered before they cause a breakdown. This helps people fix the problem in time.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected deteriorating mechanical/dosing conditions relate to sustaining reliable filling (relevance 7); undetected drift culminates in unplanned failure, contaminated or off-spec batches (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Standard periodic inspection catches some conditions but subtle pneumatic, electrical, or dosing drift frequently escapes detection until failure. Condition monitoring can catch degradation early but most sites still run time-based intervals, so deterioration is often found only at the next scheduled stop. Interpreting vibration, pressure, or dosing drift trends into a reliable failure prediction still relies heavily on experienced maintenance judgement. Predictive sensors and analytics exist but their cost limits deployment to critical assets, leaving much of the machine on cheaper reactive practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that personnel can reach hazardous filling machine motion through or around installed guarding
rows.plain: Set up the protective barriers so people cannot reach the machine’s dangerous moving parts from any side. This helps prevent injuries during operation.
rows.imp_rat: Reachability of hazardous motion around guarding is relevant to safe machine operation (relevance 7); operator access to moving parts causes severe, potentially fatal injuries (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Machine-directive-compliant guarding with interlocks and validated safety distances is mature and widely deployed, so reachable hazardous motion is rare where standards are applied. Guarding verification is a defined inspection step that adds little time at commissioning. Standardized reach and gap assessments to recognized guarding standards reduce reliance on individual judgement. Compliant fixed and interlocked guarding is a one-time capital cost with negligible recurring spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a malfunctioning guard, interlock, emergency-stop circuit, or light curtain is accepted before machine release
rows.plain: Do not release the machine until all protective devices work properly, including guards, automatic safety checks, emergency stopping, and light-based barriers. This helps protect people from moving or dangerous equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing a machine with a malfunctioning guard, interlock, e-stop, or light curtain undermines the entire safety system; when it occurs the protective layers are absent, risking severe operator injury, so severity is near-catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: FAT/SAT protocols catch most malfunctioning guards, e-stops and light curtains, but occasional acceptance of a marginal device still slips through when tests are rushed. Pre-release functional safety checks are scheduled into the acceptance protocol without major delay. Trained safety technicians follow documented test sequences, though correct judgement of pass/fail still depends on their competence. Test instrumentation and downtime are budgeted acceptance costs rather than waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of foreign matter introduced into the sterile path during assembly
rows.plain: Keep dirt, particles, and germs from getting into the germ-free product path while it is being put together. This helps keep the product safe from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign matter introduced into the sterile path during assembly compromises both sterility and particulate quality (relevance 8); severity high for injectable safety though often caught at inspection (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: HEPA-filtered zones, pre-sterilized components and gowning reduce particulate ingress well, yet occasional visible-particle rejects show residual gaps. Careful particle-free handling slows assembly slightly but timing is not the core issue. Avoiding foreign matter depends on disciplined technique and vigilance that trained operators mostly but not always maintain. Particle contamination triggers rejects and investigations, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that environmental monitoring activity introduces contamination into the aseptic zone
rows.plain: Make sure checking the surrounding air and surfaces for germs does not itself introduce contamination into the protected area. This keeps the checking process from putting the product at risk.
rows.imp_rat: Environmental monitoring itself introducing contamination is an ironic but real intervention risk (relevance 7); severity high as it can non-sterilize product during sampling (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Trained sampling reduces but does not eliminate monitoring-induced contamination, a recognized false-positive source. Sampling is brief but the intrusion window is real-time. Aseptic sampling technique is highly skill-dependent and a known contamination source. Sampling labor and media are recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of critical-zone protective airflow outside validated conditions during filling
rows.plain: Shorten the time that the protective clean-air flow is outside its proven safe range during filling. This helps keep harmful particles away from the product.
rows.imp_rat: Protective airflow outside validated conditions removes the physical barrier protecting exposed product (relevance 8); severity high as it enables airborne contamination of product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Continuous pressure and airflow monitoring keeps protective airflow within validated conditions most of the time. Airflow recovery time is measured but excursions persist until corrected. Smoke studies establish airflow patterns, reducing per-run judgement. HVAC and airflow validation are significant recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contamination hazards unaddressed by specified aseptic capabilities
rows.plain: Make sure the planned sterile-working abilities address all known ways products could become contaminated. This helps prevent contamination during production.
rows.imp_rat: Unaddressed contamination hazards leave gaps in the aseptic control design (relevance 7); severity high since an uncontrolled hazard can cause non-sterile product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Contamination-control strategy frameworks now push toward systematic hazard coverage, but gaps in less-obvious hazards still recur, leaving this only acceptably served. Building a comprehensive hazard inventory extends the specification timeline but is generally schedulable. Completeness depends heavily on the specifier's experience to foresee non-obvious hazards, which is scarce and inconsistent across teams. Missed hazards surface late as expensive rework or field deviations, imposing unpredictable downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containers or closures that lose sterile status before presentation for filling
rows.plain: Reduce the number of containers or covers that are no longer free from germs before they are brought to the filling step. Keeping them clean helps prevent the product from being contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Containers/closures losing sterile status before presentation feed compromised components into filling (relevance 8); severity high yielding non-sterile units (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Ready-to-use sterile components and validated transfer systems have substantially improved certainty of maintained sterility. Sterile component handling is scheduled into the flow without added delay when tubs/nests are used. Maintaining sterile status of components in transit relies on trained handling and transfer discipline. Components losing sterility before filling become waste, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the product-contact pathway isolated from sterilization conditions
rows.plain: Make sure nearly all surfaces and routes that touch the product receive the required sterilizing treatment. This reduces the chance that the product will be contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Any product-contact pathway isolated from sterilization is a guaranteed non-sterile surface (relevance 7); severity severe as it directly yields non-sterile product (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: SIP path design and validation minimize isolated segments, though complex assemblies can still harbor dead legs. Ensuring full pathway exposure is designed into the cycle, not a time burden. Correct assembly to avoid isolated dead legs depends on trained setup. Isolated segments failing sterilization risk batch loss, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of critical aseptic parameters crossing approved boundaries during filling
rows.plain: Keep key cleanliness and operating conditions within their approved limits while filling. This helps maintain a clean, controlled process.
rows.imp_rat: Critical aseptic parameters crossing approved boundaries during filling erodes sterility assurance (relevance 8); severity high as excursions can compromise product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Validated setpoints with automated alarms keep critical aseptic parameters within bounds reliably during most runs. Real-time process control acts fast on parameter drift. Automated interlocks reduce dependence on operator vigilance. Automation and control systems are standard capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between measured fill quantity and the approved target after adjustment
rows.plain: Make the amount put into each container as close as possible to the approved target after making an adjustment. This helps ensure each container has the right amount of product.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between measured fill quantity and target after adjustment is core to delivering accurate fills (relevance 9); post-adjustment error yields off-spec units, usually caught before release (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Servo-driven volumetric and net-weight fillers with feedback achieve target consistently for most products, though difficult products still drift. Closed-loop weight feedback allows adjustment to target within a short verification cycle. Automated checkweigher feedback reduces the operator finesse needed to hit target after adjustment. Tight adjustment reduces giveaway and out-of-spec scrap, a direct cost benefit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of nonconforming filled containers accepted as conforming
rows.plain: Reduce the number of filled containers with quality problems that are mistakenly accepted as good. This helps keep unsuitable products from moving forward.
rows.imp_rat: Nonconforming units accepted as conforming is a direct escape of defective product (relevance 7); severity high as bad product reaches patients (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Inspection and checkweigh systems reduce false accepts but a residual escape rate persists, especially for subtle defects. Acceptance decisions occur inline without slowing the run. Correct classification depends on sensor calibration and, for visual defects, inspector judgement. Accepting nonconforming units risks costly complaints or recalls downstream.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that prior-batch product or container remnants remain after changeover
rows.plain: Make sure no product or container pieces from the previous batch remain after changing to a new batch. This helps prevent old material from mixing with the new batch.
rows.imp_rat: Prior-batch remnants after changeover cause cross-contamination and carryover, central to safe filling (relevance 8); severity high given mix-up/contamination patient risk (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning and line-clearance checks make residual remnants after changeover uncommon. Cleaning verification during changeover adds defined time. Confirming no prior-batch remnants relies on inspection thoroughness and cleaning-validation adherence. Cross-contamination remnants can force batch rejection, a serious cost that cleaning avoids.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of trapped air or condensate shielding product-contact surfaces from sterilization conditions
rows.plain: Reduce trapped air or water buildup that could shield surfaces touching the product from the sterilizing treatment. This helps ensure those surfaces are properly sterilized.
rows.imp_rat: Trapped air/condensate shielding surfaces from sterilization leaves un-sterilized product-contact areas (relevance 7); severity severe as it silently produces non-sterile pathways (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Validated loading patterns and steam-trap/vent design manage air and condensate reliably in qualified cycles. Proper drainage and venting are handled by sterilizer cycle design without extra operator time. Correct load orientation to avoid air pockets depends on trained assembly practice. Trapped air causing sterilization failure scraps loads, an avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that intervention materials remain inside the aseptic filling area after filling resumes
rows.plain: Remove all materials used during the work before sterile filling starts again. This helps prevent those materials from contaminating the filling area or its contents.
rows.imp_rat: Intervention materials left in the aseptic area after resume act as contamination sources (relevance 7); severity high as they can non-sterilize ongoing product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Tool accountability checklists work well but are still a manual, error-prone verification. Post-intervention material accounting adds a verification step and time. Ensuring no materials remain relies on operator diligence and checklists. Left-behind materials risk batch loss but the control itself is low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product-path, filling, or container-handling components assembled incorrectly
rows.plain: Fewer parts that carry, fill, or hold the product should be put together incorrectly. Correct assembly helps the process run safely and properly.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly assembled product-path/handling components can breach sterility or cause fill faults (relevance 7); severity high as assembly errors can non-sterilize the batch (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Assembly SOPs and verification catch most errors, though incorrect assemblies still occasionally reach operation. Correct assembly takes structured time within changeover. Assembling many components correctly depends heavily on operator training and reference to instructions. Incorrect assembly causes rework and scrap, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering the sterile product path during idling
rows.plain: Keep germs or other unwanted material from getting into the path where the sterile product travels while the machine is stopped.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering the sterile path during idling matters to sterility but idling is a lower-exposure state than active filling, so relevance is moderate while a breach still severely compromises product safety.
rows.sat_rat: Idle-state protection relies on maintaining sterile barriers and positive pressure, but ingress during unplanned stops remains a persistent, hard-to-fully-eliminate risk. Restarting after idle requires flush and integrity checks that consume time before production can resume. Judging whether an idled sterile path is still safe depends heavily on operator experience and disciplined procedure adherence. Contamination during idling can force scrapping of product runs, a significant recurring cost when it happens.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual material obstructing product-contact surface exposure during sterilization
rows.plain: Reduce leftover material that blocks the product-contact surfaces from being fully exposed during sterilization. This helps ensure those surfaces are properly sterilized.
rows.imp_rat: Residual material obstructing surface exposure during sterilization leaves non-sterilized surfaces (relevance 7); severity severe as it silently produces contaminated product paths (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning cycles reliably expose product-contact surfaces before sterilization. Cleaning before sterilization is a scheduled upstream step adding no direct fill delay. Adequate cleaning to expose surfaces relies on validated procedures more than in-the-moment skill. Residual material blocking sterilization scraps loads, a controllable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safety functions that fail to bring the filling machine to the required safe state during commissioning
rows.plain: Reduce the number of safety features that fail to put the filling machine into its required safe condition during setup. This helps protect people and equipment before regular use begins.
rows.imp_rat: Safety functions failing to reach a safe state during commissioning is relevant to establishing safe filling operation (relevance 7); an undetected commissioning gap leaves persistent injury exposure in production (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized safety PLCs, validated stop circuits, and formal commissioning test protocols mean safety functions reliably reach safe state, with failures caught before handover in most cases. Commissioning safety-function tests follow a defined checklist that is time-bounded and scheduled. Verifying safe-state behavior requires a qualified commissioning engineer familiar with the safety system. A failed safety function found at commissioning costs engineering time to rectify but averts far larger downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile-zone conditions are outside approved limits at startup
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the clean production area is outside its approved conditions when work begins. This helps protect the product from being spoiled or contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-zone conditions outside approved limits at startup are central to a compliant sterile run; running out of spec invalidates the batch and risks contaminated product reaching patients, keeping severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Automated startup interlocks and monitored release-to-run criteria are well established, making out-of-limit starts uncommon though not impossible. Startup checks and environmental settle/verification add defined lead time before production can begin. Interlocked startup sequences and automated condition checks reduce reliance on the operator remembering every prerequisite. Pre-start environmental monitoring and any aborted starts consume resources but are routine and budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of aseptic boundary breaches during simulated personnel interventions
rows.plain: Prevent breaks in the protective barrier against contamination when workers practice reaching into or working around the process. This helps show that the process can remain clean during real work.
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic boundary breaches during simulated interventions reveal process robustness gaps (relevance 7); severity high as they predict real-run contamination risk (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Intervention lists in media fills are defined, but boundary breaches during simulation still reveal recurring technique gaps. Simulated interventions occur in scheduled media fills, not time-critical. Correct intervention technique depends on operator skill that remains variable. Media fill simulations consume line and material resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of departure of filling-zone airflow or pressure from aseptic limits during filling
rows.plain: Keep the movement and pressure of air in the filling area within the limits needed to prevent contamination. This protects the product while it is being filled.
rows.imp_rat: Filling-zone airflow/pressure departure from aseptic limits weakens the protective barrier for product (relevance 8); severity high as it enables product contamination (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Continuous differential-pressure and airflow monitoring with alarms keep departures from limits well controlled. Airflow and pressure are monitored continuously with no added task time. Automated pressure differential monitoring reduces reliance on operator judgement. Excursions can invalidate portions of a batch, a meaningful cost that alarms help avoid.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of environmental separation loss between the critical filling zone and surrounding areas
rows.plain: Reduce how long the sterile filling area loses its separation from nearby areas. Shorter loss of separation lowers the chance that contamination reaches the product.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of environmental separation between critical zone and surroundings admits contamination (relevance 7); severity high since ingress can compromise product sterility (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Pressure differential monitoring and interlocks make prolonged separation loss uncommon. Door interlocks and rapid transfer ports minimize the duration of separation loss. Reestablishing separation follows procedures with limited skill dependence. Barrier hardware carries capital cost but limits ongoing exposure loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of filled containers whose closures permit contaminant ingress
rows.plain: Keep filled containers sealed so dirt or germs cannot get inside. This helps protect what is inside from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Closures permitting contaminant ingress undermine post-fill sterility maintenance (relevance 7); severity high as breached container-closure integrity yields non-sterile product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: CCI testing and torque controls catch many defects, yet ingress-permitting closures still slip through, so certainty is moderate. Closure application runs at line speed without adding overseer time when working correctly. Capping/sealing setup requires trained setup but is largely mechanized once dialed in. Containers with leaking closures become rejects, a recurring but bounded loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of filled containers with compromised closure integrity
rows.plain: Keep the number of filled containers with loose or damaged seals as low as possible. This helps prevent the contents from leaking or becoming contaminated.
