rows.imp_rat: Compromised closure integrity defeats sterility maintenance of the finished unit (relevance 7); severity high as it produces non-sterile product post-fill (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Inline and offline CCI methods make gross seal failures detectable, though marginal defects remain a persistent residual rate. Seal-integrity checks are integrated and do not materially slow the overseer. Consistent crimping/sealing depends on correct machine setup by skilled staff. Compromised seals generate scrap that adds cost per batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of restarting filling before accepted operating conditions are confirmed
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that filling starts again before the required safe operating conditions have been checked and accepted. This helps prevent improperly made or contaminated products.
rows.imp_rat: Restarting filling before confirming operating conditions risks running under unverified sterility (relevance 7); severity high as it can release non-sterile product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Documented restart criteria and interlocks make premature restart fairly rare but not impossible. Restart condition confirmation adds a defined check before resuming. Confirming accepted conditions follows SOPs with moderate judgement required. The confirmation step is low cost relative to avoided rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that equipment is configured for the wrong product or container format
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the equipment is set up for the wrong product or package size. This helps prevent mistakes and wasted materials.
rows.imp_rat: Wrong product/container format configuration is a mix-up risk touching both dosing accuracy and sterility (relevance 7); severity high given patient harm from wrong-format or wrong-product fills (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance and format checklists catch most mismatches, though wrong-format setups still occasionally occur. Verifying correct format adds brief checks at setup. Correct configuration depends on operator reading specs correctly, an error-prone manual step. Wrong-format setup causes reworks and rejects, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a change to product, materials, personnel, facility, or filling conditions creates an unassessed contamination pathway
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that a change to the product, materials, workers, building, or filling process creates a new way for contamination to enter without being noticed and assessed. This helps keep contamination protections effective when things change.
rows.imp_rat: A change creating an unassessed contamination pathway is a classic change-control gap (relevance 6); severity high as an uncontrolled new pathway can non-sterilize batches (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Formal change control catches most changes but novel pathways can be missed by human assessment. Change assessment is done ahead of implementation, not time-critical. Identifying unassessed contamination pathways relies heavily on expert cross-functional judgement. Change control processes consume review labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrect or contaminated container enters the filling machine
rows.plain: Prevent the filling machine from receiving the wrong container or one that is dirty or contaminated. This helps protect the product and prevent filling problems.
rows.imp_rat: An incorrect or contaminated container entering the machine bears directly on producing acceptable filled units (relevance 7); it can contaminate product or cause mislabeled/defective output requiring recall (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Inline vision and format checks catch most incorrect or damaged containers, though contamination detection is less consistent than dimensional checks. Automated container inspection operates inline at production speed without adding delay. Vision and sensor systems reduce reliance on operator inspection judgement for container correctness. Rejecting bad containers early avoids downstream fill waste, offsetting inspection system cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an adverse contamination trend is overlooked during batch disposition
rows.plain: Make sure signs of a harmful rise in contamination are noticed before deciding whether a batch is safe to release. This helps prevent an unsafe batch from being approved.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking an adverse contamination trend at disposition can release a compromised batch (relevance 6); severity high as non-sterile product escapes to patients (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Trend review is standard but manual analysis can overlook subtle adverse trends before release. Trend review happens at disposition, sometimes lagging the batch. Recognizing adverse trends requires experienced data interpretation. Trending software and analyst time are moderate costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration that sterilization conditions remain outside validated limits during the cycle
rows.plain: Keep the sterilizing treatment within its accepted limits for the entire treatment run. This helps ensure the product path is properly sterilized.
rows.imp_rat: Sterilization conditions outside validated limits during the cycle means the pathway may not be sterilized (relevance 7); severity severe as under-sterilization produces non-sterile product (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Automated PLC-controlled sterilization cycles hold conditions within validated limits very consistently. Cycle duration is fixed by validation; excursions extend cycles automatically. Automated cycle control removes most operator involvement in maintaining conditions. An out-of-limit cycle scraps the load, a cost that controls prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that parameter adjustments drive fill volume or seal integrity outside approved limits
rows.plain: Make sure changing the machine’s settings does not cause too much or too little product to be added or make the seal unsafe. This keeps the product within approved limits.
rows.imp_rat: Parameter adjustments driving fill volume or seal integrity out of approved limits is central to controlled operation; the consequence is out-of-spec or unsealed product, serious for quality though usually detectable, so severity is high-moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Parameter bounding and change control are standard, but adjustments within nominal limits can still degrade seal integrity in ways not always caught immediately. Recipe-bounded parameter limits let adjustments happen quickly without lengthy re-verification each time. Configured min/max bounds and access control catch out-of-range entries that would otherwise depend on operator knowledge. Preventing out-of-limit adjustments avoids scrapped batches, so the control largely pays for itself.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of filled containers containing foreign particles
rows.plain: Have fewer filled containers with bits of unwanted material inside them. This helps protect the quality and safety of the medicine.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign particles in filled containers are a quality/safety defect adjacent to the sterility job (relevance 6); severity high given patient injury risk and recalls for particulates in injectables (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Automated visual inspection helps, but particle detection remains imperfect with real escape and false-reject rates. Particle inspection adds an inspection stage but is a planned part of throughput. Manual particle inspection is highly skill- and attention-dependent and inconsistent between inspectors. Foreign particles cause rejects and, if missed, costly recalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that microbial contamination remains undetected during aseptic process simulations
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that germs remain unnoticed during tests that imitate the clean production process. This helps show whether the process can stay free of contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected contamination in process simulations undermines confidence the aseptic process works, though a step removed from routine fills (relevance 6); severity high as a passed-but-false media fill validates an unsafe process (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Validated media fill protocols with defined acceptance make undetected contamination unlikely but sample size limits sensitivity. Media fill results require incubation, delaying detection. Reading and interpreting media fills needs trained microbiologists. Media fills are expensive, consuming line time and materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of an aseptic-condition excursion before recognition and escalation
rows.plain: Shorten the time that a loss of clean conditions continues before it is noticed and brought to the right person's attention. This limits the possible effect on the process and products.
rows.imp_rat: Excursion duration before recognition and escalation scales the quantity of at-risk product (relevance 6); severity high as prolonged excursions enlarge contamination exposure (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Non-viable alarms are fast but viable excursion recognition is delayed by incubation, causing inconsistent response times. The core need is shortening excursion duration before recognition, which viable-lag monitoring makes chronically slow. Recognition depends on analyst interpretation of delayed data. Faster detection systems require investment in continuous monitoring.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible microbial and particulate contamination pathways omitted from the sterile dispensing risk assessment
rows.plain: Make sure the assessment of contamination risks for sterile dispensing includes every realistic way germs or particles could get in. This helps prevent important risks from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted contamination pathways in the risk assessment leave hazards uncontrolled by design (relevance 6); severity high as an unassessed pathway can cause non-sterile batches (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Mature QRM methods cover common pathways well, but novel or transient pathways are routinely underrepresented. Contamination-pathway risk assessments (FMEA/HACCP) are time-consuming workshops that extend the review timeline. Identifying all credible microbial and particulate pathways requires scarce contamination-control and microbiology expertise. Omitted pathways drive contamination events and batch losses that dwarf the assessment effort's cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible cleaning, sterilization, or decontamination failures omitted from the sterile dispensing risk assessment
rows.plain: Make sure the sterile dispensing risk review includes every believable way that cleaning, sterilizing, or removing contamination could fail. This helps prevent contamination from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted cleaning/sterilization failure modes from the risk assessment leave sterilization risks uncontrolled (relevance 6); severity high as such gaps can produce non-sterile batches (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Established cleaning-validation and sterilization frameworks catch most failures, though edge cases remain gaps. Cleaning and sterilization failure analysis adds structured but lengthy review steps. Assessing SIP/CIP and decontamination failure modes needs specialized process and equipment knowledge. Missed failure modes lead to sterility failures and expensive investigations later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of fill-output variation across repeated calibration runs under unchanged conditions
rows.plain: Make the amount filled stay nearly the same each time when the system is tested under the same conditions. This helps ensure products receive the intended amount.
rows.imp_rat: Fill-output variation across repeated calibration runs speaks to the precision core of the job (relevance 8); high variability degrades quality but is usually caught in validation rather than causing acute harm (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Servo-driven and mass-flow fillers deliver good repeatability under fixed conditions, but pump wear, trapped air, and product inconsistency still produce recurring run-to-run spread. Repeated calibration runs are quick to execute but consume iterative setup time when variation persists. Interpreting run-to-run scatter and stabilizing it demands real expertise in the fill mechanism. Variation drives conservative overfill targets that quietly waste product across the run.

rows.source_job: Calibrate process controls and fill/seal parameters
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from fill and seal specifications across the operating range after calibration
rows.plain: Keep the amount put into containers and the way they are closed within the approved limits throughout normal operation. This helps ensure the product is filled and sealed correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from fill and seal specs across the operating range after calibration is central to consistent output; the consequence is systematic out-of-spec packages, costly for quality but generally detectable, so moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Documented calibration against traceable standards holds fill/seal specs well at set points, but drift at range extremes still produces occasional out-of-spec deviation. Calibration with reference weights and gauges is a well-defined routine that fits scheduled maintenance windows. Standard calibration procedures reduce reliance on expert judgement, though verifying performance across the full operating range needs competence. Calibration equipment and test material use modest resources relative to the giveaway or rejects it prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical wear, leakage, cracking, looseness, or damage remains undetected during inspection
rows.plain: Make sure an inspection does not miss important wear, leaks, cracks, loose parts, or other damage. Finding these problems early helps prevent failures and injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected wear, leakage, or cracking during inspection supports reliability of the filling job (relevance 6); missed defects lead to leaks, contamination, or breakdown, but inspection is one of several safeguards (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Established checklist-driven inspection catches most gross defects, yet subtle wear and hidden damage routinely slip through manual examination. Visual and tactile inspection is quick per point but thorough coverage of all wear areas lengthens the maintenance stop. Spotting incipient cracking, looseness, or leakage reliably depends on an inspector's trained eye and attentiveness. Inspection is low cost, but missed defects that progress to failure impose large corrective costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of contamination-critical aseptic-zone locations omitted from monitoring coverage
rows.plain: Monitor all clean-area locations where contamination could seriously affect the product. Missing a location could allow a problem to go unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Critical locations omitted from monitoring create blind spots for contamination detection (relevance 6); severity high as undetected contamination reaches patients (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: EM programs use risk-based location mapping but blind spots persist because coverage is sampled, not comprehensive. Coverage assessment is periodic, not time-sensitive. Risk-based site selection requires experienced microbiologists and can miss locations. More monitoring points add labor and media cost, pressuring coverage decisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of entering the aseptic zone with undetected glove or garment damage
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of entering the very clean work area while having a torn glove or damaged clothing that has not been noticed. Undetected damage could let germs into the area.
rows.imp_rat: Entering with undetected glove/garment damage is a preventable contamination-carry risk (relevance 6); severity high since undetected damage exposes product (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Visual checks and glove integrity testing help, but small undetected defects still slip through into the zone. Damage checks before entry are quick and built into the routine. Detecting subtle glove or garment damage relies on operator vigilance and is easy to overlook. Undetected damage risks batch contamination, an expensive downstream consequence.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of safety- and quality-critical dispensing faults omitted from the control assessment
rows.plain: Make sure the control review considers every dispensing mistake that could affect worker safety or product quality. This helps reveal weaknesses before they cause harm or defects.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted safety/quality-critical dispensing faults from the control assessment leave failure modes uncontrolled (relevance 6); severity high as unaddressed faults can harm patients (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Even mature FMEA and HAZOP practice routinely misses novel or interaction faults, so omissions persist despite structured methods. Assembling a full fault catalog for a control assessment takes weeks of review meetings even with checklists. Identifying which dispensing faults are safety- and quality-critical relies heavily on scarce experienced reviewers whose judgement varies. The review labor is costly but a small fraction of the overall qualification budget.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of fill parameters outside approved limits that can be released in an executable recipe
rows.plain: Prevent a usable production recipe from allowing important filling settings to fall outside their approved ranges. This helps keep filling consistent and within requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing a recipe with out-of-limit fill parameters can systematically misdose the batch (relevance 6); severity high as a bad recipe drives batch-wide dosing error (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Modern recipe systems increasingly enforce configured min/max limits, so out-of-limit releases are uncommon where such enforcement is deployed. Verifying every fill parameter against approved limits before release is a slow manual cross-check today. It demands trained validation staff who understand both the recipe engine and approved specs. Rework from a bad release is expensive but limit-checking itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of missing evidence about sterile-boundary and product-contact interface integrity during dispensing
rows.plain: Make sure there is enough proof that the sterile barrier and the parts touching the product are intact during filling. This helps show that the product has not been exposed to contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Missing evidence on sterile-boundary/interface integrity leaves sterility assurance unproven (relevance 6); severity high as unverified integrity can mean undetected breaches (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Integrity testing methods exist but coverage of all dispensing interfaces is frequently patchy. Assembling integrity evidence for sterile boundaries and product-contact interfaces is slow and often incomplete at review time. Interpreting integrity and interface data requires specialized engineering and aseptic knowledge. Missing integrity evidence leads to failed media fills and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of off-spec filled product produced before the operator recognizes the deviation
rows.plain: As little product as possible should be made incorrectly before the operator notices a problem. This helps reduce waste and prevents more faulty product from being produced.
rows.imp_rat: Off-spec product produced before the operator recognizes the deviation is relevant to yield and quality control; consequences are scrap volume and rework losses that scale with detection lag, so moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Inline monitoring with automatic alarms catches many deviations fast, but latency and undetected drift modes still let off-spec runs continue. The core loss is the interval between a deviation starting and the operator noticing, which manual periodic checks only partly shorten. Recognizing a subtle drift early still leans on an attentive, experienced operator watching trends. Everything produced before detection is at-risk scrap, so slow recognition directly wastes product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of packages with delivered fill outside specified quantity limits during operation
rows.plain: Keep the amount in each package within the allowed range while the machine is running. This helps ensure people receive the right quantity.
rows.imp_rat: Delivered fill outside specified quantity limits during operation is core to the fill function of the machine; consequences are underfill/overfill non-compliance and rework, serious commercially but rarely safety-catastrophic, so moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Servo dosing with inline checkweigher feedback is a mature, standard practice that keeps fill within limits very consistently. Inline checkweighing and net-weight feedback correct fill in real time without slowing throughput. Closed-loop fill control offloads precision from the operator to the machine, requiring little manual finesse. Reducing overfill giveaway while avoiding underfill rejects is a well-optimized cost lever with mature technology.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual energy, pressure, or product being released while plant interfaces are disconnected
rows.plain: Prevent leftover power, pressure, or product from being released when the equipment is disconnected from facility services. This helps protect workers from sudden movement, sprays, or other hazards.
rows.imp_rat: Residual energy, pressure, or product released while disconnecting plant interfaces is a maintenance-phase error tangential to the production job, but its occurrence can cause serious injury or hazardous spill, keeping severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Standard bleed-and-blank practice covers most cases but trapped pockets and unexpected residual product still cause release incidents. Draining, venting, and blanking lines before disconnection adds time but is a planned part of any teardown. Safe disconnection of pressurized and product lines requires experienced technicians who know sequence and residual-energy points. Bleed-down fixtures and containment materials are low cost relative to the incident they prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between verification conditions and intended product, container, fill-volume, and operating conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the checking conditions match the product, container, amount being filled, and normal way of operating. This helps show that the amount filled will be accurate in real use.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between verification and intended conditions invalidates sterility/dose assurance (relevance 6); severity high as unrepresentative verification can pass an unsafe setup (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Established DOE and bracketing approaches help, but residual mismatch between test and intended conditions is a recurring real-world gap. Aligning verification conditions to intended use is handled during protocol drafting without major delay. It demands careful matrix reasoning about product/container/volume combinations that depends on experienced reviewers. Mismatches drive rework and re-verification cost that is often unbudgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical wear remains undetected in forming, filling, or sealing components
rows.plain: Detect important damage or wear in the parts that shape, fill, or seal the product. This helps prevent worn parts from causing defects or failures.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected critical wear in form/fill/seal components is fairly central to sustaining the job (relevance moderate-high) and leads to seal failures or downtime (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Without embedded condition monitoring on most machines, critical wear frequently escapes periodic manual inspection until it causes defects. Wear inspection is often visual and manual during PM, adding time without guaranteeing detection. Spotting incipient wear in forming, filling and sealing parts relies heavily on a technician's trained eye and tactile judgement. Undetected wear leads to unplanned failures and scrap, costs that far exceed inspection effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of filling motion or product dispensing after the operator initiates the stop
rows.plain: Make filling stop as soon as the operator presses the stop control. This prevents extra product from being dispensed.
rows.imp_rat: Continued filling motion after an operator stop bears on controllable safe operation (relevance 7); delayed stopping risks operator injury though typically less extreme than full e-stop scenarios (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Programmed controlled-stop sequences are mature and consistently bring filling to rest promptly, though slightly slower than E-stop. Operator-initiated controlled stops halt dispensing within a short, predictable interval by design. The stop control requires minimal skill and is a standard operator function. Prompt stopping limits over-fill and product waste, keeping cost impact low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of critical filling parameters departing from approved operating ranges during production
rows.plain: Keep important filling settings within their approved limits during production. This helps the products meet required standards.
rows.imp_rat: Critical filling parameters departing from approved ranges threaten both dose and sterility assurance (relevance 7); severity high given broad quality impact (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: SCADA alarms and control ranges keep critical parameters within band consistently. Parameter monitoring runs continuously without adding overseer time. Automated control loops reduce reliance on operators holding parameters manually. Excursions can put product at risk, incurring investigation and discard cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualification conditions fail to represent intended fill-volume, container, product, and operating conditions
rows.plain: Make sure testing uses the intended amount, container, product, and operating conditions. This helps ensure the results apply to the real production process.
rows.imp_rat: Qualification not representing intended conditions invalidates confidence the process works (relevance 6); severity high as an unrepresentative qual can approve an unsafe process (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Bracketing and worst-case matrices are standard practice, yet mismatches between qualified and intended conditions still slip through, keeping this only acceptably served. Defining worst-case representative conditions adds up-front analysis time but is a normal part of protocol development. Judging what conditions represent the intended envelope requires seasoned validation judgement that is error-prone when delegated. Non-representative qualification forces requalification, a significant avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual pneumatic, hydraulic, or electrical energy remains after discharge
rows.plain: Make sure stored air pressure, liquid pressure, or electrical power is fully removed before work begins. This helps prevent unexpected movement, shocks, or other injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Residual pneumatic, hydraulic, or electrical energy remaining after discharge is a lockout/safety concern peripheral to the core forming job, but stored energy releasing unexpectedly can cause severe injury, so severity is very high.
rows.sat_rat: Zero-energy verification is standard, but hidden accumulators and capacitor banks occasionally retain charge after nominal discharge. Verifying zero-energy state across pneumatic, hydraulic, and electrical systems is a quick check when accumulators are known. Discharging stored energy such as capacitors and accumulators requires trained personnel aware of each store's location. Discharge and grounding tools are inexpensive and reusable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminating outer glove or garment surfaces during aseptic gowning
rows.plain: Avoid touching or dirtying the outside of gloves or protective clothing while putting them on, so germs and other unwanted material do not enter the clean area.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating outer garment surfaces during gowning is upstream of filling but seeds later zone contamination (relevance 6); severity high as it can propagate to product (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Qualified gowning programs and periodic requalification reduce failures, yet contamination of outer surfaces during donning still occurs. Gowning is a fixed-duration procedure and time is not the primary concern. Avoiding contamination of outer surfaces during gowning is almost entirely a matter of trained manual technique that varies by individual. A botched gowning wastes a sterile gown set, a modest but frequent cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of airborne particle excursions in the critical filling zone during filling
rows.plain: The number of times unwanted particles in the air rise above the allowed level in the filling area should be reduced. This helps protect the product from contamination while it is being filled.
rows.imp_rat: Airborne particle excursions signal loss of air quality relevant to sterility assurance (relevance 7); severity moderate-high as particles correlate with but aren't always viable contamination (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Continuous particle monitoring and unidirectional airflow keep excursions infrequent in qualified critical zones. Unidirectional airflow operates continuously with no operator time burden. Particle control is engineered via HEPA/laminar flow, needing minimal operator skill in steady state. HVAC and filtration carry ongoing energy cost but are established infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required gowning steps omitted or completed out of sequence before entry
rows.plain: Reduce the number of required clothing-change steps that workers skip or do in the wrong order before entering the clean area. This helps keep the area and product free from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted or out-of-sequence gowning steps compromise the personnel contamination barrier (relevance 6); severity high as gowning failures let microbes into the zone (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Posted gowning sequences and supervision reduce omissions, but out-of-sequence steps still occur occasionally. Following the full sequence adds modest structured time. Correct step order relies on operator memory and discipline, which are error-prone under time pressure. Omitted steps mainly cost re-gowning, a minor consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of aseptic filling without required environmental monitoring coverage
rows.plain: Keep product filling from continuing for long periods without the required checks of the surrounding conditions. This helps detect contamination risks while filling is underway.
rows.imp_rat: Filling without required environmental monitoring coverage blinds contamination detection during the run (relevance 6); severity high as undetected contamination reaches patients (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Procedures require EM during filling, making uncovered filling uncommon but not impossible during equipment faults. Monitoring is scheduled with the fill so gaps are brief when they occur. Coverage scheduling requires modest planning skill. Continuous monitoring adds media and equipment cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous energy or pressurized utilities remain connected during machine decommissioning
rows.plain: Before taking the machine out of service, the person should make sure that dangerous power, pressure, and other supplies are disconnected. This prevents unexpected movement, release of pressure, or other injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous energy or pressurized utilities remaining connected during decommissioning is peripheral to routine production but, when it occurs, exposes workers to severe electrocution or pressure-release injury, so severity is very high.
rows.sat_rat: Mature OSHA/ISO LOTO practice with verification checks makes leftover live connections uncommon where the standard is followed. Lockout-tagout procedures add modest time to decommissioning but are a routine, well-budgeted step. LOTO is a codified, widely trained competency that most industrial technicians perform competently. Isolation locks, tags, and verification tools are cheap relative to the machine's value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of trial packages failing fill-accuracy, seal-quality, or integrity criteria across the operating range
rows.plain: Keep trial packages from failing requirements for the amount filled, the quality of the seal, or protection from leaks across the normal operating range. This shows whether the equipment can reliably make acceptable packages.
rows.imp_rat: Trial packages failing fill, seal, or integrity criteria across the operating range matter for qualification but occur during trials rather than production, so consequences are contained scrap and rework with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Established trial-run and sampling routines catch most issues, but a meaningful percentage of trials still fail before setup converges. Running trial packages across the operating range is a lengthy iterative activity during preparation. Interpreting fill, seal and integrity failures to adjust setup requires experienced setters. Trial materials and scrapped packages during setup are a recognized and often significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of filled units outside specification after adjustment for changed product characteristics
rows.plain: Keep the number of filled products that fail requirements low when the product’s characteristics change. This helps maintain consistent product quality despite those changes.
rows.imp_rat: Fill units outside spec after product-characteristic changes is central to the fill accuracy CFJ (relevance high), and out-of-spec units drive rejects and dosing errors (severity moderate), but capped within the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without adaptive control the post-adjustment output remains variable and depends on how well the operator judged the compensation. Re-adjusting for changed product characteristics interrupts the run while new settings are dialed in and verified. Compensating for viscosity, density or material lot changes relies on operator expertise and trial adjustment rather than automated adaptation. Units produced during the re-tuning window are frequently scrapped, adding material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified dispensing tolerances permit delivered volumes outside product requirements
rows.plain: Set the allowed filling limits so that the amount delivered always stays within the product requirements. Loose limits could allow containers to be too full or not full enough.
rows.imp_rat: Tolerances that permit out-of-requirement volumes bake dosing error into the acceptance limits (relevance 6); severity high as wide limits let misdosed product pass to patients (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Well-defined pharmacopeial and product-spec tolerances make this a mature area where specified tolerances usually align with product requirements. Setting fill-volume tolerances is a quick specification step supported by pharmacopeial limits. Standard fill-weight and Ph.Eur./USP dose-uniformity requirements give clear guidance requiring modest specialist judgement. Tolerance specification itself costs little, though overly tight tolerances raise reject cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that regulated residues remain above release limits on machinery surfaces
rows.plain: Make sure unsafe leftover material on the machine is cleaned away to an acceptable level. This helps prevent people or products from being exposed to it later.
rows.imp_rat: Regulated residues above release limits on surfaces is a contamination-control concern adjacent to core fill/seal function (moderate relevance) but severe for patient safety and compliance when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Verification testing exists but residual contamination above limits is a recurring gap without consistent swab confirmation. Decontaminating surfaces to regulated release limits can add significant time depending on the residue class. Judging when surfaces meet release limits requires specialized cleaning and sometimes hygienist expertise. Cleaning agents, PPE, and verification swabbing add meaningful but not prohibitive cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the dispensing target differs from the approved batch specification
rows.plain: Make sure the amount being dispensed matches the approved amount for the product group. This helps ensure every container receives the right quantity.
rows.imp_rat: A dispensing target differing from batch spec means systematically wrong doses across the run (relevance 6); severity high as a whole batch could be misdosed and reach patients (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management systems and verification make wrong-target events rare. Recipe loading and verification is quick at batch start. Verifying target against spec relies on procedural double-checks. A wrong target could scrap a batch but verification is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the wrong fill-weight target is applied to the batch
rows.plain: Make sure the correct target weight is used for the product group. This helps keep the amount in each container consistent.
rows.imp_rat: A wrong fill-weight target applied to the batch systematically misdoses every unit (relevance 6); severity high as an entire batch could be under/overdosed to patients (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe control and independent verification make wrong fill-weight targets uncommon. Setting fill-weight target is a quick setup entry. Correct target entry depends on operator care with procedural verification. A wrong target risks batch loss but the control is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of donning a gowning component with compromised sterile status
rows.plain: Avoid putting on clothing or equipment that may no longer be germ-free. This helps keep the clean work area from being contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Donning a compromised gowning component is upstream but can carry contamination into the zone (relevance 6); severity high as it can reach product (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Sealed sterile gown packs with integrity indicators make donning a compromised component fairly uncommon. Checking component integrity before donning adds only seconds. Recognizing a compromised sterile gowning component depends on operator attention and pack inspection habits. Sterile gown packs are consumables, so discarding a suspect one is a minor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual contamination on hands before sterile gloves are donned
rows.plain: Remove as much contamination as possible from the hands before putting on sterile gloves. This helps keep the work area and product clean.
rows.imp_rat: Residual hand contamination before gloving is upstream but seeds glove-borne contamination into the zone (relevance 6); severity high as it can reach product (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized hand-sanitization procedures and glove disinfection reliably reduce hand contamination before gloving. Hand hygiene before gloving is a brief fixed step. Effective hand decontamination depends on operator adherence to technique rather than complex skill. Sanitizers and consumables involved are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of skin, hair, or personal clothing exposure after gowning
rows.plain: Keep skin, hair, and personal clothing covered as much as possible after putting on protective clothing. This helps prevent them from contaminating the work area.
rows.imp_rat: Skin/hair/clothing exposure after gowning is a persistent personnel contamination source (relevance 6); severity high as shed microbes can non-sterilize product (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Full-coverage sterile garments and buddy checks reliably minimize skin and hair exposure after gowning. Proper gowning coverage is achieved in the fixed gowning routine. Complete coverage after gowning depends on donning technique and mirror checks. Exposure risk mainly costs contamination potential, not direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the applied fill target or tolerance differs from the approved batch specification
rows.plain: Make sure the amount filled, including its allowed difference, matches the approved batch instructions. This helps ensure every batch contains the intended amount.
rows.imp_rat: An applied fill target/tolerance differing from spec misdoses the whole batch (relevance 6); severity high given batch-wide dosing error to patients (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-controlled systems with verification make the applied target matching the approved spec highly dependable. Recipe loading is a quick setup step, not a time sink. Electronic recipe management reduces reliance on operators transcribing targets correctly. A wrong target could scrap a batch, so the stakes are high though the event is rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous pressure or motion remaining after machine shutdown
rows.plain: Make sure no dangerous pressure or movement is left after the machine is turned off. This protects anyone who approaches it afterward.
rows.imp_rat: Residual hazardous pressure/motion after shutdown is tangential to the forming/filling output (moderate relevance) but severe: stored energy release can maim maintenance staff.
rows.sat_rat: Designed safe-shutdown states plus LOTO discipline reliably remove stored energy, though residual pneumatic/thermal energy occasionally lingers. Automatic de-energization and pressure bleed on shutdown add only seconds before safe access. Safe-state and lockout/tagout procedures reduce dependence on the technician correctly judging residual hazards. Pressure-relief and energy-isolation hardware are inexpensive standard inclusions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unintended mechanism movement exposing the technician to a pinch or crush hazard
rows.plain: Prevent machine parts from moving unexpectedly and trapping or crushing the technician. This allows the parts to be moved in a controlled and safe way.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended mechanism movement causing pinch/crush is not core to producing filled/sealed units (moderate relevance) but the crush-hazard consequence to a technician is severe.
rows.sat_rat: Machinery-safety standards and interlocked guarding make unintended pinch/crush exposure well controlled and uncommon. Guard interlocks and safe states impose brief access delays but do not materially slow the work. Interlocked guarding and light curtains remove reliance on the technician remembering to avoid hazard zones. Guarding, interlocks and safety PLCs are standard designed-in costs across the sector.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of hazardous machinery motion after the operator initiates the urgent stop
rows.plain: Make the machinery stop moving as quickly as possible after the operator presses the urgent-stop control. This matters because less moving time can reduce the chance of injury or damage.
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged hazardous motion after an urgent stop is a safety-peripheral function to the forming/filling job (moderate relevance) but the consequence — operator injury from delayed stop — is severe.
rows.sat_rat: Category-rated emergency stop systems with periodic validation are a mature standard that reliably halts hazardous motion quickly. The whole point is minimizing stopping time, and rated safety-stop circuits achieve short, validated stopping distances. E-stop response is fully automatic once initiated, requiring no operator skill beyond hitting the control. Safety-rated braking and stop-category components are a modest, standard cost designed into the machine.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evidence specified by the acceptance criteria is insufficient to judge dispensing sterility assurance
rows.plain: Make sure there is enough reliable evidence to decide whether the dispensing process keeps the product free from harmful germs. This supports a sound safety decision.
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient sterility-assurance evidence in acceptance criteria is a verification-design gap (relevance 5); severity high as it can approve a machine whose sterility isn't proven (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Precedent and guidance help, but evidence adequacy is judgement-driven and inconsistently applied. Judging evidence sufficiency requires iterative review rounds that lengthen approval. Deciding whether specified evidence truly demonstrates sterility assurance demands seasoned reviewer judgement. Insufficient evidence discovered late forces re-testing and re-qualification at high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration for which an out-of-tolerance filling condition remains undetected during a batch
rows.plain: Find filling problems more quickly when the amount being put into containers is outside the allowed range. This limits how much product may be affected before the problem is noticed.
rows.imp_rat: Time an out-of-tolerance fill condition stays undetected scales how many misdosed units are made (relevance 6); severity moderate as it drives quantity of dosing rejects (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Real-time checkweighing and alarms catch most excursions quickly, though intermittent faults can persist between checks. The need is about shortening how long an out-of-tolerance condition goes undetected, and continuous monitoring shortens but does not eliminate this lag. Some detection still depends on operators interpreting trends and alarms correctly. Longer undetected periods enlarge the quantity of affected product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from detection of an aseptic-zone excursion to documented disposition
rows.plain: Shorten the time between noticing that conditions in a sterile area have gone outside the allowed limits and recording what was decided about it. This allows problems to be handled and documented quickly.
rows.imp_rat: Time from excursion detection to documented disposition governs response speed, somewhat administrative (relevance 5); severity moderate-high as delay can allow more compromised product forward (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Deviation systems structure the path but disposition timelines vary considerably. Time from detection to documented disposition depends on investigation workflows that are often slow. Disposition requires QA judgement, creating bottlenecks. Investigation labor and holding costs accrue with delay.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterility-threatening material, seal, or product-contact incompatibilities remain undetected before supplier selection
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that problems with materials, seals, or product-contact parts that could let in germs are missed before choosing a supplier. This helps ensure the selected supplies can safely be used.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected material/seal incompatibilities before supplier selection is an early procurement error, peripheral to running the machine (low relevance), yet severe if a sterility-threatening incompatibility ships (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: No standardized pre-selection screening reliably catches all sterility-threatening incompatibilities, so these defects frequently slip through undetected. Material and product-contact compatibility studies are slow and often deferred until after a supplier is chosen, compressing detection into a poor window. Detecting seal, elastomer, and product-contact incompatibilities demands deep materials-science and extractables/leachables expertise that few acquisition teams hold in-house. Undetected incompatibilities surfacing during validation or production cause costly rejected batches and rework far exceeding upfront testing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containers with fill-weight errors caused by trapped gas or foaming
rows.plain: Reduce incorrect fill amounts caused by trapped air or foam. This helps ensure each container receives the intended amount.
rows.imp_rat: Trapped gas/foaming fill-weight errors are a dosing-accuracy defect mechanism (relevance 6); severity moderate as affected units are rejected (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Anti-foam nozzles and speed tuning help but trapped gas remains an inconsistent error source for prone products. Foaming issues can slow filling to allow settling. Managing foaming products demands specialized nozzle and speed expertise. Foaming causes fill errors and product rejects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement between indicated fill weight and actual container contents
rows.plain: Make the displayed fill amount agree with the amount actually inside the container. This helps workers know that the container has been filled correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement between indicated and actual fill weight defeats measurement trust in the dosing job (relevance 6); severity moderate-high as it can pass misdosed product (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Regular checkweigher calibration keeps indicated-vs-actual disagreement low but sampling gaps remain. Verification of indicated vs actual weight adds periodic checks. Calibration and verification depend on procedural discipline. Calibration and check weights are modest recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of filled-product potency deviation from the defined specification
rows.plain: Keep the strength of the product in each filled container close to the required specification. This helps ensure the product works as intended and meets quality requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Potency deviation is a dose-quality attribute somewhat separate from the aseptic dispensing job (relevance 5); severity high as wrong potency harms patients (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Formulation controls and release testing make potency deviations at the fill step uncommon. Potency is set upstream in formulation, so it seldom affects run timing for the overseer. Maintaining potency at fill depends on validated formulation and analysis rather than filling-line skill. Out-of-spec potency risks losing the whole batch, a large cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable cGMP and regulatory aseptic requirements omitted from the acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the acceptance checks include all relevant rules for clean manufacturing and regulatory requirements. This helps prevent important requirements from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting cGMP aseptic requirements from acceptance criteria is an administrative design gap, not the fill act (relevance 5); severity high since omission can allow a non-compliant, unsafe machine into service (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory guidance and internal templates catch most requirements, but gaps still recur where a reviewer's knowledge is incomplete. Compiling applicable requirements into acceptance criteria takes weeks and relies on manual regulatory review before URS/DQ approval. Completeness depends heavily on the reviewer's cGMP and Annex , which varies widely across individuals. Missed requirements surface late as costly rework, re-validation, or .

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a superseded or retired fill-volume recipe remains selectable for sterile filling
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that old or withdrawn filling instructions can still be chosen for use. This helps ensure production uses only the currently approved amount.
rows.imp_rat: A superseded recipe remaining selectable risks running the wrong fill parameters (relevance 5); severity high as it can misdose a whole batch to patients (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Established recipe lifecycle states and lockouts usually prevent superseded recipes from remaining selectable, though gaps appear in poorly configured legacy systems. Retiring and locking out old recipe versions is a quick administrative action in most MES. Standard version-control workflows require little specialized skill once configured. Version management adds negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of trial fills outside approved quantity limits
rows.plain: Make sure the test fills contain an amount of product within the approved limits. This checks that the filling process is working correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Trial fills outside approved quantity limits matter to dialing in the filling job (relevance 7), but trial output is typically discarded rather than shipped, limiting downstream harm (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Sequential trial-and-adjust with checkweighing brings most runs into limits, but the first fills after a format change routinely fall outside before convergence. Running and weighing trial fills is a standard bench step that adds bounded setup time. Judging when trial fills sit safely inside quantity limits still requires a trained setter, though statistical check tools help. Trial fills outside limits waste product and packaging during setup, a cost operations tolerate as normal changeover loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between configured fill volume and the approved target for the product run
rows.plain: Set the package fill amount as close as possible to the approved amount for that production run. This helps keep packages consistent and within the required limits.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between configured and approved fill volume directly affects the fill job's accuracy (relevance high), and mis-dosing causes batch rejection and dosing errors (severity moderate), held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Verification weighing and check-weighers make gross deviations from approved target uncommon, though drift within a run can still occur. Setting and confirming fill volume against target is a routine, relatively quick preparation step. Operators follow documented setpoints, so limited specialist skill is needed once recipes exist. Overfill deviation wastes product but verification checks keep this bounded.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a recipe that does not match the current product, dose, or container configuration
rows.plain: Choose operating instructions that fit the product being made, the amount being used, and the type of container. This helps the process run correctly for the current product.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting a recipe not matching product/dose/container is central to correct fill-finish output (relevance moderate-high) and produces mislabeled or wrong-dose product (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: MES/recipe-management with scan verification is increasingly standard, but manual selection on less-integrated lines still allows mismatches. Recipe selection from a validated library is fast and avoids manual re-entry of parameters. Barcode or MES-driven recipe download reduces reliance on the operator manually choosing correctly. A wrong recipe risks a whole batch, and recipe-management systems mitigate this at modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a malfunctioning guard, interlock, emergency stop, or protective device passes inspection
rows.plain: Make sure a broken safety device cannot incorrectly pass its inspection. These devices must work when they are needed to protect people and equipment.
rows.imp_rat: A malfunctioning guard/interlock/e-stop passing inspection is peripheral to producing filled units (low relevance) but severe — a false-pass safety device can lead to serious operator injury.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory functional safety-device testing is mature and enforced, so malfunctioning guards passing inspection is relatively rare though not impossible. Guard and interlock checks follow a defined checklist that adds little time to scheduled PM. Functional safety testing is well-proceduralized, requiring modest skill to execute a defined test sequence. Safety testing consumes minimal resources while preventing costly incidents and regulatory exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of stored product recipes containing parameters that conflict with approved fill-finish specifications
rows.plain: Make sure stored product instructions do not contain settings that conflict with the approved requirements for sterile filling.
rows.imp_rat: Stored recipes with parameters conflicting with approved specs is a data-integrity issue somewhat removed from the physical job (moderate relevance) but can yield non-conforming batches (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and recipe approval workflows help, but conflicting parameters in stored recipes are a persistent, hard-to-catch failure mode. Recipe review against specifications is a manual, time-consuming step often done under schedule pressure. Detecting a stored parameter conflicting with fill-finish specs relies on expert reviewers cross-checking documents. A latent recipe conflict is cheap to store but expensive when it reaches production as a deviation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the root cause of the filling machine malfunction
rows.plain: Identify the true cause of the filling machine problem, rather than repairing the wrong part or issue.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying malfunction root cause affects recovery quality rather than the filling job itself (relevance 5); it prolongs downtime and risks recurrence but is generally correctable without direct product harm (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostic aids and event logs help, but misattribution of cause remains common and leads to recurring failures. Root-cause diagnosis often consumes hours of iterative troubleshooting while the line stays down. Correctly isolating the true cause among mechanical, control, and dosing possibilities depends squarely on a scarce, experienced troubleshooter, which is the dominant limitation. Misdiagnosis wastes parts, labor, and repeat downtime, driving significant avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a developing machine fault remaining undetected until degradation occurs
rows.plain: Detect a machine problem early, before it gets worse and causes damage or reduced performance.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected developing faults until degradation is moderately relevant to sustained operation (moderate relevance) and causes downtime and quality drift (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Most fill/seal machines lack pervasive predictive monitoring, so developing faults commonly progress to degradation before discovery. Detecting developing faults early would prevent downtime, but current periodic checks catch them late. Interpreting subtle early symptoms demands experienced maintenance staff who are increasingly scarce. Late detection turns minor issues into major repairs and unplanned stoppages with high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile-processing capabilities omitted from the acquisition specification
rows.plain: Make sure the purchase requirements include all important capabilities needed for sterile processing.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting sterile-processing capabilities from the acquisition spec is an upfront procurement error, peripheral to the machine's runtime job (low relevance), though it can force costly requalification (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Even mature URS templates and ISPE guidance leave recurring gaps because each aseptic line is bespoke, so omissions remain a routine occurrence. Assembling a complete sterile-processing capability spec takes weeks of cross-functional review, and omissions typically surface late when time to correct is already gone. Capturing every critical sterile-processing capability depends on scarce validation and aseptic-engineering experts whose availability and thoroughness vary greatly between buyers. Missed capabilities discovered post-selection drive expensive change orders or re-procurement that dwarf the original spec effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of fill-weight change caused by drug-product temperature or viscosity shifts
rows.plain: Keep changes in fill weight from being caused by changes in the product’s temperature or thickness. This helps each container receive the intended amount.
rows.imp_rat: Fill-weight change from temperature/viscosity shifts is a root cause of dosing variability (relevance 6); severity moderate as it produces correctable/rejectable variation (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Temperature control helps but viscosity-driven fill shifts remain a recurring variable factor. Temperature/viscosity effects can require time to stabilize product. Compensating for viscosity shifts requires experienced process understanding. Off-target fills from viscosity shifts cause rejects and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from an adverse environmental indication to notification of accountable personnel
rows.plain: Notify the responsible people as quickly as possible after an environmental check shows something unusual. A fast warning allows them to investigate and respond sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Time to notify accountable personnel of an adverse indication is a response-latency issue (relevance 5); severity moderate-high as delay expands the at-risk window (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Automated environmental alarms notify quickly where instrumented, but viable indications still lag. Notification speed depends on alarm routing which can be immediate or manual. Automated alerts reduce reliance on someone noticing. Alerting systems are modest incremental cost.

rows.source_job: Remove residual product and process materials
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of residual product, process fluid, film stock, or packaging material remaining in machine cavities and fill paths
rows.plain: Leave as little medicine, process liquid, packaging film, or other packaging material as possible inside machine spaces and filling routes. This helps prevent leftover material from affecting later work.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product/film/material in cavities and fill paths is a changeover/cleaning concern adjacent to the core job (moderate relevance) but severe for cross-contamination risk.
rows.sat_rat: Emptying is routinely done but hidden cavities routinely retain some product, so completeness varies. Purging product, fluids, and film from cavities and fill paths is time-consuming manual work. Knowing all dead-legs and fill-path cavities requires familiarity with the specific machine design. Residual product and film left behind is directly wasted material and adds disposal weight cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containers receiving less than the specified volume
rows.plain: Make sure containers do not receive less product than the required amount. This helps ensure each customer gets the amount promised.
rows.imp_rat: Containers receiving less than specified volume is a direct measured-volume defect (relevance 6); severity moderate as underfill yields rejects/underdose but rarely acute harm (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Checkweigher feedback and or statistical weight checks make persistent underfilling uncommon. Fill-weight control loops correct low fills in near real time. Automated weight-based control minimizes operator skill needed for accuracy. Underfills waste containers and product but are largely prevented by feedback control.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containers receiving more than the specified volume
rows.plain: Make sure containers do not receive more product than the required amount. This avoids waste and keeps every container within the approved amount.
rows.imp_rat: Overfill is a measured-volume defect in the core dosing job (relevance 6); severity moderate as overfill wastes product and can overdose but usually caught (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Weight feedback keeps overfills consistently within limits. Overfill correction is automatic through fill control loops. Little operator skill is needed as automated control governs dispensed volume. Overfilling wastes expensive product, but weight control keeps giveaway low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of containers receiving no sterile product during a filling run
rows.plain: Make sure every container receives some of the germ-free product during filling. This prevents empty containers from being treated as finished products.
rows.imp_rat: Containers receiving no product are missed-fill defects in the dispensing job (relevance 6); severity moderate as empties are typically rejected before release (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Fill verification catches most no-fill units but occasional misses persist. Missed-fill detection operates inline without delay. Automated detection reduces operator vigilance needs. Empty containers are rejected, a minor container cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of dispensed-volume variation among containers within a batch
rows.plain: Make the amount put into containers as similar as possible within the same batch. This helps ensure each container receives the correct quantity.
rows.imp_rat: Dispensed-volume variation among containers is central to the measured-volume half of the CFJ (relevance 6); severity moderate as out-of-spec units are rejected (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Modern dosing systems hold volume variation consistently within tight bounds. Volume consistency is maintained continuously by control loops. Automated dosing minimizes skill needed to hold uniformity. Tighter uniformity reduces both giveaway and rejects.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of dispensing cycles in which sterile product misses the container opening
rows.plain: Prevent the product from missing the container opening during filling. This reduces spills and helps ensure every filling step succeeds.
rows.imp_rat: Product missing the container opening is a dispensing-placement defect and spill risk (relevance 6); severity moderate as it yields rejects and product loss (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Good tooling and alignment make missed openings uncommon but splashing still occurs at speed. Nozzle alignment is set at setup and does not add runtime. Proper nozzle-to-container alignment relies on skilled setup. Missed openings spill product, a modest recurring loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of fill-weight drift from the specified target across the batch
rows.plain: Keep the weight put into containers from gradually moving away from the required target across the batch. This helps maintain consistent product amounts from beginning to end.
rows.imp_rat: Fill-weight drift across the batch degrades dose accuracy in the core job (relevance 6); severity moderate as drift produces rejects/underdose usually caught (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Weight feedback keeps drift from target consistently small over the batch. Drift correction happens automatically during the run. Feedback control reduces need for operator retuning. Controlled drift limits both overfill giveaway and rejects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of fill-weight variation among consecutive containers during steady filling
rows.plain: Keep the weights of containers filled one after another as similar as possible during steady filling. This helps ensure each container receives a consistent amount.
rows.imp_rat: Fill-weight variation among consecutive containers is a core measured-volume attribute (relevance 6); severity moderate as variability yields rejects (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Steady-state fill variation is consistently tight on well-maintained systems. Consecutive-container consistency is maintained in real time. Automated dosing needs little operator finesse for short-term stability. Consistency reduces rejects and product waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of drug-product delivery fluctuations that alter fill weight during the batch
rows.plain: Reduce changes in the amount of product delivered to the filling process during a batch. This helps keep the amount put into each container consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Delivery fluctuations altering fill weight are a direct dosing-stability issue in the core job (relevance 6); severity moderate as fluctuations cause rejects (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Pump design and feedback mitigate fluctuations but they still occur and affect fill weight. Delivery fluctuations may require in-run adjustment time. Diagnosing pump/pressure fluctuations needs technical skill. Fluctuation-driven off-target fills waste product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of clean-to-less-clean boundary crossings during aseptic gowning
rows.plain: Avoid crossing back and forth between cleaner and less-clean areas while putting on protective clothing. This helps keep germs from entering the clean work area.
rows.imp_rat: Clean-to-less-clean boundary crossings during gowning are upstream contamination-transfer opportunities (relevance 5); severity moderate-high as they can carry contamination toward product (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Designed airlock flows and gowning sequences control crossings well, though behavioral lapses persist. Airlock and gowning sequence transitions add defined time to entry. Minimizing dirty-to-clean crossings depends on trained sequencing discipline that operators sometimes lapse on. Extra crossings mainly cost gown integrity risk rather than direct money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of filled containers with dispensed volumes below the approved lower tolerance limit
rows.plain: Keep the amount of product in each filled container from falling below the approved lower limit. This helps ensure customers receive the full intended amount.
rows.imp_rat: Volumes below lower tolerance are underfill dosing defects (relevance 6); severity moderate as underdose is typically detected and rejected (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Checkweighers reliably catch underfills, keeping escapes low. Underfill rejection is automatic and does not delay the line. Preventing underfill depends on reliable dosing calibration rather than operator skill. Underfilled units are lost product plus wasted container, a controllable but real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of filled containers with dispensed volumes above the approved upper tolerance limit
rows.plain: Keep the amount of product in each filled container from exceeding the approved upper limit. This keeps the product amount safe and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Volumes above upper tolerance are overfill dosing defects (relevance 6); severity moderate as overfill is wasteful and usually caught (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Feedback dosing keeps overfills within tight bounds most of the time. Overfill rejection is handled inline without added time. Overfill control is a dosing-setup matter handled by standard systems. Overfilling wastes expensive sterile product directly, the primary loss addressed here.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of fill-volume drift from the approved target during a batch
rows.plain: Keep the amount put into containers from gradually moving away from the approved target during a batch. This helps maintain the correct amount throughout the run.
rows.imp_rat: Fill-volume drift from target during a batch degrades dose accuracy, a core-job attribute (relevance 6); severity moderate as drift is typically caught and yields rejects (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop fill control with periodic weight checks keeps drift small and correctable during a batch. Drift correction is automated and does not interrupt the run for the overseer. Feedback dosing systems reduce the manual finesse once required to hold target. Drift causes giveaway or rejects that accrue cost over a batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of container-to-container fill-volume variation under unchanged batch conditions
rows.plain: Keep the amount filled into each container as similar as possible when the batch conditions have not changed. This helps ensure containers receive the intended amount.
rows.imp_rat: Container-to-container fill-volume variation is part of the 'measured volumes' half of the CFJ (relevance 6); severity moderate as out-of-spec dose has quality impact but rarely acute harm (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Servo pumps and inline checkweighing deliver tight, consistent container-to-container volumes, making this well controlled. Fill-weight control operates continuously without slowing production. Modern volumetric and checkweigh systems reduce reliance on operator adjustment for consistency. Excess variation causes rejects and product giveaway, a moderate ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containers with volume errors following fill-nozzle contact
rows.plain: Reduce the number of containers filled with too much or too little after the filling nozzle touches them. This helps ensure every container receives the intended amount.
rows.imp_rat: Volume errors after fill-nozzle contact are a localized dosing defect (relevance 5); severity moderate as such units are rejected (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Non-contact dosing and dripless nozzles reduce this but contact-related errors still occur. Nozzle contact issues addressed at setup, minimal runtime cost. Preventing post-contact volume error relies on setup skill. Affected containers become rejects, a modest loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of container-path misalignment after format-component installation
rows.plain: Keep the path that containers follow lined up correctly after new parts are installed. This helps containers move smoothly without jams or damage.
rows.imp_rat: Container-path misalignment after format installation causes handling and fill-placement faults (relevance 5); severity moderate as it yields rejects or missed fills (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Tooling guides and adjustment checks help, but post-installation misalignment still surfaces during startup. Alignment adjustment during format changeover adds setup time. Achieving correct container-path alignment demands experienced hands-on adjustment. Misalignment causes jams and container losses, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of acceptance criteria conflicting with applicable cGMP and regulatory aseptic requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the requirements used to judge options do not conflict with rules for making sterile products safely. This makes the final choice easier to defend and safer to use.
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria conflicting with cGMP is an administrative design error, peripheral to the fill act (relevance 4); severity moderate-high as it can allow a non-compliant machine into use (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Structured review and QA sign-off catch overt conflicts, but subtle contradictions between overlapping standards still slip through. Reconciling conflicting criteria against regulations is a slow manual cross-check during review cycles. Detecting conflicts demands deep knowledge of overlapping cGMP, Annex 1, and USP expectations that few reviewers fully hold. Undetected conflicts cause disputes and rework late in qualification, inflating project cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between installed nozzles, guides, star wheels, or timing components and the container path
rows.plain: Make sure the installed outlets, guides, rotating wheels, and timing parts line up with the path the containers follow. This helps containers move through the machine smoothly and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between nozzles, guides, star wheels, or timing components and the container path is relevant to reliable filling because it can cause jams, spills, or container damage, and its consequences (downtime, product loss) can be moderately serious. However, it is largely a setup/mechanical-tolerance issue that is correctable and lower in the combined ordering, so both proxies are held at the batch ceiling of 5.
rows.sat_rat: Dowels, reference marks, and set stops improve repeatability, but fine alignment remains a manual art with recurring residual misalignment across changeovers. Aligning nozzles, star wheels, and timing components is an iterative manual task that consumes substantial setup time. Good alignment leans heavily on an experienced fitter's feel and eye, making it strongly expertise-dependent. Misalignment drives jams and product loss during setup, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between the entered fill target and the approved batch specification
rows.plain: Make sure the amount entered as the filling goal matches the approved amount for the batch. This helps ensure the batch is filled correctly.
rows.imp_rat: A discrepancy between entered fill target and approved batch spec can produce an entire non-compliant batch (severity) and is central to filling correctly (relevance), held at the 5.0 ceiling for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Automated recipe download of the approved target is a mature practice that largely eliminates entry discrepancies where deployed. Electronic recipe transfer of the fill target eliminates manual entry time. MES-driven target loading removes operator arithmetic and transcription judgement. Correct targets prevent whole-batch specification failures and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the selected fill mode is incompatible with the current product, container, or approved recipe
rows.plain: Choose a filling method that suits the product, its container, and the approved instructions. This helps avoid filling problems or damage.
rows.imp_rat: An incompatible fill mode versus product/container/recipe can spoil an entire run (severity) and is fairly central to correct filling setup (relevance), yet ranked below the prior batch so capped at 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-locked control systems consistently enforce compatible mode selection, though manual overrides remain a residual failure path. Recipe selection is loaded electronically at changeover with no added verification delay. Recipe management systems remove the operator's need to remember compatible mode-product-container combinations. Preventing an incompatible mode avoids a scrapped run, justifying the recipe system.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment among installed format parts and tooling
rows.plain: Make sure the installed machine parts line up correctly with one another. This helps the machine handle the product properly after it is changed for a different size or shape.
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment among installed format parts/tooling affects forming and sealing quality (moderate relevance) and produces defective seals or jams (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Dowels, tool marks and setup sheets improve repeatability, yet residual misalignment remains common with manual setup. Aligning format parts and tooling is a slow, hands-on changeover task. Correct alignment leans heavily on experienced setters' feel and judgement, a scarce and error-prone dependency. Misalignment causes scrap and rework, but the alignment activity itself is mostly labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of threading film through the wrong forming or sealing path
rows.plain: Make sure the film is guided through the correct path for shaping and sealing. This helps the machine form and close the package properly.
rows.imp_rat: Threading film through the wrong forming or sealing path is central to producing a valid package (high relevance), and it causes scrap and downtime when it happens (moderate severity), but capped at 5.0 for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Threading diagrams and color-coded paths help, but wrong-path threading still recurs with new or rushed operators. Threading film correctly can be quick for experts but causes long troubleshooting when done wrong. Correct threading path depends on operator familiarity and attention, a classic vigilance-and-experience failure. Mis-threading wastes film and packages until caught, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between joined frames, forming sections, hoppers, and dosing assemblies
