rows.plain: Make sure the joined machine sections, shaping parts, hoppers, and measuring units line up correctly. This helps the assembled machine work smoothly and deliver the right amount of material.
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between joined frames, forming sections, hoppers, and dosing assemblies is moderately central to correct forming/filling (moderate relevance) and causes dosing and seal defects (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Machined datums and installation checks make gross assembly misalignment uncommon on modern equipment. Aligning joined frames, forming sections and dosing assemblies is done once at installation and is not the main time driver. Precise structural and assembly alignment requires skilled millwrights and metrology know-how. Alignment tooling and labor are one-time installation costs rather than ongoing waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of verification fills remaining outside specified tolerance after calibration
rows.plain: Make sure test fills are within the allowed range after the measuring devices are adjusted. This confirms that the equipment is putting in the correct amount.
rows.imp_rat: Verification fills outside tolerance after calibration is a QC-check error moderately relevant to fill accuracy (moderate relevance), causing rework and requalification (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Documented calibrate-then-verify loops mean few verification fills remain out of tolerance when standard metrology practice is followed. Post-calibration verification fills add a bounded, scheduled step to preparation. Standard calibration procedures reduce dependence on individual finesse once metrology is in place. Verification consumes some product and instrument time but avoids far costlier out-of-spec runs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product-contact surfaces or residue traps omitted from the documented cleaning scope
rows.plain: Make sure the written cleaning instructions cover every surface that touches the product and every place where leftover material can collect. This helps prevent old material from contaminating later production.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting product-contact surfaces from the cleaning scope is a documentation gap (moderate relevance) but can allow residue contamination with serious consequences (higher severity); held at 5.0 or below.
rows.sat_rat: Riboflavin studies and cleaning validation help, but omissions from documented cleaning scope remain a recurring, hard-to-fully-close gap. Building a complete cleaning-scope document is a lengthy engineering exercise during preparation. Identifying every product-contact surface and residue trap demands deep process and equipment knowledge that is easy to under-apply. An omitted trap is cheap on paper but drives costly contamination and revalidation later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an identified defective package remains in the product flow after the diversion point
rows.plain: Make sure a known defective package is removed and does not continue with the good products. This prevents it from reaching the next stage or a customer.
rows.imp_rat: A defective package remaining in flow past the diversion point defeats reject logic (moderate relevance) and lets bad product reach customers when it happens (high severity); capped for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Reject-verification with bin sensors and failure-to-eject alarms is mature, though occasional mis-timed or failed ejects still let defects pass. Automatic reject diversion acts inline without slowing the line. Reject confirmation sensors remove reliance on the operator manually pulling defective units. Reject mechanisms and confirmation are standard, low-cost additions to inspection systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loading packaging material that does not match the batch specification
rows.plain: Load only packaging that matches the requirements for the batch being made. The wrong packaging could make the finished product unsuitable.
rows.imp_rat: Loading packaging material not matching batch spec affects sealing/packaging correctness (moderate relevance) and causes rejected or mispackaged product (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Material verification by scan is common but not universal, so mismatched-material loading still occurs on lines relying on visual checks. Scanning material against the batch record adds a quick verification step at loading. Barcode reconciliation reduces dependence on the operator visually confirming the right reel or component. Loading wrong packaging can waste an entire run, and verification hardware is cheap insurance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of packages bearing an incorrect date, lot, batch, or identification code
rows.plain: Make sure packages show the correct date and product identification information. Correct markings help people identify and trace each package.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect date, lot, or ID codes on packages don't stop sealing (moderate relevance) but trigger recalls and traceability failures when they occur (high severity); capped for batch position.
rows.sat_rat: Inline print inspection and coder integration are well established, yet coding errors remain a leading cause of recalls when verification is weak. Coder data is set at changeover and printed inline with no added cycle time. Central code management and print verification reduce reliance on the operator keying dates and lots correctly. Mislabeled packages trigger costly rework or recall, and print-inspection cameras limit this affordably.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leakage or pressure loss after installing replacement seals, O-rings, and gaskets
rows.plain: After replacing the soft sealing parts, make sure the connection does not leak or lose pressure. A proper seal keeps the equipment working as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Leakage after seal/O-ring/gasket replacement is a maintenance-outcome error moderately tied to reliable operation (moderate relevance), causing leaks and rework (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard replacement plus pressure/vacuum decay testing catches most leaks, though improperly seated or damaged seals still occasionally pass and fail in service. Seal and O-ring replacement is routine but post-install leak/pressure testing adds verification time. Correct seating and torque of seals depends on technician care; improper fitting is a common workmanship error. Seal kits are inexpensive but a leak after service can waste product and require rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that compressed air entering the machine fails the required cleanliness standard
rows.plain: Make sure the compressed air entering the machine is clean enough for the machine's requirements. Dirty air could contaminate the equipment or the work.
rows.imp_rat: Compressed air failing cleanliness standards is peripheral to the mechanical form/fill/seal action (moderate relevance) but can contaminate product with serious consequences (higher severity); held at 5.0 or below.
rows.sat_rat: Point-of-use filtration and air-quality testing are standard, though verification frequency gaps let sub-standard air occasionally reach the machine. Verifying compressed-air cleanliness requires sampling that adds some lead time. Interpreting ISO 8573 air-quality results needs some specialist understanding but follows standards. Filtration and testing carry cost but prevent expensive contamination events.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of sterile product dispensed when a primary container is absent or misaligned
rows.plain: Avoid wasting germ-free product when its container is missing or not in the right position. The product should go into the intended container rather than being dispensed elsewhere.
rows.imp_rat: Dispensing product with no/misaligned container is a waste and spill outcome (relevance 5); severity moderate-low as it's yield loss and possible mess, not patient harm (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: No-container-no-fill interlocks are mature and reliably prevent dispensing when a container is absent. No-container/no-fill sensors act instantly with no time cost. Sensor interlocks remove operator dependence for this check. Prevents product dispensed to nowhere, saving product cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containers outside fill-weight tolerance immediately after filling resumes
rows.plain: Fill fewer containers with too much or too little material just after filling starts again. This helps keep the amount consistent from one container to the next.
rows.imp_rat: Containers outside tolerance right after resume is a bounded transient dosing defect (relevance 5); severity moderate-low as resume units are typically diverted (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Restart transients reliably produce some off-tolerance units until the loop restabilizes. Post-restart fills take time to stabilize back into tolerance. Judging when fills are back in tolerance needs operator experience. Out-of-tolerance restart containers are discarded, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containers filled outside tolerance during startup or restart stabilization
rows.plain: Fill fewer containers with too much or too little material while the equipment is starting or settling after a restart. This helps keep the amount in each container accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Containers filled outside tolerance during startup stabilization is a bounded transient defect (relevance 5); severity moderate-low as startup units are commonly diverted (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Startup rejection routines reliably segregate off-tolerance units so few escape. Startup stabilization is a defined phase but adds setup time before good product flows. Reaching stable fill at startup benefits from experienced setup but is increasingly routine. Containers filled off-tolerance during startup are discarded, a recurring predictable loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in acceptance criteria for measured-volume dispensing under aseptic conditions
rows.plain: Make the pass-or-fail rules clear for dispensing the right amount of product while keeping it germ-free. Clear rules help people know exactly what must be shown during testing.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in dispensing acceptance criteria is an administrative clarity gap on the dosing half (relevance 4); severity moderate as ambiguity can pass inconsistent dosing judgments (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Fill-volume acceptance is comparatively well-standardized numerically, so ambiguity is somewhat less than for aseptic criteria though still present. Defining measured-volume acceptance criteria is fast when pharmacopeial references are reused. Clear expression of dispensing acceptance still relies on the author's precision and metrology understanding. Ambiguity here triggers arguments over pass/fail that cost time and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in qualification acceptance criteria for sterile-product dispensing
rows.plain: Make the pass-or-fail rules clear for showing that germ-free product can be dispensed correctly. This prevents disagreements about whether the process works well enough.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in qualification acceptance criteria is an administrative clarity issue (relevance 4); severity moderate as ambiguity can permit inconsistent, weak sterility judgments (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Protocol templates reduce ambiguity but subjective wording still recurs, leaving qualification acceptance criteria only acceptably clear. Writing acceptance criteria is not time-consuming once templates exist. Crafting unambiguous criteria requires disciplined technical writing and regulatory literacy that varies widely between authors. Ambiguous criteria cause dispute and re-execution, imposing avoidable cost late in projects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing change parts that do not match the approved product and container format
rows.imp_rat: Installing change parts not matching the approved format can misfeed or mis-fill (severity) and is central to correct setup (relevance); ranked below the prior batch, capped at 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Color-coding, part numbering, and changeover checklists help, but manual selection from similar formats means mismatches still occur without poka-yoke or scan verification. Selecting and fitting the correct change parts is a routine changeover task whose duration is well understood. Correct part matching still relies on operator recognition and documentation discipline unless parts are keyed or coded. Installing wrong change parts causes scrap and rework, a cost partly contained by part labeling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of containers filled outside target quantity before the deviation is detected
rows.imp_rat: Containers filled outside target before detection directly undermines fill accuracy (relevant), but the deviation is eventually caught, limiting downstream harm to a bounded batch loss (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Where checkweighing exists deviations are caught fast, but sample-based lines still let batches of off-target containers through before detection. inline checkweighing detects deviations within one container of onset, minimizing the exposed window. Automatic reject on out-of-tolerance removes operator vigilance from the containment decision. Fewer off-target containers escaping means less rework and fewer scrapped fills.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of containers with fill quantity outside specification after a filling-speed adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Fill quantity drifting out of spec after a speed adjustment directly harms the core accuracy job (relevance) but is a transient tuning condition with bounded scrap (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Servo control largely compensates for speed changes, but transient over/under-fills during ramps still occur on many lines. Speed changes trigger a brief settling period before fills stabilize. Anticipating fill drift after a speed change draws on operator tuning knowledge. Off-spec containers during speed transitions represent avoidable waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of fills outside quantity tolerance after compensating for product viscosity variation
rows.imp_rat: Fills outside tolerance after viscosity compensation strike at fill accuracy (relevance), though viscosity variation is a specific compensable subset with recoverable losses (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Temperature control and adaptive dosing help, but lot-to-lot viscosity variation remains a recurring source of out-of-tolerance fills. Viscosity compensation may require trial fills that add setup and adjustment time. Compensating for viscosity variation often depends on operator experience with the product's behavior. Uncompensated viscosity swings cause giveaway or off-spec scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of identified defective fills remaining in the production stream after segregation
rows.imp_rat: Identified defective fills escaping segregation into the production stream can reach customers (severity), though it is a downstream quarantine failure less central to the filling act (moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Reject-verification and bin-full sensing make segregation fairly robust, but reject confirmation failures still let some defects rejoin the stream. Automatic reject gates divert defective fills inline without stopping production. Automated segregation removes operator manual sorting judgement. Effective segregation prevents defective product reaching customers, avoiding recall cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical filling anomaly is absent from machine status indications during production
rows.imp_rat: A critical anomaly missing from status indications lets defects propagate undetected (severity), though it is a monitoring-layer error one step removed from the dispensing job (moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm and status systems cover many faults but alarm gaps, floods, and undetected anomaly modes leave meaningful blind spots. HMI alarms surface anomalies in real time, but investigating them can interrupt flow. Interpreting whether an alarm reflects a critical anomaly still leans on operator experience. Missed anomalies can propagate defective fills, creating downstream loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an incorrectly seated container passing verification before dispensing
rows.imp_rat: An incorrectly seated container passing verification before dispensing can cause spillage or a bad unit (severity), though it affects individual containers rather than the whole fill job's central accuracy (moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Position and presence sensors reliably block dispensing on unseated containers, though partial-seat edge cases occasionally slip through. Seating sensors verify container position instantly before the dispense cycle. Presence/position sensing removes operator visual verification of seating. Catching a misseated container prevents spilled fills and cleanup cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an abnormal temperature, pressure, speed, state, or error condition is represented as normal
rows.imp_rat: An abnormal condition represented as normal masks danger and lets defects continue (severity), though it is a sensing/indication error a step removed from the core dispensing job (moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm/state verification and sensor cross-checks catch most misrepresentation, though masked or miscalibrated signals can still show abnormal as normal. HMI and sensor validation checks are quick to run during commissioning after maintenance. Confirming that displayed states match true machine conditions needs some controls knowledge but is procedure-guided. Sensor and instrument verification uses existing tools at modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unintended dosing, filling, or sealing behavior after the control-software update
rows.imp_rat: Unintended dosing/filling/sealing behavior after a software update directly threatens output quality (moderate relevance) and can produce a run of bad packages (moderate severity); capped by batch rank.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and test batches mitigate risk, but unexpected software interactions still occasionally produce unintended dosing or sealing behavior. Validating a control-software update requires test runs that extend the maintenance downtime. Confirming safe dosing/sealing behavior after an update needs controls and process expertise that few technicians combine. An unvalidated update can cause runs of defective product, a costly failure mode.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation in pressure, suction, power, or temperature from machine requirements after service
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of pressure, suction, power, or temperature after service affects fill/seal quality (moderate relevance) and produces defects until corrected (moderate severity); held at 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Setpoint verification after service is standard practice and catches most deviations, though undocumented interactions occasionally leave parameters off requirement. Post-service verification of pressure, temperature and power is a routine bench/line check within the maintenance window. Standard gauges and setpoint procedures keep the required skill moderate for verifying utilities after service. Verification instruments are cheap relative to the scrap avoided by catching a wrong setting.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of phase misalignment among film feed, forming, filling, and sealing functions after replacement
rows.imp_rat: Phase misalignment among feed, forming, filling, and sealing after replacement directly disrupts synchronized operation (moderate relevance) and produces defective packages (moderate severity); capped at 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Servo-driven machines with electronic phasing help, but on mechanical or mixed systems phase misalignment after replacement is a recurring setup risk. Re-phasing film feed, forming, filling and sealing after replacement can require iterative adjustment and test cycles. Achieving correct phase relationships across functions demands skilled setup judgement on many machines. Mis-phasing wastes film and product during setup until it is dialed in.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of filled units outside specified fill limits during machinery validation
rows.imp_rat: Filled units outside limits during validation signals fill-accuracy problems (moderate relevance) and can delay qualification when it occurs (moderate severity), kept at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature checkweigher, in-process control, and validation-protocol practice make out-of-limit fill during validation a well-managed and largely detectable condition. Fill-limit validation runs follow well-defined protocols with predictable timelines during factory and site acceptance testing. Statistical fill-weight validation is standard technician-and-metrology work supported by established SOPs, reducing reliance on rare expertise. Out-of-limit units during validation waste product and time, but validation is precisely where such waste is expected and budgeted to be caught.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of container-transfer, dosing-flow, or line-cadence incompatibilities overlooked during the integration assessment
rows.imp_rat: Overlooked container-transfer or line-cadence incompatibilities during integration cause line stoppages (moderate relevance) and costly rework when discovered late (moderate severity); at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without full-scale integration modeling, cadence and transfer mismatches are commonly missed until equipment is on-site, a recurring and costly surprise. Integration assessments consume significant engineering time and container-transfer or cadence mismatches often emerge only during trial runs. Predicting dosing-flow and line-cadence interactions across upstream and downstream equipment requires seasoned line-integration engineers in short supply. Overlooked integration incompatibilities force expensive retrofits and line reconfiguration after purchase.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required certifications absent or inapplicable to the intended machine configuration and market
rows.imp_rat: Missing or inapplicable certifications is peripheral to the mechanical forming/filling/sealing job itself (low relevance) though a compliance gap can block market entry when it occurs (higher severity); held at or below 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established regulatory mapping practices and vendor documentation packages make missing or inapplicable certifications a relatively uncommon and detectable failure. Certification checklists are reasonably quick to run once market and configuration are fixed, though verifying applicability adds review cycles. Regulatory-affairs staff routinely map required certifications, but matching them to a specific configuration and target market still needs specialist judgement. Certification gaps found late trigger re-certification fees and market-entry delays, but mature checklists keep this cost mostly contained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connecting the machine to a supply with mismatched voltage or phase
rows.imp_rat: Connecting to a mismatched voltage or phase supply is a one-time setup error peripheral to routine operation (low relevance), but can damage equipment severely when it happens (higher severity); average held at 5.0 or below.
rows.sat_rat: Nameplate checks, keyed connectors and phase verification make mismatched-supply connection rare in professional installs. Confirming voltage and phase is a fast pre-connection check. Electricians follow standard verification with meters, requiring routine rather than scarce skill. Correct connection avoids costly equipment damage at negligible check cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation of supplied utility pressure, temperature, or flow from machine requirements
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of utility pressure, temperature, or flow from machine requirements affects fill/seal quality (moderate relevance) and can cause bad packages when out of range (moderate severity), kept at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard utility checklists and gauges catch most deviations, though transient supply variation can still slip by. Checking utility pressure, temperature and flow is a quick pre-start verification. Utility specs are documented so limited specialist skill is needed to confirm supply. Out-of-spec utilities can waste a run but verification gauges make this cheap to prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the fault root cause when alarms and symptoms overlap
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying fault root cause when alarms overlap extends downtime (moderate relevance) and wastes maintenance effort (moderate severity), but is a diagnostic support concern kept below 5.0.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm systems flag symptoms but rarely isolate true root cause, so misidentification remains a frequent and costly problem. Root-cause diagnosis with overlapping alarms often means lengthy trial-and-error troubleshooting. Correctly disentangling overlapping symptoms depends almost entirely on scarce, deeply experienced troubleshooters. Misdiagnosis wastes parts, labor and extended downtime chasing the wrong cause.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile product released between intended dispensing cycles
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of germ-free product that escapes between the planned dispensing steps. This helps ensure product is released only when and where it is intended.
rows.imp_rat: Product released between intended dispensing cycles is a drip/waste outcome (relevance 4); severity is material loss and minor contamination housekeeping (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Positive shut-off valves reliably limit inter-cycle release. Between-cycle release control operates automatically. Positive shut-off valves require little operator skill. Product released between cycles is wasted, a small recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of sterile product rejected after loss of aseptic conditions during filling
rows.plain: Avoid throwing away germ-free product because the conditions needed to keep it free of germs were no longer maintained during filling.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected sterile product after aseptic loss is a waste consequence, peripheral to the sterility job itself (relevance 4); severity is economic loss, not patient harm (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: The rejection decision is consistent but errs heavily toward scrapping, so the waste outcome is predictable but large. Detecting and reacting to loss of aseptic conditions takes time, during which product continues to be at risk. Deciding how much product to reject after an excursion requires experienced quality judgement. Conservative practice often forces large rejections of sterile product, a substantial and poorly-mitigated cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of filled containers rejected for out-of-limit volumes
rows.plain: Reduce the number of filled containers rejected because they contain too much or too little product. This prevents unnecessary waste and keeps the amounts consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Rejection for out-of-limit volumes is a waste consequence, not the dosing act itself (relevance 4); severity is scrap-cost economic (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Statistical and inline weight checks make out-of-limit rejects consistently minimized. Inline checkweighing rejects out-of-limit units without slowing the line. Automated rejection removes reliance on operator judgement for volume conformance. Rejected containers cost product and packaging but automated control keeps counts low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of sterile product left in the product-contact path after routine filling ends
rows.plain: Leave as little germ-free product as possible in the tubes and equipment after filling is finished, so usable product is not thrown away.
rows.imp_rat: Product left in the path after routine filling is a residual-waste outcome (relevance 4); severity is material loss economic (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Holdup volumes are predictable but consistently represent unavoidable residual loss. Line clearance to recover holdup adds end-of-run time. Recovering residual product needs procedural knowledge. Product left in the contact path is unrecoverable loss of costly sterile product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of sterile product lost through leaks, drips, or spills during filling
rows.plain: Prevent germ-free product from being lost through leaks, drops, or spills while containers are being filled.
rows.imp_rat: Product lost through leaks/drips/spills is a waste outcome adjacent to filling (relevance 4); severity is economic loss and minor housekeeping (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Positive shut-off and dripless nozzles reduce but do not eliminate spill losses. Cleaning up leaks and drips adds time to the run. Dripless nozzle setup requires modest skill. Leaks, drips and spills represent direct loss of expensive sterile product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of filled containers rejected for missing or improperly seated closures
rows.plain: Reduce the number of filled containers rejected because their closures are missing or not fitted correctly. This prevents product loss and helps keep containers properly closed.
rows.imp_rat: Rejection for missing/improperly seated closures is a waste/yield outcome (relevance 4); severity is scrap loss, though seating relates to CCI it's framed as waste (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Stopper detection and reject stations handle most cases but seating failures still occur. Capping/stoppering is inline with no operator time burden. Automated closure application needs little operator skill. Missing/misseated closure rejects waste filled product, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between onset and operator recognition of an aseptic-condition excursion
rows.plain: Shorten the time between a loss of germ-free conditions and the worker noticing it. Faster recognition gives people more time to protect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Time to operator recognition of an excursion governs contaminated output volume (relevance moderate-high); severe, but capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Environmental monitoring is often retrospective, so recognition latency is inconsistently short. Recognizing an aseptic excursion often lags because monitoring results or alarms are not instantaneous. Interpreting subtle excursion signals requires trained attention. Delayed recognition can widen product at risk, raising cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile-path components installed in incorrect positions or orientations
rows.plain: Install fewer sterile flow-path parts in the wrong place or facing the wrong direction. Correct installation helps the process work as intended and remain protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly positioned sterile-path components directly undermine aseptic dispensing (relevance moderate), and misassembly can breach sterility catastrophically (severity high), but the ranking caps this below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Poka-yoke features and checklists help, but orientation errors still occur with complex assemblies. Verifying position and orientation adds brief checks. Correct orientation of sterile-path parts relies on operator knowledge and can be mistaken with lookalike parts. Misoriented components cause rework or product loss, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile-path components damaged during handling or assembly
rows.plain: Damage fewer parts of the sterile flow path while handling or putting them together. Undamaged parts help keep unwanted contamination out.
rows.imp_rat: Damage to sterile-path components during handling threatens the sterile boundary (relevance moderate) and can compromise product sterility when it occurs (severity moderate-high), yet it sits below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Handling procedures reduce damage, yet delicate parts are still occasionally damaged during assembly. Careful handling slightly slows assembly but time is secondary. Avoiding damage to delicate sterile components depends on careful manual handling skill. Damaged sterile components must be discarded and re-sterilized, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time that an aseptic-condition excursion remains undetected during filling
rows.plain: Shorten the time that a loss of germ-free conditions can go unnoticed while product is being filled. Finding the problem sooner limits the amount of product affected.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected excursion duration governs how much bad product is made (relevance high); severe patient-safety consequences, but capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous environmental monitoring detects many excursions quickly, though some parameters are only sampled, leaving detection gaps. The need is fundamentally about shortening how long an excursion goes undetected during filling. Faster detection relies on monitoring systems more than operator skill. Longer undetected excursions mean more product to discard, a scaling cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of product filled after an aseptic excursion begins
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of product filled after germ-free conditions have been lost. This limits the amount of product that may be affected.
rows.imp_rat: Product filled after an excursion begins is directly contaminated (relevance high), and distributing non-sterile product is severe, though ranking caps it here.
rows.sat_rat: Monitoring alarms help limit product filled after an excursion, but detection lag still allows some suspect product through. Detecting and stopping after an excursion begins depends on detection latency. Recognizing an excursion in progress requires operator attention and monitoring interpretation. Product filled after an excursion is wasted, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of filled containers leaving the aseptic zone with compromised closure integrity
rows.plain: Containers leaving the clean work area should have seals that are fully intact. Damaged or incomplete seals could allow germs to enter later.
rows.imp_rat: Compromised closure integrity leaving the aseptic zone directly defeats sterility maintenance (relevance high) with severe patient risk, but ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: In-line capping controls and inspection make compromised closures leaving the zone fairly uncommon. Closure integrity checks occur downstream without adding fill-line time. Automated capping and seal systems reduce operator skill dependence. Compromised closures cause rejects, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an aseptic breach requires sterile product disposal during startup
rows.plain: Make sure a loss of sterile conditions during startup is found before it forces the sterile product to be thrown away. This helps reduce wasted product.
rows.imp_rat: Startup disposal from an aseptic breach is a waste outcome, marginal to the core sterility job (relevance 4); severity is scrap cost, not patient harm (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Startup is a high-risk window where interventions still trigger disposal decisions fairly often, leaving this only moderately served. Recovering from a startup breach consumes time but the concern is the breach itself. Judging whether a startup breach mandates disposal demands quality expertise that is not always immediately on hand. Startup breaches directly cause sterile product disposal, a frequent and painful loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of filled containers rejected for fill volumes outside acceptance limits
rows.plain: Reduce the number of filled containers rejected because they contain too much or too little product. This helps meet production goals while keeping amounts within the allowed range.
rows.imp_rat: Rejection for out-of-limit fill volume is a waste/yield outcome downstream of the volume defect (relevance 4); severity is economic scrap loss (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Statistical fill control and inline rejection keep out-of-limit containers to a small consistent fraction. Rejects are removed inline without stopping the machine. Reducing out-of-limit fills relies on well-set dosing that is largely automated. The core loss here is rejected filled containers, and current checkweigh feedback keeps that reject rate low but nonzero.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile product discarded after loss of aseptic conditions during filling
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of germ-free product thrown away after germ-free conditions are lost during filling. This limits product waste when a problem occurs.
rows.imp_rat: Discarded sterile product after aseptic loss is a waste/economic outcome, peripheral to sterility delivery (relevance 4); severity is financial rather than patient harm (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Discard rules are consistently applied, but they favour safety over minimizing waste. Discard decisions after aseptic loss consume review time before disposition. Determining the affected quantity requires informed judgement about the breach extent. The need is explicitly about reducing wasted sterile product, and conservative practice tends to discard generously, driving high material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile product dispensed above the specified fill volume
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of germ-free product put into containers above the required amount. This prevents overfilling and unnecessary product loss.
rows.imp_rat: Product dispensed above specified fill volume is a waste/overfill loss outcome (relevance 4); severity is economic material loss (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Consistent dosing keeps giveaway predictable, though deliberately safe targets leave routine excess. Product giveaway does not affect run timing. Minimizing giveaway is achieved through calibrated dosing rather than special skill. Dispensing above target is pure sterile-product waste, and target margins are often set conservatively high, so real giveaway persists.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that aseptic conditions are disrupted during operator interventions
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a worker’s actions break the germ-free conditions needed to protect the product. This helps keep the product safe during work.
rows.imp_rat: Disrupting asepsis during operator interventions is a leading contamination route (relevance moderate-high) with severe patient consequence, capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Barrier technology and validated intervention procedures reduce disruption meaningfully, though human-factor failures still occur. Intervention design and training take time but are standard aseptic-operations practice. Maintaining asepsis during interventions is highly operator-skill dependent and vulnerable to lapses. Disruptions cause batch rejects and rework, a recognized recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contamination-control claims are accepted without supporting evidence under foreseeable dispensing failures
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that claims about preventing contamination are accepted without proof, especially if equipment used to dispense the product fails in an expected way. Evidence is needed to show the product remains protected.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting contamination-control claims without evidence weakens sterility assurance (relevance moderate-high); severe if unfounded claims mask hazards, but capped.
rows.sat_rat: QA challenge culture catches many unsupported claims, but foreseeable-failure scenarios are inconsistently interrogated. Verifying that claims are evidence-backed adds review iterations. Challenging unsupported contamination-control claims requires confident, expert reviewers. Accepting unsupported claims risks field failures with severe recall costs.

rows.source_job: Import or transfer existing product data
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of validated parameter values altered during data conversion
rows.imp_rat: Altered validated parameter values during data conversion is a data-integrity concern peripheral to routine running (low relevance) but can shift the process off-spec when it occurs (moderate severity); below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Verification-after-conversion practices exist but manual mapping still lets values change undetected. Data conversion and re-verification of parameters is a tedious step during migration or upgrades. Catching altered validated values requires careful cross-checking expertise, an attention-heavy task. An undetected altered value is cheap to create but expensive when it corrupts a run.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that finished packages from different production lots are mixed during collection
rows.imp_rat: Mixing finished packages from different lots during collection is a downstream handling issue peripheral to the machine's core job (low relevance) but harms traceability when it occurs (moderate severity); below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance procedures reduce mixing, but the collection/accumulation zone remains a recognized weak point where lot commingling still happens. Enforcing lot separation at collection can require holds or line clearance that slow accumulation. Preventing lot commingling at the collection point often depends on operator discipline and clear procedures. Lot mixing can force quarantine or scrap of otherwise good product, a significant cost risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of production data being associated with the wrong batch or run
rows.imp_rat: Associating production data with the wrong batch is a records/traceability error peripheral to the physical job (low relevance) but complicates recalls when it occurs (moderate severity); below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Where MES and equipment integration exist, batch-data association is reliable, but manual or partially integrated lines still risk misattribution. Automated data capture logs to the batch record continuously without added operator time. MES/electronic-batch-record association reduces reliance on operators manually attributing data to the right run. Misattributed data can invalidate release decisions, and integrated data systems mitigate this at scaling cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of repaired assemblies returned to service with incorrect wiring, alignment, or fastening
rows.imp_rat: Repaired assemblies returned with incorrect wiring or alignment cause repeat failures (moderate relevance) and rework (moderate severity), but this is a maintenance-quality issue kept below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Reassembly checklists and post-repair checks catch many errors, but incorrect wiring or alignment still returns to service often enough to matter. Correct reassembly is achievable in the normal repair window when procedures are followed. Avoiding wiring, alignment and fastening errors depends on technician competence and attention, a common source of workmanship faults. Reassembly errors cause repeat repairs and rework labor, a recurring avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that compressed air introduces oil, water, particles, or microorganisms into the filling machine or sterile area
rows.imp_rat: Compressed air introducing oil/water/microbes is a genuine sterility hazard (severity) but is a utility-side contamination path somewhat peripheral to the core dispensing job (lower relevance).
rows.sat_rat: ISO 8573 filtration and drying practice is established, but coalescing filter breakthrough, undetected drift, and inconsistent point-of-use testing leave a real recurring contamination risk. Installing and validating filtration and drying trains is a bounded commissioning task, though testing air quality adds lead time. Specifying and verifying compressed-air quality classes requires specialist knowledge that plant teams do not always hold. Multi-stage filtration, desiccant drying, and periodic testing carry meaningful capital and ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that installed format components do not match the approved incoming container dimensions
rows.imp_rat: Format components not matching container dimensions cause jams or bad fills (both relevant and moderately severe), but it is a setup-verification error rather than the core dispense function.
rows.sat_rat: Documented format sets and sample-container fit checks catch most mismatches, but incoming container tolerance drift and manual verification leave recurring escapes. Verifying installed format components against incoming container dimensions adds a bounded check to changeover. Catching a dimensional mismatch depends on operator diligence and gauge use rather than automated confirmation. A format mismatch missed until running causes jams and rejected containers, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator applies a response that does not match the actual filling machine fault
rows.imp_rat: An operator applying a mismatched fault response can worsen a stoppage (moderate severity) but is a reactive troubleshooting error peripheral to routine fill execution (moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostic guidance exists but ambiguous fault presentations still lead operators to apply mismatched responses fairly often. Diagnosing the true fault before responding often extends downtime. Matching a response to the actual fault relies heavily on operator troubleshooting expertise, which is scarce and inconsistent. Wrong responses can worsen damage or waste product and time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of filled containers with product on sealing surfaces because of dripping or splashing
rows.imp_rat: Product on sealing surfaces from drip/splash can cause seal failures (moderate severity) and touches fill cleanliness (moderate relevance), though not the central accuracy job.
rows.sat_rat: Positive shut-off and bottom-up nozzles reduce dripping, but splashing and stringing with certain products remain a persistent quality issue. Drip management adds little time but cleaning contaminated sealing surfaces can require intervention. Nozzle setup and anti-drip tuning depend on operator adjustment skill. Product on sealing surfaces causes seal failures and rejected containers, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of product residue remaining in the filling machine fluid paths after the clean-in-place cycle
rows.imp_rat: Product residue remaining after CIP is a cross-contamination risk (severity) but pertains to cleaning validation rather than the central filling function (lower relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Well-validated CIP with conductivity/TOC endpoint monitoring reliably clears fluid paths, though dead-legs and complex valves leave occasional residual pockets. CIP cycles are automated and time-defined, so residue removal happens within a predictable window. Automated CIP reduces skill dependence once recipes are validated, requiring mainly monitoring. Recirculated cleaning solutions and monitored rinse are cost-efficient in mature installations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serviced filters, valves, pumps, drives, or sensors left outside operating tolerance
rows.imp_rat: Serviced components left outside operating tolerance can degrade fill performance (both moderate), but this is a maintenance-quality error indirect to the dispensing job.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration procedures and post-maintenance verification catch most out-of-tolerance parts, though some drift or setup errors escape into operation. Post-service calibration and functional checks add measurable time to each serviced component. Bringing filters, valves, pumps, and sensors back within tolerance requires trained technicians and correct calibration procedure. Calibration equipment and rework of out-of-tolerance parts add cost but are standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of seals, gaskets, nozzles, bearings, or other wear parts incorrectly seated, aligned, or secured
rows.imp_rat: Wear parts incorrectly seated or aligned can leak or mis-fill (moderate severity), but this is a maintenance-assembly error one layer removed from active filling (moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Work instructions and torque specs help, but incorrect seating and alignment remain a frequent source of early failures because verification is largely visual. Correctly seating and aligning seals, bearings, and nozzles takes careful hands-on time that resists shortcuts. Proper seating, alignment, and torque of wear parts is highly manual craft work where technician skill dominates the outcome. Misassembled parts cause premature wear and rework, generating avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricant entering the sterile product-contact area during maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant entering the sterile product-contact area is a serious contamination event (severity), but it is a maintenance-window edge case peripheral to routine filling operation (lower relevance).
rows.sat_rat: SOPs, food-grade lubricant specification, and post-lube inspection substantially control the risk, though occasional contamination from over-application or migration still occurs. Careful lubrication with masking and cleanup during maintenance adds modest handling time. Preventing food-grade lubricant migration into product-contact zones relies on technician discipline and knowledge of correct greases and quantities. Food-grade lubricants and dedicated tools cost more, but the amounts involved are small.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of interventions after which filling-machine components remain in an incorrect state
rows.imp_rat: Components left in incorrect state after intervention can breach asepsis/dosing (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Post-intervention state errors still occur despite checklists. Verifying component state after intervention adds checks. Confirming correct reassembly relies on operator knowledge and checklists. Wrong-state components risk defects, a potential cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of nonessential operator movement within the critical filling zone during intervention
rows.imp_rat: Nonessential operator movement in the critical zone raises contamination exposure (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Movement discipline varies by operator, keeping this inconsistently controlled. Minimizing movement can slow intervention completion. Economy of motion in the critical zone is a trained aseptic discipline. Excess movement risks contamination cost but movement itself is free.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of potentially affected containers omitted from the deviation hold
rows.imp_rat: Affected containers omitted from a deviation hold risks releasing suspect product (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Hold-scope determination is judgement-based and inconsistently complete. Defining the deviation hold scope takes investigative time. Determining affected-unit boundaries needs experienced judgement. Omitted units risk escapes; over-inclusion wastes product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a flagged container remains in conforming flow after segregation
rows.imp_rat: A flagged container staying in conforming flow defeats segregation (relevance moderate) and can ship defective product (severity moderate-high), capped.
rows.sat_rat: Reject-station logic reliably removes flagged containers but rare gate failures occur. Automated reject gates act immediately on flagged units. Automated segregation reduces operator judgement needs. Reject handling is low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a segregated container re-enters or mixes with conforming output
rows.imp_rat: A segregated container re-mixing with conforming output defeats rejection (relevance moderate) and risks shipping defects (severity moderate-high), capped.
rows.sat_rat: Dedicated reject paths reliably prevent remixing though manual handling can reintroduce error. Physical reject bins separate flow instantly. Segregation design minimizes operator handling skill. Segregation hardware is a fixed low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of detected nonconforming containers missing from the segregated set
rows.imp_rat: Nonconforming containers missing from the segregated set risks releasing defects (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Counting and reconciliation catch most misses but manual gaps persist. Reconciling the segregated set takes end-of-run time. Ensuring all nonconforming units are captured relies on operator diligence. Missing rejects risk quality escapes, a potential large cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that fill-measurement capability outside accepted limits is classified as production-ready
rows.imp_rat: Classifying out-of-limit fill capability as production-ready propagates dosing errors (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence (severity moderate), capped.
rows.sat_rat: Statistical fill checks catch gross capability problems, but marginal capability can still be misclassified as ready. Verification testing adds defined startup time. Judging fill-measurement capability requires understanding of acceptance criteria, an expertise dependence. Passing incapable equipment causes out-of-spec fills and losses, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that machine parts are positioned incorrectly after technical intervention
rows.imp_rat: Machine parts positioned incorrectly after intervention can breach asepsis or dosing (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Post-intervention checks help, but incorrect repositioning after maintenance remains a recurring risk. Re-verifying part positions after intervention adds time. Correct repositioning after technical work depends heavily on technician skill and memory. Misplaced parts cause faults and losses, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required recovery checks omitted before filling restarts
rows.imp_rat: Recovery checks omitted before restart risks resuming in an unverified state (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Restart checklists reduce omissions, but under pressure some checks are still skipped. Completing recovery checks adds restart delay. Knowing which recovery checks are required depends on procedural knowledge. Omitted checks risk defective restart product, a cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required utility is interrupted before sterilization cycle completion
rows.imp_rat: Utility interruption before sterilization completes can leave equipment non-sterile (relevance moderate) with real contamination consequence (severity moderate-high), capped.
rows.sat_rat: Backup utilities and monitored supplies make mid-cycle interruptions uncommon in qualified facilities. A utility interruption forces cycle restart, adding significant delay. Utility continuity is an infrastructure matter, not operator skill. An interrupted cycle scraps the load and utilities, a notable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product-contact components damaged during sterilization
rows.imp_rat: Product-contact components damaged during sterilization can breach sterility (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence when undetected (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Validated cycles and correct loading make sterilization-induced damage uncommon. Sterilization damage is discovered after the cycle, potentially delaying setup. Proper loading to avoid damage depends on trained handling. Damaged components require replacement and re-sterilization, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting out-of-target fills as meeting the fill specification
rows.imp_rat: Accepting out-of-target fills as meeting spec propagates dosing errors (relevance moderate) with moderate-high patient consequence (severity moderate), capped.
rows.sat_rat: Automated checkweighers and weight control in many lines make accepting out-of-target fills fairly uncommon. Fill checks add routine time without major delay. Correctly accepting or rejecting fills depends on adherence to specification limits. Accepting out-of-target fills causes downstream rejects, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that aseptic conditions are compromised while verification containers are obtained or checked
rows.imp_rat: Compromising asepsis while obtaining verification containers introduces contamination during a routine check (relevance moderate) with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Defined sampling ports and procedures help, but each intervention still carries real aseptic risk. Obtaining verification containers interrupts flow briefly. Sampling without compromising asepsis requires careful trained intervention. A compromised sampling event scraps product, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of aseptic interventions performed by personnel without current qualification
rows.imp_rat: Interventions by unqualified personnel raise contamination risk (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Gowning qualification and access controls make unqualified interventions rare where enforced. Qualification checks are a quick gate, not a time burden. This need is about ensuring only qualified personnel intervene, and training/qualification programs directly and effectively manage that dependence. Unqualified interventions risking a batch are avoided by enforced qualification, limiting cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of aseptic filling assignments covered by personnel lacking current contamination-control qualification
rows.imp_rat: Unqualified personnel on aseptic assignments raises contamination risk (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Training matrices and gowning qualification make unqualified assignments uncommon in mature sites. Qualification tracking is periodic, not time-critical. This need is directly about ensuring personnel hold current contamination-control skill qualification. Training and qualification programs carry ongoing labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contamination arising during qualification remains undetected
rows.imp_rat: Undetected contamination during qualification yields false sterility confidence (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Validated incubation and growth-promotion practices make undetected qualification contamination uncommon. Incubation of media-fill units imposes a fixed multi-week detection delay. Microbiological reading and detection are standardized, needing trained but not scarce skill. Detection infrastructure is a known, budgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that out-of-tolerance dispensed volumes are accepted as accurate
rows.imp_rat: Accepting out-of-tolerance volumes as accurate propagates dosing error (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence (severity moderate), capped.
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated checkweighers with defined limits reliably reject out-of-tolerance volumes, making false acceptance uncommon. Verification against tolerance is fast with automated weight capture. Statistical acceptance rules are well-established, requiring little specialized judgement to apply. Automated rejection minimizes wasted accepted-out-of-spec product cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that dose-accuracy verification compromises aseptic conditions around sterile product and open containers
rows.imp_rat: Dose-accuracy verification compromising asepsis around open containers introduces contamination during checks (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Non-invasive checkweighing is now common and largely decouples dose verification from aseptic risk, serving this reasonably well. Designing aseptic-compatible dose checks adds some setup time but is routine. Automated in-line checkweighing reduces reliance on manual sampling skill significantly. In-line checkweighers add equipment cost but avoid destructive sampling waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that aggregate risk characterization masks a severe microbial or particulate hazard during dispensing
rows.imp_rat: Aggregate risk masking a severe hazard hides real contamination (relevance moderate) with high potential consequence (severity moderate-high), capped.
rows.sat_rat: Risk-ranking tools are widely used but aggregation routinely hides individual severe hazards. Disaggregating risk scores to find masked hazards adds analytical time. Seeing through aggregated risk ratings to a hidden severe hazard requires strong QRM judgement. A masked hazard reaching production causes catastrophic contamination costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of intervention types with uncharacterized operator reach, proximity, or intrusion into critical aseptic zones
rows.imp_rat: Uncharacterized operator intrusion into critical zones leaves contamination risk unquantified (relevance moderate) with moderate-high consequence (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Smoke studies help but operator intrusion characterization is often incomplete across intervention types. Characterizing operator reach and intrusion requires observation studies that take time. Judging reach and intrusion into critical zones demands ergonomic and aseptic expertise. Uncharacterized intrusions cause contamination with costly consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a fill-volume recipe is altered by a person lacking authorization during its lifecycle
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized recipe alteration can produce wrong fill volumes (relevance moderate) with dosing-safety consequences (severity moderate-high), capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: RBAC and electronic signature gates make unauthorized recipe alteration rare where they are properly implemented. Access control checks add little time once role-based permissions are set. Configuring authorization tiers needs a competent administrator but is well-understood. Role-based access is a standard, low-cost feature of current control systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of wetted surface area retaining product residue after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Residue retained on wetted surfaces after cleaning is a contamination concern (moderate severity) but is a cleaning-effectiveness metric peripheral to filling (lower relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning plus swab verification gives consistent results on accessible surfaces, with recurring gaps on hard-to-reach wetted geometry. Cleaning to a validated endpoint takes a set time and rinse verification adds a short check. Verifying residual surface residue depends on correct swab technique and sampling judgement. Swab tests, rinse water, and lab analysis are recurring but budgeted costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of shortfall between actual sanitizer contact and the approved requirement for wetted components
rows.imp_rat: Sanitizer contact shortfall on wetted components risks residual bioburden (severity) but is a cleaning-cycle concern tangential to the act of filling itself (lower relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Validated coverage and contact-time controls work well on mapped paths but shadowed or air-locked components can still receive short contact undetected. Ensuring adequate sanitizer contact time is built into validated cycles, extending the cleaning duration deliberately. Achieving full wetted-surface contact depends on correct setup and knowledge of coverage dead-legs, a moderate skill demand. Sanitizer volume and cycle energy are routine consumables with controlled cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting incorrect, contaminated, damaged, loose, or misaligned changeover components before production resumes
rows.imp_rat: Accepting contaminated or misaligned changeover components before resuming can affect fill quality, but this is a changeover-verification step of limited centrality, keeping relevance low with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Changeover checklists and part identification reduce errors, though misidentification and undetected damage still occur before restart. Pre-startup changeover-component checks add a brief verification step before resuming production. Recognizing correct, undamaged, and properly aligned changeover parts depends on operator familiarity and attention. Accepting a wrong or damaged part risks scrapped product and downtime, but the check itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of residual product remaining in the filling machine fluid pathways after flushing
rows.imp_rat: Residual product in fluid pathways after flushing carries contamination risk, but as a cleaning-effectiveness item it sits lower than core fill accuracy, so relevance stays low with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Defined flush volumes with endpoint checks clear most residual product, with occasional carryover in dead-legs. Flushing runs to a defined volume or endpoint within a predictable time. Flushing is largely automated or simple, requiring little specialized skill. Flush media and any product loss are routine, controlled costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the executed purchase order and the agreed machinery configuration, price, delivery, and compliance terms
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between the executed purchase order and the agreed machinery configuration, price, and compliance terms are a procurement/administrative error that is peripheral to the physical filling job itself (low relevance), though a mismatch can cause delivery of wrong equipment or compliance gaps (moderate severity). Constrained by the batch ceiling, importance stays at or below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Formal PO approval workflows and ERP controls make most terms consistent, but manual translation of negotiated terms into the PO still introduces occasional mismatches. PO review against agreed terms is a standard sign-off step that rarely adds major delay. Reconciling configuration, price, delivery, and compliance terms is routine procurement work needing moderate rather than scarce expertise. Discrepancies caught at PO stage are cheap to fix; those that pass through create pricing and delivery disputes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of filling machine frame movement during production after floor anchoring
rows.imp_rat: Frame movement during production after anchoring can degrade fill accuracy and cause wear, but it is a comparatively rare stability defect, so relevance to the core job stays low with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Correctly specified chemical or mechanical anchors and grouting reliably eliminate frame movement, with issues rare where standard practice is followed. Anchoring and grouting is a one-time install activity with well-defined cure and check time. Proper anchoring follows standard civil/mechanical practice within most installation teams' competence. Anchor bolts and grout are minor one-time costs relative to the machine.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incorrect parameter values stored in each product recipe profile
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect parameter values in a recipe profile can produce out-of-spec fills, but recipe setup is a configuration-time defect that is peripheral to continuous operation, keeping relevance modest.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management with range validation and approval workflows catches many errors, but manual data entry still lets incorrect values persist in profiles. Entering and storing recipe parameter values is quick but proofreading each profile adds time. Correct recipe authoring depends on a knowledgeable engineer, though templates reduce per-value judgement. A wrong stored value costs a bad run's product until detected, contained by verification steps.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a recovery action that does not match the actual filling-cycle interruption state
rows.imp_rat: Selecting a recovery action mismatched to the interruption state can cause improper restarts, but recovery-action selection is a peripheral edge case relative to the core filling job, so relevance is modest even though consequences can be moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Guided recovery prompts help on newer HMIs, but ambiguous interruption states still produce mismatched recovery choices. Choosing the correct recovery action can delay restart when the interruption state is unclear. Selecting the right recovery step depends on operator understanding of the interrupted cycle state, a skill-intensive judgement. A wrong recovery action can scrap in-process fills or trip further faults.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that dosing logic, alarms, interlocks, recipes, or audit functions malfunction after the software update
rows.imp_rat: Post-update malfunction of dosing logic, alarms, or interlocks could produce serious dosing or compliance failures (higher severity), but this software-update scenario is a specialized event rather than central to routine filling, keeping relevance low under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and validation protocols reduce post-update malfunctions, yet untested edge cases and unexpected interactions still slip through. Post-update regression testing of dosing logic, interlocks, and recipes adds significant validation time before release. Verifying software behavior across alarms, interlocks, and audit functions demands specialized automation and validation expertise. Formal software validation is resource-intensive but required in regulated filling operations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification fills outside approved quantity and repeatability tolerances after the upgrade
rows.imp_rat: Qualification fills outside approved tolerances after an upgrade signal a real accuracy problem, but this is a bounded post-upgrade verification error, limiting relevance under the ceiling despite moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Structured IQ/OQ/PQ qualification reliably confirms fill accuracy and repeatability, though marginal drift can still pass borderline criteria. Qualification fill runs after upgrade take defined time to gather statistically sufficient data. Designing and judging qualification fill acceptance requires validation and statistical competence. Test product and analysis consumed during qualification are a controlled, expected cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of trained staff responding incorrectly to critical machine hazards during competency verification
rows.imp_rat: Staff responding incorrectly to critical hazards is a training-verification error (moderate relevance to the machine job) but carries real safety consequences when it occurs (elevated severity), constrained by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented training and assessments reduce incorrect hazard responses, but some trained staff still respond wrongly under real conditions. Competency verification is scheduled training time that is generally well-managed. The need is fundamentally about building operator skill, and structured training programs directly address it. Training time is a recognized labor cost rather than waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the abnormal condition that triggered the machine alarm
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the abnormal condition behind an alarm delays correct response (moderate relevance) and prolongs downtime (moderate severity), a diagnostic aid held well below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm systems flag that something is wrong but often poorly discriminate the specific abnormal condition, leaving frequent misidentification. Time is lost while the operator diagnoses which underlying condition actually triggered a generic alarm. Correctly interpreting an alarm's true root cause leans heavily on experienced-operator judgement, especially with vague fault codes. Misdiagnosis leads to wrong corrective action and wasted product or downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine parameters set outside approved values for the next product or package format
rows.imp_rat: Parameters set outside approved values for a new format can produce defective packs (moderate relevance/severity), yet acceptance checks usually catch them before sustained production; capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management makes format-parameter setting fairly reliable, but manual overrides and un-managed settings still allow some out-of-approved values. Recipe download for a format changeover sets parameters quickly rather than through manual entry. Stored validated recipes reduce reliance on the operator remembering correct values for the next format. Out-of-approved parameters risk a scrapped first run, mitigated affordably by recipe control.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment of replacement sealing jaws, bars, or cutting blades after installation
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment of replacement sealing jaws or blades affects seal integrity (moderate relevance), but is detectable and correctable at install, so consequences are contained; held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alignment aids and test seals catch gross errors, but fine misalignment after installation still causes seal-quality issues fairly regularly. Jaw and blade alignment uses fixtures and shims but still requires trial seals to confirm, adding setup time. Precise alignment of sealing jaws and cutting blades is an experience-dependent manual craft. Misaligned jaws produce scrap seals and wasted film until corrected.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of qualification runs failing throughput or package-format acceptance criteria after the upgrade
rows.imp_rat: Qualification runs failing throughput or format criteria after an upgrade signal capability gaps (moderate relevance), but failures are caught during qualification before commercial use (moderate severity); held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Formal IQ/OQ/PQ practice makes qualification outcomes fairly predictable, though throughput or format criteria still fail often enough to require reruns. Qualification runs after an upgrade consume dedicated line time before production resumes. Designing and judging qualification acceptance needs validation expertise that is well established in regulated plants. Failed qualification runs waste material and extend downtime, a notable recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required preventive-maintenance activities omitted from the machine calendar
rows.imp_rat: Omitting preventive-maintenance activities from the calendar can erode reliability over time (moderate relevance), but effects are gradual and correctable (moderate severity); capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Where a CMMS is deployed, omission of scheduled PM activities is uncommon, making this one of the better-served needs. CMMS scheduling automates PM triggering so little time is lost deciding what is due. Following a generated PM calendar needs low skill; the system prompts the required tasks. CMMS licensing is modest and omitted PM would cost far more in failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of machine alignment shift after return to operating conditions
rows.imp_rat: Alignment shift after return to operating conditions can affect seal quality (moderate relevance), but is monitorable and re-adjustable, keeping consequences contained (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Cold-set alignment often drifts under operating conditions, and without post-warm-up verification these shifts recur commonly. Confirming alignment holds after warm-up requires a settling period and recheck that lengthens return-to-service. Anticipating and correcting thermal or load-induced alignment shift relies on seasoned technician knowledge. Alignment shift after return causes defects and repeated adjustment, wasting production time and material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which trial conditions differ from intended sterile fill-finish production
rows.imp_rat: Trial conditions differing from intended sterile fill-finish production undermines validation representativeness (moderate relevance), but consequences are recoverable through re-runs rather than catastrophic; capped by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Trials at vendor sites almost always diverge from intended production in product, environment, and scale, making this a chronic and largely unmitigated gap. Recreating true sterile fill-finish conditions for a trial is slow, and buyers usually accept abbreviated trials to keep the acquisition schedule. Designing a trial that faithfully mirrors intended aseptic production conditions demands rare process-representativeness expertise most teams lack. Fully representative trials with actual product and aseptic setup are prohibitively expensive, so buyers routinely settle for cheaper placebo or non-sterile trials.

rows.stk: EHS team, contracted waste disposal specialists
rows.source_job: Process and dispose of regulated waste
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of routing regulated machine waste through an unapproved disposal channel
rows.imp_rat: Routing regulated waste through an unapproved channel is peripheral to the machine's forming/filling/sealing job (low relevance), though a compliance breach can carry regulatory penalty (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Licensed-hauler practice exists but misclassification still routes regulated waste through unapproved channels. Routing waste through approved channels adds paperwork and scheduling time. Correctly classifying regulated machine waste requires environmental compliance knowledge that not all sites have in-house. Approved disposal channels cost more than uncontrolled routes, incentivizing shortcuts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unexpected filling-machine movement injures the operator during an intervention
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected machine movement injuring the operator is peripheral to the fill job (relevance low) but injury is severe when it happens (severity moderate-high).
rows.sat_rat: Guarding and interlocks make injury from unexpected motion uncommon. Safety interlocks stop motion promptly during interventions. Lockout procedures reduce reliance on operator caution alone. Safety systems carry cost but prevent injury losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required interventions that leave the triggering line condition unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Interventions leaving the triggering condition unresolved cause recurrence/rework (relevance moderate) with moderate consequence (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Interventions frequently address symptoms without resolving root cause, so recurrence is common. Unresolved conditions force repeat interventions, adding time. Fully resolving the triggering condition demands diagnostic skill. Unresolved conditions cause repeat losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of primary containers damaged during required filling interventions
rows.imp_rat: Containers damaged during interventions cause integrity loss (relevance moderate) with moderate consequence (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Container damage during interventions still occurs at an inconsistent rate depending on operator. Careful intervention to avoid damage slows the task. Avoiding container damage during interventions relies on operator dexterity. Damaged containers are a modest recurring loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of interrupted sterile drug-product flow into the filling zone during the production run
rows.imp_rat: Interrupted sterile flow into the filling zone is primarily a throughput delay (relevance low-moderate) with recoverable consequences (severity low-moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Buffered supply reduces flow interruptions but they still occur during the run. Product-flow interruptions directly reduce productive filling time. Restoring flow requires troubleshooting skill. Interrupted flow risks product hold-time loss, a cost factor.
