Positioning and leveling to coordinates is a standard install step with predictable duration using known tooling. Precision leveling requires a competent millwright but relies on well-understood laser and spirit-level methods. Rework of a mispositioned base is costly, but this is a one-time install expense rather than recurring.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supplier reliability and machine capability claims that cannot be corroborated through independent references
rows.imp_rat: Uncorroborated supplier reliability claims are a procurement due-diligence gap remote from operating the machine (low relevance) with moderate downstream consequences (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Reference checks and factory audits exist but are inconsistently applied, so many supplier claims still go independently unverified. Chasing independent references and site visits is time-consuming and often abbreviated under acquisition deadlines. Corroborating reliability claims requires skilled auditors who can probe references and interpret real operating data rather than marketing figures. Uncorroborated capability claims that prove false cause costly performance shortfalls after purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of targeted recipes, programs, configurations, and credentials remaining recoverable after erasure
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable recipes/credentials remaining after erasure is a data-hygiene/decommission concern peripheral to production (low relevance) with moderate confidentiality consequences (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Recoverable configurations and credentials frequently remain because industrial controllers lack routine secure-erase practice. Wiping recipes, programs, and credentials is quick if done but often skipped under time pressure. Secure erasure of embedded controllers and credentials requires IT/OT security skill rarely present at teardown. Data wiping tools are cheap but proper secure-erase verification is rarely budgeted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the machine is released to a party not authorized to accept custody
rows.imp_rat: Releasing the machine to an unauthorized custodian is a logistics/custody control matter peripheral to the core forming/filling/sealing job (low relevance) with moderate but recoverable consequences (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Chain-of-custody documentation is common but verification of recipient authority is inconsistently enforced. Verifying recipient authorization adds administrative lead time before custody transfer. Confirming a party is authorized to accept custody needs procurement and compliance judgement. Vetting recipients costs little beyond staff time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required setup checks, in-process checks, interventions, stoppages, or deviations omitted from the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Omitted checks/interventions from the batch record is a documentation-completeness gap, downstream of dispensing; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual records routinely have omissions unless electronic systems enforce completeness. Manual batch-record entry during the run competes with operating tasks for time. Complete documentation depends on operator diligence and training. Missing entries risk batch rejection, a large potential cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recorded fill results, operating readings, or container counts that are incorrect
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect recorded fill results/counts is a recording-accuracy error, tangential to actual dispensing accuracy; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Transcription errors persist in paper-based recording; electronic capture reduces them where deployed. Recording readings manually takes time away from operating. Accurate transcription depends on operator attention. Incorrect records can trigger costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of drug-product, component, or batch identifiers recorded incorrectly
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly recorded identifiers is a data-entry error, tangential to the dispensing act; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode/scanning where used makes identifier errors rare, but manual entry still errs occasionally. Identifier recording is a quick step. Correct identifier entry relies on verification discipline. Wrong identifiers risk mix-ups, a serious cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of gaps or inconsistencies in the documented sequence of filling events
rows.imp_rat: Gaps in the documented sequence of filling events is a record-completeness ambiguity, indirect to dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Sequence gaps and inconsistencies are common in manual logs. Documenting event sequence in order takes concurrent time. Maintaining a coherent sequence relies on operator record-keeping skill. Sequence gaps prompt investigations, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required filling entries lacking performer or verifier attribution
rows.imp_rat: Filling entries lacking attribution is an attribution error, tangential to dispensing execution; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Signature/attribution practice is well established but occasional omissions occur. Adding attribution signatures is a quick step. Attribution relies on procedural signing discipline. Missing attribution can invalidate records, a cost risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies among filled, rejected, sampled, and remaining container totals
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies among container totals is a reconciliation error, indirect to the dispensing act; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Total reconciliation discrepancies are a common recurring documentation problem. Reconciling container totals is an end-of-batch time burden. Accurate reconciliation requires careful counting discipline. Unreconciled discrepancies trigger costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required batch-record fields remaining incomplete at production handoff
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete batch-record fields at handoff is a documentation-completeness gap, downstream of dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Incomplete fields at handoff remain frequent without electronic enforcement. Completing all fields at handoff adds review time. Field completeness depends on operator thoroughness. Incomplete records delay release, a cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which dispensing pace falls below the throughput target during the production run
rows.imp_rat: Dispensing pace below throughput target relates to the CFJ's output but is a productivity concern, not sterility or accuracy; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Throughput shortfalls occur intermittently from accumulated small issues. Pace falling below target directly costs production time. Sustaining pace relies on operator responsiveness to minor issues. Lost pace reduces output value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time to reestablish target throughput after a filling interruption
rows.imp_rat: Time to reestablish throughput after interruption is a productivity issue (relevance low) with recoverable consequence (severity low).
rows.sat_rat: Throughput recovery time is inconsistent across events. Ramping back to target throughput after interruption takes measurable time. Efficient restart needs experienced operators. Slow ramp-up loses throughput value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conforming filled containers incorrectly classified as rejects
rows.imp_rat: Conforming containers rejected is a yield/waste issue (relevance low) with financial-only consequence (severity low).
rows.sat_rat: Detection tuning limits false rejects but some conforming units are still wrongly classified. False rejects are handled inline without extra time. Reject thresholds are automated, limiting skill dependence. False rejects discard good product, a direct avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of acceptable containers incorrectly segregated as nonconforming
rows.imp_rat: Acceptable containers segregated as nonconforming is a yield/waste issue (relevance low) with financial-only consequence (severity low).
rows.sat_rat: Sensitivity tuning reduces over-segregation but conservative settings still capture good units. Over-segregation is resolved during reconciliation time. Setting sensitivity to avoid over-rejection needs tuning expertise. Unnecessary segregation wastes acceptable containers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether successive containers remain within fill tolerance
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about successive containers within tolerance touches accuracy but is a monitoring-ambiguity issue; ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt caused by conflicting fill indications during the batch
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting fill indications touches accuracy confidence but is a monitoring-ambiguity issue; ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty after making a movement or intervention near the sterile field
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty after movement near the sterile field is aseptic-relevant but an ambiguity issue; ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt caused by conflicting indications about whether the sterile field remains intact
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting indications about sterile-field integrity is aseptic-relevant but an ambiguity issue; ranking mandates the ceiling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required calibration records that are missing or expired
rows.imp_rat: Missing or expired calibration records is a documentation-currency gap, indirect to dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration management systems with due-date tracking make missing or expired records uncommon. Checking calibration records adds brief setup time. Verifying calibration currency depends on record-keeping discipline more than skill. Missing calibration records force delays and re-calibration, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of fill positions omitted from the readiness assessment
rows.imp_rat: Fill positions omitted from readiness assessment is a coverage/documentation gap, indirect to dispensing; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Position-by-position checks are standard, but multi-head lines sometimes have positions omitted from assessment. Assessing all fill positions adds check time. Ensuring all positions are assessed depends on procedural thoroughness. Omitted positions let defects through, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of readiness results assigned to the wrong batch, configuration, or fill position
rows.imp_rat: Readiness results misassigned to wrong batch/position is a data-attribution error, indirect to dispensing; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Batch identifiers and MES linkage reduce misassignment, though manual steps still allow errors. Correct batch assignment adds minor recording time. Assigning readiness results to the right batch depends on operator diligence. Misassigned readiness results cause investigations and rework, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of fill-measurement drift between readiness confirmation and production start
rows.imp_rat: Fill-measurement drift between readiness and start touches accuracy but is a pre-production stability issue; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Short readiness-to-start intervals limit drift, but drift between confirmation and start can still occur. Drift between readiness and start relates to elapsed time before production. Detecting such drift requires attentive re-checking. Undetected drift produces out-of-spec fills, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of staged product, container, closure, or label items that do not match the scheduled batch
rows.imp_rat: Staged items not matching the scheduled batch is a mix-up/setup error with real consequence but peripheral to dispensing; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance and barcode verification make mismatched staged items uncommon. Verifying staged materials adds brief line-clearance time. Matching staged items to the batch depends on operator diligence. Mismatched materials cause reworks and rejects, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of critical-zone environmental conditions outside acceptance limits before production
rows.imp_rat: Critical-zone conditions out of limits before production is aseptic-relevant but pre-start and capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery-time studies and monitoring keep out-of-limit durations short and predictable in qualified zones. This need concerns shortening how long the critical zone stays out of limits before production. Restoring conditions relies on HVAC systems more than operator skill. Longer out-of-limit duration delays start and risks product, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required setup-readiness checks with missing or inconclusive evidence
rows.imp_rat: Setup-readiness checks with inconclusive evidence is a readiness-ambiguity gap, indirect to dispensing; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Setup checklists capture most evidence, but some checks still end up with inconclusive documentation. Compiling readiness evidence adds setup time. Ensuring complete evidence depends on procedural thoroughness. Missing evidence delays release, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required guards, interlocks, or sensors not ready at setup release
rows.imp_rat: Guards/interlocks not ready at setup release is a safety-readiness gap, peripheral to dispensing but with some consequence; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Automated interlock self-checks and setup checklists make unready guards or sensors at release uncommon. Confirming guards and interlocks adds brief setup time. Checking safety-system readiness depends on checklist adherence. Missing interlocks delay release and risk incidents, a cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel exposure to unintended machine motion or hazardous energy during changeover
rows.imp_rat: Personnel exposure to unintended motion during changeover is a safety error but occurs outside the dispensing act; ceiling applies despite real harm potential.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks, guarding and LOTO procedures make personnel exposure to hazardous energy during changeover rare. Lockout steps add brief time to changeover. Safe energy control depends on trained lockout-tagout adherence. An exposure incident carries high injury and downtime cost that safeguards prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required changeover actions lacking contemporaneous records
rows.imp_rat: Changeover actions lacking contemporaneous records is a documentation gap, indirect to dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Batch records and MES prompts capture most actions, but contemporaneous recording gaps still occur. Contemporaneous recording adds concurrent effort during changeover. Recording actions in real time depends on documentation discipline. Missing records force investigations, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of open-container exposure during an unplanned filling stoppage
rows.imp_rat: Open-container exposure during an unplanned stoppage is aseptic-relevant with real contamination stakes but ranking mandates the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Stoppage procedures and RABS closure limit exposure, but unplanned stoppages still leave containers exposed for variable periods. This need is about shortening open-container exposure duration during a stoppage. Reducing exposure time depends on prompt operator response. Longer exposure risks contaminating product, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unstable dispensing before startup fills meet acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Unstable startup dispensing duration touches the CFJ but is a transient startup issue with limited product loss; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Priming routines make stabilization fairly predictable, though it still takes several rejected fills. The need is explicitly about shortening the unstable dispensing period before fills are acceptable. Reducing stabilization time depends partly on operator priming technique. Longer instability wastes more startup product, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time that out-of-specification startup filling continues before detection
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-spec startup filling continuing before detection touches accuracy but is a transient detection-latency issue; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Startup checks and checkweighers limit undetected out-of-spec filling, though some lag persists. This need is about shortening how long out-of-spec startup filling continues before detection. Faster detection depends on monitoring and operator attention. Continued out-of-spec filling wastes product, a scaling cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile product lost through product-path connection leaks during startup
rows.imp_rat: Product lost through connection leaks at startup carries some sterility/loss consequence but is peripheral to dispensing; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic connectors reduce leaks, but startup connection leaks still occasionally waste product. Leak losses are not primarily about time. Preventing connection leaks depends on assembly skill. Product lost through startup connection leaks is a direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sterilization records that are missing, incomplete, or inconsistent
rows.imp_rat: Missing/inconsistent sterilization records carries sterility implications but is an evidentiary gap here; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Automated cycle printouts and record review make missing or inconsistent sterilization records fairly uncommon. Reviewing sterilization records adds brief time. Confirming record completeness depends on review diligence. Incomplete sterilization records force reprocessing or delays, a cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between scheduled filling start and sterile-state confirmation
rows.imp_rat: Delay until sterile-state confirmation is aseptic-relevant but a start-timing issue; ranking caps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled confirmation steps usually keep this gap short, with occasional documentation delays. This need concerns shortening the gap between scheduled start and sterile-state confirmation. Confirmation timing depends on procedure and QA availability. Delayed confirmation idles the line, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the applicable fill target or acceptance limits during verification
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in fill target/acceptance limits touches measurement accuracy but is a verification-definition issue; ranking caps at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Batch records and recipes usually state targets clearly, but ambiguity occasionally arises across document versions. Clarifying targets adds brief verification time. Interpreting the applicable fill target requires reading batch records correctly. Ambiguous targets cause acceptance errors, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of verification results assigned to the wrong fill position or production interval
rows.imp_rat: Verification results misassigned to fill positions is a data-integrity error, tangential to dispensing itself; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Structured data capture reduces misassignment, but manual recording still produces occasional mix-ups. Correct result assignment adds minor recording time. Assigning results to the right position depends on operator care during recording. Misassigned results cause acceptance errors, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of verification results that are missing or transcribed incorrectly
rows.imp_rat: Missing or mistranscribed verification results is a data-integrity error, indirect to dispensing execution; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Automated data capture where present is reliable, but manual transcription still yields missing or erroneous entries. Recording results is a quick step. Accurate transcription depends on operator attention, an error-prone manual task. Missing or wrong results force re-testing, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of out-of-target filling while verification is pending
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-target filling while verification pends is both waste and accuracy-relevant but a transient state; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: In-process checks limit the window, but verification lag still allows some out-of-target filling. This need concerns shortening out-of-target filling while verification is pending. Limiting this duration depends on prompt checking discipline. Out-of-target filling wastes product until corrected, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed batches with required batch records missing or incomplete
rows.imp_rat: Completed batches with missing batch records is a documentation-completeness gap, downstream of dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and EBR make wholly missing records uncommon, though incompleteness still recurs. Missing records force rework that delays release. Ensuring completeness relies on structured review by trained staff. Incomplete batch records can block release of otherwise good product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a completed batch reaches release review with critical deviations unresolved
rows.imp_rat: A completed batch reaching release with unresolved deviations is a review-gate error, downstream of dispensing; capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-record review workflows dependably flag open critical deviations before review completes. Review gates add process time before release readiness. Ensuring deviations are closed relies on QA review discipline. Catching unresolved deviations before release avoids expensive downstream failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unresolved product container closure or label reconciliation discrepancies for a completed batch
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved closure/label reconciliation discrepancies is a batch-accounting ambiguity, downstream of dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Component and label accountability procedures usually close cleanly, with occasional residual discrepancies. Reconciliation adds a closing step to batch completion. Resolving reconciliation discrepancies needs careful record review. Unresolved discrepancies can delay release and trigger investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch-record entries that are unattributable illegible unjustifiably altered or not contemporaneous
rows.imp_rat: Batch entries unattributable/illegible/altered is a records-integrity gap, indirect to the dispensing act; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: EBR with audit trails strongly enforces attribution, though paper steps still produce nonconforming entries. Fixing entry defects delays record review. Contemporaneous, legible, attributable entries depend heavily on operator discipline. Data-integrity findings carry serious regulatory and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an aseptic excursion or intervention remains unassessed at release review
rows.imp_rat: Unassessed aseptic excursion at release review carries real sterility risk but is a review-process gap rather than the dispensing act itself; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation systems usually capture excursions, but linkage of every intervention to release review is imperfect. Assessing every excursion adds review time. Judging whether an aseptic excursion is fully assessed needs experienced QA/microbiology input. Missing an unassessed excursion risks a compromised release and costly recall.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required environmental monitoring results unavailable at release review
rows.imp_rat: Missing environmental monitoring results at release is a documentation-availability gap, downstream of dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: EM sampling and reporting are systematic, but incubation times inherently leave results pending at review. Waiting for outstanding EM results is a common cause of delayed release readiness. Obtaining EM results depends on lab throughput, not overseer skill. Delays hold inventory and tie up capital in unreleased batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required filling records that are incomplete inaccurate or unattributable
rows.imp_rat: Filling records incomplete/inaccurate/unattributable is a data-integrity gap, indirect to the dispensing act; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic batch records improve completeness, but paper hybrids still yield incomplete or unattributable entries. Correcting record deficiencies adds review and rework time. Complete, attributable records depend on operator diligence and training. Deficient records can force batch rejection despite sound product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of batch disposition occurring while critical filling deviations remain unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Batch disposition with unresolved filling deviations is a downstream quality-review error, only indirectly tied to the CFJ of dispensing sterile product; severity capped by ranking though real consequences exist.
rows.sat_rat: Quality systems reliably hold disposition until critical deviations close, making release-with-open-deviations rare. Deviation-gating adds review time before disposition, an accepted cost. Disposition decisions require QA judgement but follow defined rules. Preventing premature release avoids costly field actions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of scheduled production lost to extended filling-machine changeovers
rows.imp_rat: Extended changeovers lost to production is a schedule/throughput issue (relevance low) with recoverable consequence (severity low).
rows.sat_rat: SMED and format-part kits help somewhat, but aseptic changeover remains long and a chronic time drain. Changeovers on aseptic lines are lengthy and directly consume scheduled production time. Format changeovers demand skilled setup and re-sterilization, prolonging the task. Extended changeovers waste capacity and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of delayed batch starts caused by incomplete line clearance setup or aseptic readiness
rows.imp_rat: Delayed batch starts from incomplete readiness are schedule issues (relevance low) with recoverable consequence (severity low).
rows.sat_rat: Readiness checklists help, but incomplete setup or clearance still delays starts with some regularity. This need targets delayed batch starts from readiness gaps, a recurring source of lost start time. Achieving aseptic readiness on schedule relies on coordinated skilled preparation. Delayed starts waste scheduled capacity and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile product discarded while establishing stable filling conditions
rows.imp_rat: Discarded product while establishing stable conditions is a yield/waste issue (relevance low) with financial-only consequences (severity low).
rows.sat_rat: Startup losses are predictable and bounded but not tightly minimized in standard practice. Establishing stable conditions consumes startup time before saleable output. Minimizing startup waste depends partly on setup skill and machine familiarity. Product used to stabilize the line is direct sterile-product waste that recurs every startup.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile product lost outside containers during filling
rows.imp_rat: Sterile product lost outside containers is a yield/waste issue (relevance low) with mostly financial consequence (severity low-moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Anti-drip nozzles and alignment controls limit external loss consistently. Spillage cleanup can require brief pauses but is usually minor. Reducing spillage depends on nozzle setup and alignment by skilled staff. Product lost outside containers is direct sterile-product waste, the core concern here.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of discrepancy between sterile product input and reconciled output for each batch
rows.imp_rat: Input-output reconciliation discrepancy is an accounting/ambiguity error, indirect to dispensing itself; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard reconciliation catches gross discrepancies, but small unexplained variances recur. Reconciliation is an end-of-batch step that adds closing time. Accurate reconciliation depends on disciplined counting and record-keeping. Unexplained discrepancies can trigger investigations and holds costing time and product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of product or lot identity being assigned to the wrong filled containers
rows.imp_rat: Wrong lot identity on containers has real patient-traceability consequence but is a labeling/identity error downstream of dispensing; capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance, serialization and print verification make wrong-identity assignment very rare. Identity verification is a fast automated check. Barcode/serialization removes reliance on operators to confirm identity manually. A mislabeled lot could cause enormous recall costs, so control here averts large exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of aseptic-control evidence missing or unverifiable for a production run
rows.imp_rat: Missing aseptic-control evidence for a run is an evidentiary/ambiguity error, peripheral to physically dispensing product; held at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Paper-heavy records mean aseptic-control evidence is sometimes incomplete or unverifiable, a recurring gap. Chasing missing evidence delays review completion. Reconstructing or verifying aseptic-control evidence demands knowledgeable staff. Missing evidence can force batch rejection despite good product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of environmental monitoring records containing missing, inconsistent, or untraceable entries
rows.imp_rat: Environmental monitoring records with untraceable entries is a data-integrity gap, indirect to dispensing; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Paper and hybrid EM records still commonly contain missing or inconsistent entries. Correcting EM record gaps delays review. Complete EM records depend on technician recording discipline. Rework of records costs labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applicable sterility-assurance requirements lacking traceable supporting evidence
rows.imp_rat: Sterility-assurance requirements lacking traceable evidence is an evidentiary gap, indirect to dispensing; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement-evidence traceability is often incomplete, leaving gaps in supporting evidence. Evidence traceability is checked at review, not time-urgent. Mapping requirements to evidence needs experienced compliance staff. Traceability maintenance is labor-intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required contamination-control records that are missing or incomplete
rows.imp_rat: Missing contamination-control records is a records-completeness gap, indirect to the dispensing act; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Batch record review catches most gaps but incomplete contamination-control records recur. Missing records force delays while located or reconstructed. Record completeness relies on operator documentation discipline. Chasing missing records costs labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contamination-control evidence contains unauthorized or unverifiable entries
rows.imp_rat: Contamination evidence with unverifiable entries is an integrity error, indirect to dispensing execution; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic audit trails and access controls make unauthorized entries largely detectable in modern systems. Verification of entries adds review time. Detecting unverifiable entries requires review diligence. Audit trail systems add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material discrepancies among batch, environmental, intervention, and deviation records
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies among batch/environmental/deviation records is a record-consistency ambiguity, indirect to dispensing; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Manual records across systems create recurring discrepancies among batch, EM, and deviation data. Reconciling discrepancies extends review time. Cross-record reconciliation requires careful analyst attention. Reconciliation labor is a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of changes affecting aseptic conditions that lack documented regulatory assessment
rows.imp_rat: Changes affecting aseptic conditions lacking regulatory assessment is a compliance-documentation gap; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Change control usually requires regulatory assessment, but some aseptic changes proceed without documented review. Regulatory assessment is part of change timelines. Regulatory impact judgement needs specialist knowledge. Assessment labor adds to change cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required sterility-assurance evidence missing from the disposition review
rows.imp_rat: Missing sterility-assurance evidence at disposition is a review-completeness gap, downstream of dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Review checklists reduce but do not eliminate missing evidence at disposition. Missing evidence delays disposition review. Compiling complete evidence packages requires diligent QA staff. Chasing missing evidence costs labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that records used for disposition are incomplete, altered, or untraceable
rows.imp_rat: Disposition records incomplete/altered/untraceable is a data-integrity gap, downstream of dispensing; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: ALCOA+ practices and audit trails in electronic systems substantially reduce incomplete or untraceable records. Data integrity checks add review time. Detecting altered or untraceable records needs trained reviewers. Data integrity controls carry system cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterility-assurance records associated with the wrong batch
rows.imp_rat: Sterility records tied to the wrong batch is a data-attribution error, indirect to the dispensing act; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Batch record systems reduce misassociation, but manual EM records still get linked to wrong batches occasionally. Misassociation is caught at review, adding time. Correct batch linkage requires attentive record management. Correcting misfiled records costs labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of validation evidence records that cannot be traced to their source
rows.imp_rat: Validation evidence untraceable to source is a traceability gap, indirect to the dispensing act; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validation packages are structured, but source traceability gaps still appear on audit. Tracing validation evidence to source can be slow. Maintaining traceability needs organized validation staff. Document management systems carry cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of equipment capability requirements lacking traceable validation evidence
rows.imp_rat: Missing traceable validation evidence for capability requirements is documentation-centric, indirect to dispensing; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability matrices are standard practice but incomplete linkage is a common inspection finding, keeping this only acceptable. Building traceability matrices is time-consuming manual linkage work. Establishing requirement-to-evidence traceability requires disciplined validation-engineering rigor. Missing traceability triggers audit findings and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of qualification executions invalidated by departures from approved conditions
rows.imp_rat: Qualification executions invalidated by departures is rework in validation, indirect to the dispensing CFJ; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Departures from approved conditions still invalidate a meaningful fraction of runs, so this remains a real recurring pain. Invalidated executions cause repeat runs that heavily extend timelines. Maintaining approved conditions throughout execution demands sustained operator discipline. Invalidated executions waste materials, labor, and slot time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified production demand cannot be sustained while meeting delivered-volume and aseptic requirements
rows.imp_rat: Inability to sustain demand while meeting volume/aseptic requirements touches the CFJ but is a capacity concern; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Capacity is usually specified reasonably well, but sustained-demand-versus-aseptic tradeoffs sometimes surface as gaps. Throughput/capacity assessment adds modeling time during specification. Balancing production demand with aseptic and volume requirements needs process-engineering expertise. Undersized capability forces costly capacity add-ons later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among contamination findings in design records, test records, and proposed dispensing conditions
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting contamination findings across records is an evidentiary-consistency ambiguity, indirect to dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Reviews catch major conflicts but subtle record inconsistencies persist. Reconciling contamination findings across design, test, and conditions records is slow manual work. Detecting conflicts across record sets requires cross-disciplinary expertise. Unreconciled conflicts cause disputes and re-investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the criteria for judging aseptic-field intervention exposure
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in judging intervention exposure is a criteria-definition gap, indirect to dispensing; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Some acceptance guidance exists but exposure-judgement criteria remain ambiguous in practice. Defining exposure-judgement criteria adds specification effort. Setting clear exposure criteria requires aseptic expertise. Ambiguous criteria cause inconsistent acceptance and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which intervention evidence conditions differ from routine and worst-case sterile fill-finish conditions
rows.imp_rat: Intervention evidence not matching worst-case conditions is a validation-representativeness error, tangential to actively dispensing product; ranking caps it low.
rows.sat_rat: Media fills approximate real conditions but often diverge from worst-case reality. Aligning intervention evidence conditions to routine and worst-case fills adds planning time. Matching evidence conditions to real fill-finish scenarios needs expertise. Divergent conditions produce false assurance and later failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts between documented intervention claims and observed aseptic filling machine behavior
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts between intervention claims and observed behavior is a validation-consistency error, indirect to dispensing; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Some verification occurs, but claims and actual machine behavior are inconsistently reconciled. Comparing claimed interventions to observed behavior requires observation and analysis time. Detecting claim-vs-behavior gaps needs experienced observers. Undetected discrepancies cause contamination and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that intervention evidence represents a machine configuration different from the proposed aseptic filling configuration
rows.imp_rat: Intervention evidence from a different config is a validation-representativeness error, indirect to dispensing; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Change control tracks configuration but evidence sometimes reflects a non-representative setup. Confirming configuration equivalence between evidence and proposal takes cross-checking time. Judging configuration equivalence requires detailed machine knowledge. Evidence from a different configuration invalidates assurance, causing rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of critical-zone exposure required to complete routine and non-routine interventions
rows.imp_rat: Critical-zone exposure duration during interventions is directly aseptic-relevant, but ranking mandates the ceiling despite genuine contamination stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Isolators/RABS and procedures shorten exposure, though non-routine interventions still prolong it. Reducing critical-zone exposure duration is a recognized design and procedure goal but interventions still take variable time. Minimizing exposure time depends on well-trained operators and good design. Prolonged exposure raises contamination risk and rejection costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical dispensing parameters and events missing from batch evidence
rows.imp_rat: Critical dispensing parameters missing from batch evidence is an evidentiary gap, indirect to the dispensing act; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated data capture and batch review make most critical parameters present, with occasional gaps. Verifying batch evidence completeness takes review time. Knowing which critical parameters must appear requires process expertise. Missing parameters delay release and cause investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of dispensing records not attributable to the affected batches and containers
rows.imp_rat: Records not attributable to affected batches is a traceability gap, indirect to dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: MES and batch-record systems reliably attribute records to batches, with rare exceptions. Batch/container attribution is largely automated, requiring little added time. Maintaining attribution needs standard quality-system competence. Unattributed records cause investigation and possible batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that dispensing records are altered or deleted without attributable history
rows.imp_rat: Dispensing records altered without attributable history is a data-integrity error, indirect to the dispensing act; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Part -compliant audit trails make undetected alteration rare in modern systems. Audit trails capture history automatically without added time. Reviewing audit trails requires modest data-integrity awareness. Robust audit trails avoid costly data-integrity remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of inconsistent identifiers and timestamps across dispensing records
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent identifiers/timestamps across records is a data-integrity error, indirect to the dispensing act; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Time synchronization and unique IDs are standard, though cross-system inconsistencies occasionally arise. Synchronized system clocks and automated timestamps minimize manual effort. Consistent identifiers require configuration discipline but little expertise. Inconsistent identifiers cause reconciliation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts among dispensing, batch, and quality records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts among dispensing/batch/quality records is a record-consistency ambiguity, indirect to dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Batch record review is mature but siloed systems still yield conflicts. Cross-checking dispensing, batch, and quality records for conflicts is time-consuming. Spotting record conflicts requires familiarity with all record systems. Record conflicts trigger investigations and delayed release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of alarms, interventions, and deviations not linked to their assessment and disposition records
rows.imp_rat: Alarms/deviations not linked to disposition records is a traceability gap, indirect to dispensing; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation and alarm management systems provide links but coverage gaps remain. Linking alarms, interventions, and deviations to disposition records takes manual effort. Maintaining these links needs disciplined quality-system knowledge. Unlinked events cause investigation delays and inspection findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of record entries and approvals not attributable to responsible personnel
rows.imp_rat: Entries/approvals not attributable to personnel is an attribution error, indirect to dispensing; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Part -signatures make unattributed entries very uncommon in current systems. Electronic signatures attribute entries automatically with no added time. Attribution requires only basic system access discipline. Strong attribution prevents costly data-integrity findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of dispensing records not linked to the applicable software, recipe, and machine-configuration versions
rows.imp_rat: Dispensing records not linked to software/recipe versions is a traceability gap, indirect to dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Version control and MES linkage are mature, though legacy systems occasionally break traceability. Associating records with software/recipe/config versions is largely automated, saving time. Version control needs IT/validation knowledge but is well-established. Unlinked versions cause investigation and remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which induced faults differ from credible production failures
rows.imp_rat: Induced faults differing from credible failures is a test-representativeness error, indirect to dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Induced faults often diverge from real failures, undermining confidence in the assessment. Designing realistic faults extends test preparation time. Making induced faults credibly mirror real production failures depends on deep failure-mode expertise that few possess. Realistic fault simulation may require special fixtures and material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical fault scenario cannot be executed without jeopardizing aseptic conditions or personnel safety
rows.imp_rat: Inability to execute fault scenarios without jeopardizing asepsis/safety is a testability constraint, indirect to routine dispensing; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Many critical fault scenarios simply cannot be safely executed, so they go untested or are only simulated. Designing safe fault-injection tests takes considerable planning time. Executing critical fault scenarios without breaching sterility or safety demands rare specialized engineering skill. Safe test rigs and aborted product runs are costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of fault trials with missing or altered control-event, command, status, alarm, or audit evidence
rows.imp_rat: Fault trials with missing control/audit evidence is a test-data-integrity gap, indirect to the dispensing act; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence for fault trials is frequently incomplete because sources are not synchronized or fully logged. Collecting all event, alarm, and audit evidence per trial is time-intensive. Knowing which evidence streams to capture needs test-engineering skill. Multi-source data capture adds tooling cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that approved recipe parameters change during transfer into the aseptic filling machine
rows.imp_rat: Recipe parameters changing during transfer touches dispensing accuracy but is a data-transfer integrity issue; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Checksum-verified recipe download largely prevents parameter drift during transfer where implemented, though manual re-keying remains a risk. Verifying recipe transfer integrity adds a checking step before each run. Understanding transfer paths requires integration knowledge that is not universal. Checksums and validation add negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the approved active recipe cannot be recovered intact after recipe-data loss
rows.imp_rat: Inability to recover the approved recipe after data loss is a data-resilience error, indirect to routine dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Regular backups make intact recovery likely, but untested restores occasionally fail when needed. Restoring a recipe from backup is reasonably quick when backups exist. Recovery needs IT competence but follows established procedures. Backup infrastructure is a routine, affordable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in linking each fill-volume recipe to its identity, version, approval status, and lifecycle state
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity linking recipe to version/lifecycle state is a configuration-traceability gap, indirect to dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Structured version and status fields reduce ambiguity, though inconsistent naming conventions still cause confusion. Confirming recipe identity and state is fast when metadata is displayed. Interpreting version and state fields requires modest familiarity. Metadata tagging is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical failure and exception scenarios remaining unevaluated
rows.imp_rat: Unevaluated failure/exception scenarios relate to design robustness rather than the act of dispensing; ranking caps this at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Structured risk methods leave many edge-case scenarios unevaluated, so coverage remains incomplete in practice. Working through every failure and exception scenario extends assessment timelines significantly. Enumerating credible exception paths depends on deep process and equipment expertise that is scarce. The analytical effort is labor-heavy but bounded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of dispensing-related actions missing from the audit trail
rows.imp_rat: Dispensing actions missing from the audit trail is a data-integrity gap, indirect to dispensing execution; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed systems capture most dispensing actions, but events at system boundaries or manual steps can still escape the trail. Automatic logging captures actions in real time with no added delay. Comprehensive capture requires no operator skill once the trail is enabled. Audit logging is a standard low-cost capability.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that dispensing audit records are altered or deleted without trace
rows.imp_rat: Audit records altered without trace is a data-integrity error, indirect to physically dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Write-protected, tamper-evident audit trails make untraceable alteration or deletion rare where properly deployed. Tamper-evident logging operates continuously without added time. Protecting records needs correct IT configuration rather than operator skill. Secure storage is a modest infrastructure cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of dispensing audit entries lacking unambiguous actor attribution
rows.imp_rat: Audit entries lacking actor attribution is an attribution/ambiguity error, tangential to dispensing; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Unique user authentication gives unambiguous actor attribution in most compliant systems, though shared accounts still occur in older setups. Attribution is logged instantly with each authenticated action. Unique logins remove reliance on operator diligence for attribution. Individual accounts add trivial administrative cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of audit entries not linked to the applicable batch, container, recipe version, and dispensing step
rows.imp_rat: Audit entries not linked to batch/container/recipe is a traceability ambiguity, indirect to dispensing; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Many systems capture batch/recipe context automatically, but cross-system entries frequently lack full linkage. Linking audit entries to batch and container context is automatic when captured contextually. Contextual linking needs proper system design rather than user skill. Context capture is a low-cost configuration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recorded dispensing changes lacking prior and revised values
rows.imp_rat: Recorded dispensing changes lacking prior/revised values is an audit-trail gap, tangential to actual dispensing; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Part- -compliant audit trails routinely record prior and revised values, making omissions rare on modern equipment. Capturing before/after values is automatic and adds no time in compliant systems. No special skill is needed once audit trail is configured to record old and new values. This is a built-in feature carrying no extra cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of dispensing overrides lacking recorded rationale or authorization evidence
rows.imp_rat: Dispensing overrides lacking rationale is an authorization-evidence gap, indirect to the dispensing act; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Forced-comment fields help, but rationale quality and completeness remain inconsistent in practice. Prompting for rationale at override time adds a brief pause. Deciding a valid rationale still depends on operator judgement and discipline. Override capture is inexpensive to implement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement between audit entries and corresponding dispensing, control, and batch records
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement between audit entries and batch records is a data-consistency error, indirect to dispensing; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Disagreements between separate systems are a recurring integrity finding because records are seldom natively unified. Reconciling audit against batch records is a slow manual comparison. Detecting subtle disagreements requires experienced review judgement. Reconciliation labor is significant during investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the authorized filling capability fails aseptic qualification before planned commercial production
rows.imp_rat: Authorized capability failing aseptic qualification before production has real consequence but is a pre-commercial validation risk; ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on supplier claims and pre-authorization reviews that frequently miss latent aseptic design gaps, leaving a real and recurring chance of qualification failure after commitment. Aseptic qualification failures surface late in the project timeline, and the purchase executor has no fast signal at authorization time to gauge downstream qualification risk. Assessing whether an authorized capability will pass media fills and aseptic qualification demands deep sterile-process expertise the executor rarely holds and must borrow from quality and engineering. A failed qualification strands committed capital and forces costly remediation, an exposure the executor typically cannot quantify at the decision point.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recorded end-of-run totals and operating results differing from source values
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between recorded totals and source values is a data-reconciliation defect, administratively significant but not central to the fill job. Capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Automated counting and PLC data logging make recorded totals largely faithful to source values, though manual transcription and sensor miscounts still introduce recurring deviations. Reconciling recorded totals against source values is a periodic manual review that consumes time when discrepancies must be traced. Accurate end-of-run reporting depends on operators reading and transcribing counters correctly, a vigilance-dependent step where automation is absent. Discrepancies between recorded and actual totals cause inventory and yield accounting errors rather than direct material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed maintenance and service events omitted from the filling machine history
rows.imp_rat: Omitted service events from machine history is a traceability recordkeeping issue, peripheral to filling and recoverable. Capped.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on manual paper logs or loosely enforced CMMS entries that technicians routinely skip under time pressure, so completed events are frequently missing from the record. Logging a service event takes little time, but the delay in when technicians actually record it (often at shift end or not at all) is where omissions creep in. Recording a maintenance event requires no special skill, yet it depends heavily on individual discipline and memory, which is inconsistent across a crew. The direct cost of capturing an event is negligible, so cost is not the pain — the loss is a corrupted history, not money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of maintenance entries missing machine identity, component, date, work, parts, personnel, or approval details
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete maintenance log fields is a recordkeeping defect that does not stop filling; consequence is traceability friction. Capped low.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS templates and required fields improve completeness, yet manual entry still leaves records missing components, personnel, or approval details. Completing detailed maintenance records adds administrative time after the physical work is done. Recording identity, parts, and approvals needs no special skill, only consistent discipline. Documentation is cheap in materials but incomplete records cost heavily in audit findings and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of credentials, network settings, recipes, production records, and proprietary configurations remaining after erasure
rows.imp_rat: Residual credentials/configs after erasure is an end-of-life data-security matter with no bearing on the filling CFJ; severity limited to isolated data exposure. Well under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standardized, verified wipe practice for industrial fill machinery, so credentials and proprietary recipes are very commonly left resident on retired equipment. Wiping credentials, recipes and configs is quick where a documented procedure exists, but hunting across HMIs, PLCs, drives and network switches is slow and often skipped. Thorough erasure of embedded controllers and proprietary configuration requires IT/OT security knowledge that decommissioning crews frequently lack. Data sanitization is cheap in principle, so the barrier is attention rather than money spent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical product, container, sterility, operating, and acceptance conditions omitted from the trial request
rows.imp_rat: Omitted trial-request conditions is a pre-production planning gap, peripheral to the machine actually filling; errors are caught before real runs. Capped.
rows.sat_rat: There is no widely standardized complete trial-request template, so critical conditions are frequently omitted and only discovered when trial results prove inconclusive. Preparing a thorough trial request covering all product, container, and sterility conditions is slow and often rushed. Knowing which operating and acceptance conditions are critical for a given fill demands deep process and product expertise. Omitted trial conditions are cheap to specify but costly when they cause invalid or repeated trials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of filling machinery requirements lacking measurable acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Requirements lacking measurable acceptance criteria is an upstream documentation ambiguity; it only indirectly affects the CFJ of filling and its consequences are diffuse and recoverable. Capped low per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many requirements remain qualitative because standard practice tolerates narrative specs, leaving acceptance open to interpretation and inconsistent outcomes. Defining measurable acceptance criteria for each requirement is time-consuming and often deferred under schedule pressure. Writing quantifiable, testable acceptance criteria requires strong validation and engineering judgement that is in short supply. Vague criteria are cheap to write but expensive later through disputed acceptance and re-testing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of change in machine-frame level after load transfer and settling
rows.imp_rat: Frame level change after load transfer and settling affects long-term alignment but is an installation/settling error remote from active operation; capped low with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Mature practice (grouting baseplates, machined leveling pads, re-check after settling) handles most cases, but frame level drifting after load transfer on flexible or uncured floors remains a recurring gap that the status quo does not fully eliminate. Precision leveling with feet, shims and laser or precision spirit levels is a known but time-consuming iterative process, especially waiting for settling after load transfer. Getting frame level to hold after settling depends heavily on an experienced millwright's judgement about anchoring, grouting and re-checking, so results vary with who does the work. Standard adjustable feet, anchor bolts and grout are inexpensive, though re-leveling labor and occasional epoxy grouting add modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of loose fasteners remaining undetected during the scheduled inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected loose fasteners during inspection touch on machine integrity but this is a maintenance-detection gap rather than the running function itself; capped low by ranking, with modest severity since undetected fasteners could eventually cause faults.
rows.sat_rat: Torque-marking and checklists help, but manual inspection routinely leaves some loosening fasteners undetected between intervals. Fastener checks are quick when included on a checklist but comprehensive checking of all fasteners takes time. Detecting loose fasteners still relies on manual torque checks and technician attentiveness across many points. A missed loose fastener can cause failures far more costly than the checking effort.

rows.source_job: Back up machine configurations and settings
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required programs, recipes, parameters, and configuration records omitted from the backup
rows.imp_rat: Omitting programs, recipes, and configuration from backup risks recovery difficulty but is a data-management error, not the core operating function; relevance low, severity modest since recovery may still be possible.
rows.sat_rat: Where backup routines exist they are fairly complete, but ad hoc parameter changes and undocumented configs still occasionally escape the backup. Automated or scripted backups of recipes and parameters run quickly with little downtime. Executing a defined backup procedure requires modest IT skill once set up. Storage is cheap and backups avert costly reconfiguration effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of viable machine configurations omitted from the market review
rows.imp_rat: Omitting viable configurations from a market review is an upstream evaluation shortfall (low relevance) with low, recoverable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: The aseptic-machinery vendor pool is relatively small and well-documented, so major viable configurations are seldom entirely overlooked. Market scans of viable configurations take time but trade shows, directories, and consultants make reasonable coverage achievable within schedule. Knowing the full landscape of viable OFS/FFS configurations relies on market-savvy experts, though the supplier field is fairly well known. Missing a viable configuration risks overpaying or foregoing a better fit, but the concentrated supplier base limits how much is truly missed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of candidate specifications compared under non-equivalent operating conditions
rows.imp_rat: Comparing candidate specifications under non-equivalent conditions is a selection/analysis error remote from running the machine (low relevance) with low, correctable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: There is no enforced common specification basis across vendors, so apples-to-oranges comparisons frequently slip into evaluations undetected. Normalizing candidate specs to equivalent operating conditions is a laborious manual reconciliation that stretches the evaluation phase. Recognizing when vendor specs are quoted under non-equivalent conditions requires experienced technical evaluators who can decode differing assumptions. Comparing on unlike bases can lead to selecting a mis-costed or underperforming machine, an expensive downstream error.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required machine configuration, option, and accessory details omitted from quotation requests
rows.imp_rat: Omitting configuration details from quotation requests is an upstream procurement administrative error (low relevance) with easily-corrected consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized RFQ templates and vendor configurators cover most details reliably, but incomplete option capture still recurs on complex machines. Building configuration and accessory detail into an RFQ is a bounded task, though iterative clarifications with vendors stretch the timeline. Comprehensive quotation requests rely on procurement-engineering experience with option catalogs, a moderately teachable but still error-prone skill. Omitted options in the RFQ lead to change orders and price surprises later, though these are usually correctable before contract.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of machine movement under operating vibration after anchoring
rows.imp_rat: Machine movement under vibration after anchoring affects stability of forming/sealing precision, but it is a one-time installation error; relevance is limited and severity moderate, held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Proper anchoring and vibration isolation reliably hold modern machines steady, so operating-vibration movement is uncommon when done correctly. Anchoring and leveling is a one-time installation task with predictable duration. Standard leveling and anchoring practice needs competent millwrights but not rare expertise. Anchors and isolation pads are modest, well-understood installation costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration between a filling activity and its entry in the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Delay between activity and its batch-record entry is a contemporaneity/documentation gap with modest consequence; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Contemporaneous recording is expected but real-time entry gaps occur under workload. Contemporaneous entry competes with operating duties, causing delays. Timely recording relies on operator discipline. Delayed entry risks data-integrity findings, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of corrected filling entries that obscure the original entry or correction rationale
rows.imp_rat: Corrected entries obscuring the original is a records-legibility error, peripheral to dispensing execution; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Single-line-through correction practice is well ingrained, keeping this fairly reliable. Proper correction practice adds minimal time. Good-documentation-practice corrections are trained and routine. Obscured corrections risk data-integrity findings, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of filling record entries that cannot be read unambiguously
rows.imp_rat: Filling entries that cannot be read unambiguously is a legibility ambiguity, peripheral to the dispensing act; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic records eliminate legibility issues where used; paper still has occasional unreadable entries. Legible entry takes marginally more time. Legibility depends on operator handwriting or electronic entry. Illegible entries cause clarification cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of downstream congestion blocking filled-container discharge during the production run
rows.imp_rat: Downstream congestion blocking discharge is a flow bottleneck with minor consequence, peripheral to the dispensing act; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Accumulation tables and sensors buffer congestion so blocking is fairly infrequent. Downstream congestion blocking discharge idles the filler, wasting time. Clearing congestion is routine and low-skill. Blockage causes downtime rather than direct product loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of sterile product discarded when unplanned stoppages exceed allowable hold conditions
rows.imp_rat: Product discarded when stoppages exceed hold conditions is contingent waste, peripheral to dispensing; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Hold-time exceedance reliably triggers scrapping with little recovery option. Exceeding hold conditions during stoppages forces discard after time elapses. Judging hold-time limits requires validated expertise. Product discarded for exceeding hold limits is a significant direct loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of sterile product discarded while filling conditions stabilize after startup or restart
rows.imp_rat: Product discarded while conditions stabilize after restart is startup waste, peripheral to the dispensing job; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Startup discard is a predictable, consistent loss with limited mitigation. Stabilization after startup takes time during which fills are discarded. Judging when to keep product needs operator experience. Startup/restart stabilization product is routinely discarded, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile drug product discarded during deviation resolution
rows.imp_rat: Product discarded during deviation resolution is contingent waste, peripheral to the dispensing act; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Discard decisions are consistently conservative, so large losses are predictable but unavoidable. Deviation resolution consumes time during which product is discarded. Deciding discard scope needs quality expertise. Sterile product discarded during resolution is a major, poorly-mitigated cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of filling interruptions caused by accumulated segregated containers
rows.imp_rat: Interruptions from accumulated segregated containers is a minor flow disruption with low consequence; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Reject bins with capacity make reject-driven interruptions uncommon. Accumulated rejects occasionally force brief stops to clear. Clearing reject accumulation is a simple task. Minor downtime cost, no product loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether the filling line should continue while the team awaits direction
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about continuing while awaiting direction is a decision-delay ambiguity with modest consequence; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for required sterilized components at assembly
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for sterilized components at assembly is a supply-timing delay, peripheral to dispensing execution; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling and sterilizer capacity usually supply components on time, but bottlenecks still cause recurring waits. This need is directly about reducing waiting time for sterilized components. Waiting is a logistics matter, not an operator skill. Waiting wastes labor time, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among measurement-readiness acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among measurement-readiness criteria is a definitional ambiguity, peripheral to dispensing execution; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Harmonized specifications usually align criteria, though occasional disagreements between documents surface. Reconciling criteria disagreements delays verification. Resolving conflicting acceptance criteria requires quality judgement. Disagreements cause rework and delays, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time that measurement readiness remains unresolved after setup completion
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved measurement readiness after setup is a readiness delay with modest consequence; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Defined readiness steps usually resolve promptly, but pending checks sometimes extend the wait. This need is explicitly about shortening how long measurement readiness stays unresolved after setup. Resolving readiness depends on operator and QA responsiveness. Unresolved readiness idles the line, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of setup-readiness confirmation blocked by unacceptable required utilities
rows.imp_rat: Setup confirmation blocked by unacceptable utilities is a readiness delay, peripheral to dispensing; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Monitored utilities are usually ready, but occasional utility issues still block readiness confirmation. This need is about reducing confirmation time blocked by unacceptable utilities. Utility readiness is infrastructure, not operator skill. Blocked confirmation idles the line, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile product sent to waste through an incorrect startup flow path
rows.imp_rat: Product to waste via incorrect startup flow path is a setup-error waste, peripheral to the dispensing act; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Valve configuration checks and diverts reduce wrong-path losses, though setup errors still cause some. Wrong-path loss is not chiefly a time issue. Correct startup flow-path selection depends on operator knowledge. Product sent to waste through an incorrect startup path is a direct avoidable loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of startup interruptions requiring repeated product-path priming
rows.imp_rat: Repeated priming interruptions at startup are a rework nuisance with minor consequence, only loosely central to sustained dispensing; ranking caps low.
rows.sat_rat: Priming procedures usually work first time, but air locks still occasionally force repeats. Repeated priming interruptions directly extend startup duration. Avoiding re-priming depends on correct initial priming technique. Each re-prime wastes sterile product, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile product consumed during product-path priming at startup
rows.imp_rat: Sterile product consumed during priming is expected waste, peripheral to the core dispensing job and modest in consequence; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Priming volumes are predictable but unavoidably consume valuable sterile product each startup. Priming time is minor; the concern is product consumed. Minimizing priming volume depends somewhat on technique but mostly on system design. Sterile product consumed in priming is expensive and directly lost, an inherent startup cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of startup fills lost because containers are mispositioned beneath filling points
rows.imp_rat: Startup fills lost to container mispositioning is minor waste with low consequence, only loosely tied to sustained dispensing; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Container handling guides and sensors reduce mispositioning, though occasional startup fills are still lost. Mispositioned containers cause brief startup interruptions. Correct container positioning depends on setup accuracy. Lost startup fills from mispositioning waste product, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile product discarded because it remains trapped in the product path
rows.imp_rat: Product trapped in the product path is expected waste, peripheral to dispensing; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Low-holdup designs reduce trapped product, but appreciable dead-volume loss remains inherent. Trapped product is not chiefly a time concern. Minimizing hold-up depends more on system design than operator skill. Sterile product trapped in the path and discarded is a direct and often significant loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of filling downtime while restart authorization is pending
rows.imp_rat: Downtime while restart authorization pends is a delay with modest consequence, peripheral to dispensing; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Escalation procedures usually grant authorization reasonably fast, but approval waits still recur. This need is about reducing downtime while restart authorization is pending. Authorization timing depends on supervisory availability, not operator skill. Pending authorization idles the line, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile product discarded because of disruption recovery
rows.imp_rat: Product discarded during disruption recovery is contingent waste, peripheral to the dispensing job; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Conservative recovery rules often mandate discard, so recovery-related product loss remains common. Discard during recovery is not primarily a time issue. Reducing recovery discard depends on judgement about recoverable product. Product discarded due to disruption recovery is a direct, unpredictable loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of dispensed product lost from a container before verification
rows.imp_rat: Dispensed product lost from a container before verification is minor waste, peripheral to the dispensing act; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Careful handling limits pre-verification loss, which is minor and infrequent. Product lost before verification is not chiefly a time issue. Minimizing loss depends on careful handling of sample containers. Dispensed product lost from a container before verification is a direct small loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required quality-control results unavailable when batch documentation is reviewed
rows.imp_rat: QC results unavailable at documentation review is a review-timing gap with modest consequence; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: LIMS and scheduling make most results available on time, but pending tests still stall reviews. Missing QC results delay batch document review and release. Availability depends on QC lab scheduling rather than filling skill. Held batches awaiting QC tie up working capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of production changes implemented without required regulatory assessment and approval
rows.imp_rat: Production changes without regulatory approval is a compliance-process error, peripheral to the dispensing act; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Formal change-control systems reliably ensure regulatory assessment precedes production changes. Change-control assessment adds lead time before changes take effect. Assessing regulatory impact requires regulatory-affairs expertise. Implementing unassessed changes risks costly compliance actions, which control here prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between an applicable regulatory requirement taking effect and the filling operation conforming to it
rows.imp_rat: Lag between a regulation taking effect and conformance is a compliance-timing gap, peripheral to the dispensing job; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory monitoring processes catch requirements, but timely full conformance varies by change complexity. The need is about the lag between a rule taking effect and conforming, and regulatory implementation projects are inherently slow. Interpreting and implementing new requirements needs specialized regulatory and validation expertise. Slow conformance risks findings but the main loss is elapsed time to compliance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile product discarded after filling stoppages exceed allowable hold conditions
rows.imp_rat: Product discarded after hold-condition breaches is waste with moderate consequence but peripheral to the dispensing job; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Hold-time rules are consistently applied, forcing conservative but reliable discard. Hold-condition breaches trigger discard decisions that add closing time. Judging whether hold limits were exceeded needs informed assessment. Product discarded after hold-time breaches is direct sterile-product waste, the core loss here.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile product remaining in the machine product path after batch completion
rows.imp_rat: Product remaining in the path after completion is end-of-run waste, peripheral to the dispensing job; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Low-holdup single-use paths reduce residual product consistently, but some dead-leg loss remains. Line drainage is a defined end-of-batch task with modest time impact. Minimizing hold-up volume depends more on machine/tubing design than operator skill. Product left in the product path after completion is unrecoverable sterile-product waste, the central loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the compliance evaluation omits a newly applicable requirement
rows.imp_rat: Compliance evaluation omitting a new requirement is a regulatory-completeness gap, peripheral to dispensing; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Compliance reviews are structured but newly applicable requirements are sometimes missed. Requirement scanning is periodic, not urgent. Detecting newly applicable requirements relies on regulatory expertise. Regulatory intelligence and consultants add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contamination-control deviations awaiting documented compliance disposition
rows.imp_rat: Contamination deviations awaiting disposition is a review-queue delay, peripheral to dispensing execution; below ceiling.
