rows.sat_rat: Deviation management systems track status but backlogs persist commonly. The loss is a backlog of deviations awaiting compliance disposition. Disposition requires QA judgement, a bottleneck. Backlogs tie up product and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from filling completion until all required sterility-assurance evidence is available
rows.imp_rat: Time until sterility-assurance evidence is available is a post-fill availability delay with modest consequence; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Sterility test incubation reliably takes , a fixed unavoidable delay. The core loss is elapsed time from fill completion until all sterility evidence is available, dominated by incubation. Assembling evidence needs coordination but modest skill. Holding inventory during the wait carries cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from credible evidence of a changed microbial threat to incorporation into contamination-control readiness criteria
rows.imp_rat: Lag from microbial-threat evidence to updated criteria is a readiness-currency delay, peripheral to dispensing; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria updates lag emerging threats because the process is manual and infrequent. The core need is speed of incorporating emerging microbial threats into readiness criteria, typically slow. Interpreting new threat evidence needs microbiological expertise. Updating criteria and methods carries revalidation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unresolved ownership for contamination-control gaps requiring cross-functional action
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved ownership for contamination-control gaps is an organizational delay, peripheral to dispensing execution; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: CAPA systems assign ownership but cross-functional gaps often linger without clear accountability. The core loss is prolonged duration of unresolved ownership for cross-functional gaps. Coordination skill affects but does not define the need. Delayed resolution ties up resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of requirement changes that force qualification work to be repeated after execution begins
rows.imp_rat: Requirement changes forcing qualification rework is a project-management nuisance, minor consequence and peripheral to CFJ; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Change control exists but late requirement changes still frequently force re-execution, so this is poorly served. Requirement changes mid-qualification cause substantial rework and schedule loss with poor mitigation today. Managing change impact requires experienced change-control judgement that is often reactive. Repeated qualification after changes is a major avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of dose measurements invalidated by sample loss, evaporation, handling, or recording errors
rows.imp_rat: Dose measurements invalidated by sample handling errors is measurement rework with low consequence; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: In-line non-destructive checkweighing largely avoids sample-handling invalidation, though manual gravimetric checks still lose some. Handling and recording controls add minor procedural steps. Careful sampling to avoid loss/evaporation depends on technician skill and diligence. Invalidated measurements waste samples and require repeats.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of acceptance thresholds lacking a documented scientific or regulatory basis
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance thresholds lacking documented basis is a rationale-documentation gap, peripheral to dispensing; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many thresholds cite established limits, but some remain undocumented as to basis. Documenting scientific/regulatory basis for each threshold adds authoring time. Justifying thresholds requires solid scientific and regulatory grounding. Unjustified thresholds trigger inspection findings and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contradictions among acceptance criteria owned by quality, manufacturing, engineering, and regulatory stakeholders
rows.imp_rat: Contradictions among stakeholder acceptance criteria is a governance ambiguity, peripheral to dispensing; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-functional review reduces contradictions but ownership silos still produce them. Reconciling stakeholder-owned criteria adds coordination time. Resolving cross-functional contradictions requires facilitation and domain knowledge. Contradictions cause rework and delays across functions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of acceptance criteria based on superseded regulatory expectations
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance criteria on superseded regulations is a compliance-currency error, peripheral to physically dispensing; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory-intelligence practices help, but lag means some criteria reference outdated expectations. Checking criteria against current regulations requires periodic review time. Tracking superseded expectations (e.g., Annex needs current regulatory awareness. Superseded criteria cause non-compliance and remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt that inspection records represent the proposed aseptic filling configuration
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about inspection-record config representation is an evidentiary uncertainty, peripheral to dispensing; below ceiling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of recipe-access privileges exceeding each person's assigned responsibilities
rows.imp_rat: Excess recipe-access privileges is an access-control error, peripheral to dispensing execution; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Privilege creep is common and access frequently exceeds assigned duties despite periodic review. Periodic access reviews are time-consuming and often deferred. Mapping privileges to responsibilities needs someone who understands both roles and system permissions. Access reviews consume administrative effort with modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of gaps in the recorded history of recipe creation, review, release, revision, and retirement
rows.imp_rat: Gaps in recipe lifecycle history is a records-completeness error, peripheral to the dispensing act; below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe lifecycle records are usually complete in MES, but gaps appear at manual transitions and legacy migrations. Maintaining full lifecycle history is automatic in lifecycle-managed systems. Little skill is needed when the workflow tool records each stage. Lifecycle tracking is a built-in feature at low marginal cost.

rows.source_job: Register warranty and activate equipment entitlements
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applicable warranty, support, and service entitlements remaining inactive after machine acceptance
rows.imp_rat: Warranty/support entitlements remaining inactive after acceptance is an administrative activation error; peripheral to the core job, low relevance and modest severity if a claim later fails.
rows.sat_rat: OEM commissioning and acceptance handovers increasingly include registration steps, so many entitlements do get activated, but a meaningful share still slip through when handover checklists are incomplete. Registering entitlements at acceptance is a brief one-time task, so time is rarely the reason it lapses. Knowing which warranty, support, and service entitlements apply and must be activated requires procurement/handover knowledge that maintenance staff may not hold. Inactive entitlements mean paying out-of-pocket for coverage already purchased, a clear avoidable waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed service events omitted from the machine maintenance history
rows.imp_rat: Omitting service events from maintenance history is a record-keeping error that only indirectly touches operation; low relevance and low severity as it rarely halts production.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on manual paper logs, spreadsheets, or lightly-used CMMS entries that technicians routinely bypass, so omitted events are a chronic and unmeasured gap in maintenance history. Logging a completed service event takes only moments, but that speed is irrelevant when the entry is simply skipped under time pressure at the end of a repair. No special expertise is needed to record an event, yet consistent recording depends on the disciplined vigilance of busy technicians who often defer or forget it. The cost of capturing an event is negligible, so money is not the barrier to closing the gap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the supplier disputes warranty coverage because machine identification and service records are incomplete
rows.imp_rat: Supplier warranty disputes from incomplete identification records are a commercial/administrative error, not central to the machine operating; low relevance, moderate financial severity.
rows.sat_rat: Nameplate identification is usually captured and OEM service agreements provide a defined dispute path, but the completeness of the underlying service records is inconsistent, leaving recurring exposure to disputes. Assembling machine ID and service records for a warranty claim can drag on when documentation is scattered, delaying dispute resolution. Compiling defensible records for a supplier requires someone who knows what the warranty terms demand, a judgement not every maintenance team member reliably applies. A disputed or denied warranty claim shifts repair costs back onto the owner, making incomplete records a direct financial exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required maintenance, production, calibration, validation, and disposal records missing from the final archive
rows.imp_rat: Missing records from the final archive is an end-of-life documentation error, disconnected from the operating CFJ; low relevance, mild compliance severity.
rows.sat_rat: Regulated environments enforce documentation practices that capture most records, yet scattered manual and paper records still leave occasional gaps in the archive. Compiling records into the final archive is administrative work that can lag the physical maintenance. Assembling complete documentation demands diligence and knowledge of regulatory requirements more than technical skill. Record-keeping consumes labor time and missing records can trigger costly compliance findings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that missing machine history and validation records cause buyer due diligence to fail
rows.imp_rat: Missing history/validation records failing buyer due diligence is a resale-transaction concern, essentially unrelated to producing formed/filled/sealed output; low relevance and modest commercial severity.
rows.sat_rat: Record completeness varies widely, and missing history routinely causes buyer due diligence to stall or fail. Assembling machine history and validation records at disposal is slow when data is scattered. Compiling coherent validation history requires people who understand the records and where they live. Missing records reduce resale value and can force costly re-validation by the buyer.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of obsolete service access remaining enabled after machinery retirement or transfer
rows.imp_rat: Obsolete service access lingering after retirement is a security/administrative hygiene issue that is peripheral to the machine's core forming/filling/sealing job (low relevance); consequences are contained access-control risk, rarely catastrophic to production (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Obsolete service access commonly lingers for long periods because retirement rarely triggers access review. Disabling obsolete remote access is fast once someone remembers to do it. Tracking every service account and VPN tie to a retired machine needs cross-team IT/OT coordination that is often absent. Deactivating access costs almost nothing but the effort is routinely deprioritized.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of facility surfaces, utilities, or cleanroom barriers damaged during machine dismantling
rows.imp_rat: Damage to facility surfaces or cleanroom barriers during dismantling is a decommissioning error affecting the site, not the machine's forming function; low relevance though barrier damage carries some severity.
rows.sat_rat: Experienced rigging crews usually protect surfaces, but incidental damage during tight removals still occurs. Careful dismantling to protect facility surfaces and barriers takes longer than a rushed teardown. Avoiding damage to cleanroom barriers and utilities depends on skilled riggers who plan the removal path. Facility repairs from careless dismantling can be expensive, motivating care.

rows.source_job: Integrate filling machine with control system
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of control commands, interlocks, alarms, and machine states mapped incorrectly between integrated systems
rows.imp_rat: Mis-mapped commands/interlocks between integrated systems is an integration-config defect that ranks below the prior batch here (low relevance) and is caught in commissioning (low severity); capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Point-to-point testing exists but integration errors are notoriously common and hard to fully verify, so mismapped states routinely surface late despite best-effort checks. Mapping and verifying every control command, interlock, alarm, and state between integrated systems is a slow, iterative commissioning task prone to repeated debugging cycles. Correct integration mapping demands scarce controls/automation engineers who understand both the filler and the host systems, making it highly expertise-dependent. Mismapped interlocks cause costly troubleshooting, downtime, and potential equipment or product loss during commissioning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of user accounts with permissions exceeding or falling short of assigned filling machine responsibilities
rows.imp_rat: User accounts with mismatched permissions is an access-governance defect only loosely tied to the fill job (low relevance) and adjustable (low severity); held at the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access control on modern HMIs provides a workable framework, but permission assignments are often set loosely or copied without review, leaving recurring mismatches against actual responsibilities. Setting up user accounts and permissions is a modest one-time configuration task that adds little time to preparation. Mapping roles to responsibilities requires someone who understands both the org's duties and the machine's access model, a moderate administrative skill. Mis-scoped permissions rarely cost money directly during preparation, keeping cost impact low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between documented parameter, component, interface, utility, and position data and the commissioned configuration
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between documented and commissioned configuration is a documentation-accuracy defect, peripheral to the filling CFJ in this ranking (low relevance) and correctable (low severity); capped.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation is frequently incomplete or out of sync with the commissioned machine, and manual reconciliation leaves many discrepancies uncaught, making this a persistent weak point. Reconciling as-built parameter, component, interface, utility, and position data against documentation is a tedious task that lengthens commissioning. Accurate configuration verification requires knowledgeable engineers who can interpret specifications and detect discrepancies, a demanding attention-heavy task. Undocumented configuration drift creates costly downstream troubleshooting and validation rework, though not catastrophic material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that filling machinery registration is submitted after the manufacturer eligibility deadline
rows.imp_rat: Late registration past a manufacturer eligibility deadline is an administrative delay distant from the fill job (low relevance), though a missed window carries modest cost (low severity); capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Deadlines are occasionally missed when tracking is informal, but the low-frequency, well-bounded nature of this task means it is mostly handled. The error is purely a missed deadline, and calendar reminders plus warranty-tracking make timely registration straightforward to hit. Submitting a registration form requires minimal skill, just knowing the deadline and process. Registration is typically free or trivial in cost, so money is not the concern.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of corrosion, dust deposition, moisture damage, or component degradation during storage
rows.imp_rat: Corrosion or dust during storage is a preservation defect marginal to executing fills (low relevance) and typically remediable before commissioning (low severity); held at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Conventional protective storage practices (wrapping, desiccant, indoor storage) are mature and, when applied, reliably limit corrosion and dust, though uncontrolled environments still cause damage. Preparing equipment for storage is a one-time effort and delay is not the issue; the damage accrues over storage duration. Basic storage protection (covers, desiccants, controlled environment) is well-understood and needs only moderate skill. Climate-controlled space and protective materials cost money, but the primary loss is component degradation not overspend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that filling machine preventive service is scheduled after its due date
rows.imp_rat: Preventive service scheduled late is a maintenance-timing delay only indirectly bearing on getting fills done (low relevance) and usually absorbed without incident (low severity); kept at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated due-date triggers make on-time scheduling fairly consistent, though resource conflicts and production priorities still push some PMs past due. The error itself is a schedule slippage — PM performed after the due date — and CMMS reminders plus planned-maintenance calendars are mature and widely deployed to prevent it. Scheduling PM on time is a well-understood planning task supported by standard software, needing little specialized skill. Scheduling tools are inexpensive and broadly available, so cost rarely blocks timely PM.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of corrosion, microbial growth, seal degradation, or moisture damage developing during extended inactivity
rows.imp_rat: Corrosion or microbial growth during idle periods touches machine condition but is peripheral to the running fill job in this ranking (low relevance) and detectable before use (low severity); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard shutdown/lay-up procedures exist but are inconsistently applied to filling machines during unplanned or extended inactivity, so seal and microbial damage still commonly develops. Preservation prep during extended shutdown takes time, but the failure is degradation that develops slowly over the idle period rather than a delay. Effective lay-up of hygienic filling equipment (draining, sanitizing, desiccants, rotation) requires real process knowledge that many sites lack for long idle periods. Corrosion, microbial growth and seal degradation impose material and rework costs, but the core loss is the equipment condition deteriorating.

rows.stk: Enterprise | Controls Engineer or Administrator | One-time
rows.source_job: Export stored recipes and production data
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of controller-resident recipes, settings, audit records, and production data omitted from the export
rows.imp_rat: Omitting controller-resident recipes and audit data from export is peripheral to the actual filling job (low relevance) and consequences are recoverable data-transfer issues (low severity); ranking caps it below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Proprietary export utilities vary and often cover only part of the controller memory, leaving audit records or production data silently omitted from the exported set. Exports run quickly once initiated, but they are done reactively and irregularly so current controller state is often not captured in time. Extracting all controller-resident recipes, audit records and production data requires vendor-specific knowledge of export tools, and gaps result when that skill is absent. Export tooling is typically bundled with the controller, so money is not the dominant concern.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required maintenance, validation, configuration, production, and compliance records missing from the retired-machine archive
rows.imp_rat: Missing records in a retired-machine archive is an end-of-life administrative gap with negligible impact on active filling. Scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Records commonly go missing because documentation was inconsistent over decades of operation, and no standard checklist ensures a complete retirement archive. Assembling the retired-machine archive is time-consuming because records are scattered across paper files, CMMS, and legacy validation systems. Knowing which maintenance, validation and compliance records are required and where they live demands institutional memory that erodes over a machine's long service life. Archiving itself is inexpensive, so the loss is missing information rather than direct spend.

rows.stk: Enterprise | Operations or Compliance Manager | One-time
rows.source_job: Notify stakeholders of filling machine retirement
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required internal stakeholders, service providers, authorities, and registries omitted from filling machine retirement notifications
rows.imp_rat: Omitting stakeholders from retirement notifications is a decommissioning communication gap with no effect on the filling CFJ. Scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Without a standardized stakeholder register some notifiable parties — especially pressure-vessel or refrigerant registries — are routinely omitted, though core internal notifications usually go out. Issuing retirement notifications is fast once the recipient list is known, but compiling that list across departments and registries can drag. Identifying every required authority, registry and service provider relies on regulatory awareness that only experienced compliance staff reliably possess. Sending notifications costs little, so the issue is completeness of coverage rather than expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supplier reliability claims lacking independent evidence relevant to sterile fill-finish applications
rows.imp_rat: Supplier reliability claims lacking independent evidence is a vetting-quality error remote from executing fills (low relevance), with modest consequences on selection risk (low severity); capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier reliability claims are frequently taken at face value because independent, application-relevant evidence is hard to obtain, leaving a persistent trust gap. Obtaining independent evidence for reliability claims requires slow reference visits and third-party data gathering. Evaluating whether independent evidence is relevant to sterile fill-finish demands experienced technical assessors. Independent verification carries audit and travel cost, so teams often accept cheaper vendor self-claims.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in warranty coverage, claim triggers, response obligations, and remedies for the filling machinery
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in warranty coverage and remedies is a contractual-clarity error remote from doing the fill (low relevance), though it affects recourse cost (modest severity); held at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Standard warranty clauses and legal review reduce ambiguity substantially, though claim triggers and response obligations still leave interpretive gaps in some contracts. Negotiating and clarifying warranty terms fits within normal contracting timelines. Drafting unambiguous warranty triggers and remedies needs contractual and legal skill that is generally available. Ambiguous warranty terms are cheap to leave vague but expensive when claim disputes arise.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required financial, technical, compliance, and capacity evidence missing from the filling machinery approval package
rows.imp_rat: Missing evidence in the approval package is an administrative procurement error, peripheral to the filling CFJ (low relevance) though a gap could delay approval (modest severity); capped by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Approval-package templates help, but multi-disciplinary evidence is often incomplete when submitted, forcing rework loops. Compiling financial, technical, compliance, and capacity evidence for the approval package is a lengthy collection effort. Knowing what evidence approvers require across disciplines needs broad cross-functional experience. Missing evidence is cheap to gather but delays approval and ties up capital decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable permits, declarations, certifications, and customs documents overlooked for the filling machinery
rows.imp_rat: Overlooked permits and customs documents is a compliance-completeness defect distant from the fill CFJ (low relevance), though it could stall import (modest severity); capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Customs brokers and standard checklists handle common documentation well, but unusual certifications for specialized machinery are occasionally overlooked. Identifying permits, customs, and certification documents is a defined step, though international shipments can add delay. Knowing applicable declarations and customs documents needs trade-compliance expertise that is often outsourced to brokers. Overlooked documents cause demurrage, fines, and clearance delays that are costly to remedy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the transport route, receiving area, rigging path, or installation entrance cannot accommodate the machinery
rows.imp_rat: A transport/rigging path that cannot fit the machine is a one-time install logistics problem, marginal to ongoing filling (low relevance) and resolvable (low severity); held below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Documented pre-installation site surveys and drawing checks are standard and largely dependable, though occasional undocumented obstructions or dimension errors still cause surprises on arrival. Route and access surveys are done well ahead of delivery, so they seldom add time when performed properly. Rigging and site-access planning is a mature discipline handled by experienced riggers and installers who reliably measure clearances and load paths. A missed clearance means expensive re-rigging, demolition, or crane rework, but pre-delivery site surveys keep that cost rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transport damage remains undetected after the filling machine is uncrated and inspected
rows.imp_rat: Undetected transport damage is a pre-operation logistics defect only loosely tied to executing fills (low relevance) with typically warranty-covered consequences (low severity); held under the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established checklists, photo documentation, and shock/tilt indicators catch obvious damage well, yet concealed internal or calibration damage still slips through with real recurring frequency. Uncrating and inspection is a scheduled receiving task that rarely bottlenecks preparation, so time pressure is limited. Spotting concealed transport damage on complex filling machinery requires an experienced inspector who knows what subtle indicators to look for, and untrained receiving staff routinely miss it. Undetected damage surfacing later drives costly rework, warranty disputes, and delay, but standard inspection practice avoids most of that spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of product released from completed supply-line connections during filling machine operation
rows.imp_rat: Product released from completed supply-line connections during operation touches contamination and loss but ranks below the prior batch here (low relevance) with contained consequences (low severity); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard pressure tests, torque specs, and visual leak checks reliably confirm connections, though intermittent seal failures during operation still occur occasionally. Leak-checking supply-line connections is a routine, quick part of setup that rarely delays preparation. Making and verifying tight sanitary or pressurized connections is a well-understood mechanical skill most technicians possess. Product released from leaking connections wastes material and cleanup effort, but pressure/leak testing catches most before production spend accumulates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of machine damage during freight transit
rows.imp_rat: Freight transit damage is a logistics-phase error outside normal operation; it can cause costly repairs (some severity) but is peripheral to the running CFJ, keeping relevance low.
rows.sat_rat: Standard shock-indicator, custom crating, and Incoterms practice make significant transit damage an uncommon, well-mitigated event. Specialized machinery freight is well-established and rarely adds unexpected delay beyond normal transit windows. Crating, rigging, and shock-monitoring of heavy precision equipment are handled by experienced specialist logistics providers, reducing buyer skill burden. Damage-in-transit insurance and proven packaging keep the financial exposure of freight damage largely contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the signed purchase agreement and final negotiated terms
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between signed agreement and negotiated terms are a purely contractual/administrative error with no bearing on the forming/filling/sealing job; very low relevance, moderate contractual severity.
rows.sat_rat: Established contract-review and sign-off workflows make discrepancies between signed and negotiated terms rare and reliably caught. Final contract reconciliation is a defined legal step that rarely delays acquisition materially. Procurement and legal teams routinely redline and reconcile terms, a mature competency requiring standard rather than scarce skill. Discrepancies between agreement and negotiated terms are caught before signature, keeping financial leakage minimal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of observed machine wear or damage omitted from the final condition record
rows.imp_rat: Omitting observed wear/damage from the final condition record is an end-of-life documentation error, disconnected from active operation; low relevance and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Final condition records are often informal, so observed wear and damage are commonly under-recorded or omitted. Documenting final wear and damage adds a short inspection step at disposal. Accurately assessing and recording machine condition requires an experienced inspector's judgement. Condition recording is low cost, mostly inspector time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required configurations, programs, logs, and maintenance records omitted from the export
rows.imp_rat: Omitting records from an export package is a data-transfer administrative error, peripheral to running the machine; low relevance and low severity given records can often be re-exported.
rows.sat_rat: Without a standard export manifest, required records are frequently omitted from the handover. Exporting all configs, programs, logs, and maintenance records is laborious across disparate systems. Knowing what to export and how to extract it from legacy controllers requires specialist familiarity. Export effort is mostly labor cost, low unless data is deeply siloed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of associated financial, equipment, and insurance records continuing to list the disposed machine
rows.imp_rat: Financial/insurance records still listing a disposed machine is a records-cleanup error with no operational bearing; very low relevance and mild accounting/compliance severity.
rows.sat_rat: Record updates are inconsistently triggered by disposal, so disposed machines often remain listed for extended periods. Updating financial, asset, and insurance registers lags well behind physical disposal. Reconciling records across finance, EAM, and insurance systems needs administrative coordination more than technical skill. Stale records mean paying insurance and taxes on disposed assets, a direct ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of serviceable modules damaged while being removed from the retired machine
rows.imp_rat: Damaging serviceable modules during removal from a retired machine is a salvage-value/waste error at end of life, peripheral to the CFJ; low relevance with modest financial severity.
rows.sat_rat: Salvage practice exists where value is recognized, but modules are still routinely damaged in disassembly. Removing serviceable modules intact is slower than scrapping the machine outright. Preserving reusable modules demands technicians who know the machine and handle components carefully. Damaged modules lose resale or reuse value, a direct financial loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes while awaiting clarification of a questionable fill
rows.plain: They want fewer sudden worries while waiting for an explanation of a questionable product fill. This helps them know whether the product was filled correctly and can be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety spikes awaiting fill clarification are emotional, peripheral to dispensing; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when filling-line conditions present conflicting signals
rows.plain: Stay calm when conditions on the production line give mixed signals. This helps the worker keep control and respond appropriately rather than guessing.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure amid conflicting line signals is emotional, tangential to the physical fill; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety spikes while awaiting clarification of an abnormal line indication
rows.plain: They want fewer sudden worries while waiting for an explanation of an unusual machine or process reading. This helps them keep the line running steadily and spot any real problem.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety spikes awaiting line-indication clarification are emotional, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when an unexpected intervention demands an immediate sterility judgment
rows.plain: Stay calm if an unexpected action occurs and you must quickly decide whether the work area is still free from harmful germs. This helps protect the work from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure during a sterility judgment is a personal-emotional error only tangentially tied to actually dispensing sterile product; capped low by ranking.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch contamination is attributed to the operator before the source is established
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that an entire production batch is blamed on the operator for contamination before the real source is known. This helps ensure the cause is investigated fairly before anyone is held responsible.
rows.imp_rat: Premature attribution of contamination to operator is a blame/fairness issue, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost when the operator's aseptic handling is challenged by quality reviewers
rows.plain: They want quality reviewers to keep trusting the worker when they question how the product was handled in a way that prevents germs from getting in. This matters because careful handling protects the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when handling is challenged is a reputational matter, distant from dispensing; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical aseptic interventions assigned to others because the operator's handling discipline is doubted
rows.plain: Reduce the number of important germ-free handling tasks given to someone else because people doubt the operator’s careful technique. This helps the operator retain responsibility when they are capable of doing the work safely.
rows.imp_rat: Interventions reassigned due to doubt is a role/trust matter, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch fill discrepancies are attributed to the filler before the cause is established
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that differences in how much product was filled are blamed on the filling-machine operator before the real cause is known. This helps ensure the problem is investigated fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Premature attribution of fill discrepancies to the filler is a blame ambiguity, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost when the filler's batch records are questioned by quality leadership
rows.plain: They want quality leaders to keep trusting the person filling the product when they question the work records for a batch. This matters because accurate records show that the product was made correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when batch records questioned is reputational, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the filler is bypassed for critical assignments because production leadership doubts their precision
rows.plain: The person filling the containers wants to be trusted with important assignments. They do not want leaders to pass them over because they doubt the person's accuracy.
rows.imp_rat: Filler bypassed due to doubted precision is a trust/role matter, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for required format components during changeover
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for the parts needed for the new product or setup during the switch. This helps the change happen faster and reduces production delays.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for format components during changeover is a logistics delay with minor consequence, remote from the dispensing CFJ; low on both.
rows.sat_rat: Kitting and staging reduce waits, but missing or misplaced format parts still cause recurring delays. This need is explicitly about reducing waiting for format components during changeover. Availability is a logistics issue, not skill. Waiting idles labor and line, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of aseptic-zone entry delay caused by unavailable correctly sized sterile gowning components
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to enter the sterile work area because the available sterile clothing is the wrong size. This helps the worker begin the task sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Gowning-component sizing delay affects zone entry logistics but is peripheral to the machine's dispensing job; consequence is a modest delay. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Stocked gown inventories by size usually prevent delays, but size shortages still occasionally hold up entry. This need is explicitly about reducing entry delay from unavailable correctly sized gowning components. Availability is a supply matter, not operator skill. Waiting for sized gowns idles labor, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile product consumed beyond startup sampling requirements
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of sterile product used beyond what is needed to take samples when starting the process. This keeps startup waste as low as possible.
rows.imp_rat: Product consumed beyond startup sampling is minor waste, remote from the dispensing CFJ; low on both.
rows.sat_rat: Defined sampling plans control consumption, but conservative practices still consume extra valuable product. Extra sampling consumption is not primarily a time issue. Limiting sample consumption depends modestly on procedure adherence. Sterile product consumed beyond sampling requirements is a direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required recovery actions lacking traceable batch documentation
rows.plain: Make sure every action taken to recover from a problem is linked to clear records about the affected batch. This makes it possible to understand exactly what was done and why.
rows.imp_rat: Missing traceable batch documentation for recovery actions is a compliance/administrative gap, peripheral to the core dispensing act; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation and batch records capture most recovery actions, but rushed recoveries still leave documentation gaps. Documenting recovery actions adds concurrent effort. Recording recovery actions traceably depends on documentation discipline under pressure. Missing recovery records force investigations, a cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among restart-readiness assessments
rows.plain: Make sure everyone agrees on whether the equipment and process are ready to be started again. This helps prevent unsafe or faulty operation after a stoppage.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among restart-readiness assessments is a coordination ambiguity distant from the core fill; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Restart checklists align assessments in most cases, though occasional conflicts among assessors arise. Reconciling restart assessments adds brief delay. Resolving conflicting restart-readiness views requires judgement. Conflicts cause delays and possible wrong restarts, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty about the startup go-or-hold judgment while the team awaits direction
rows.plain: Decide quickly whether the clean production process can start or must be held. A prompt, well-supported decision prevents the team from waiting and helps avoid starting under unsafe conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty on go-or-hold judgment is a confidence/timing ambiguity; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt about which startup acceptance indication to trust when signals conflict
rows.plain: When startup signals disagree, there should be a clear way to decide whether the equipment is ready to use. This helps prevent work from beginning under unsafe or unsuitable conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt over which startup indication to trust is a decision ambiguity distant from the fill; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when a startup discrepancy is challenged before the team
rows.plain: Stay calm when someone points out an unexpected difference at startup in front of the team. This helps the team understand the issue and respond safely.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when startup discrepancy challenged is emotional, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty about making the ready-line handover call while stakeholders wait
rows.plain: Make the decision to hand the ready production line to the next team quickly and clearly. This prevents people from waiting while no one is sure whether the line is ready.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty on handover call is a confidence/timing ambiguity; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when changeover departs from the expected sequence
rows.plain: Stay calm when the change from one job or product to the next does not follow the expected order. This helps keep the handoff on schedule and prevents mistakes.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure during unexpected changeover is emotional, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt about which readiness signal to trust when stakeholders provide conflicting information
rows.plain: When people give conflicting updates, there should be less uncertainty about which sign shows that the work is truly ready. This helps the handover happen on time.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt over which readiness signal to trust amid conflict is a coordination ambiguity; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty after ambiguous movement near the critical zone
rows.plain: Quickly understand what happened after an unclear movement near the most sensitive clean area. This helps determine whether germs or other contamination could have been introduced.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty after ambiguous movement near the critical zone is a perception matter, ranked low here; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of anxiety while a possible contamination concern remains unresolved
rows.plain: Make people less worried while they investigate whether unwanted contamination has occurred. This helps them respond carefully while the answer is still unknown.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety during unresolved contamination concern is emotional, distant from the fill; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when the integrity of an aseptic intervention is questioned
rows.plain: Stay calm when people question whether an action taken to protect the product from germs was done properly. This helps determine whether the product remained protected.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when intervention integrity questioned is emotional, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an aseptic deviation is publicly attributed to the operator's gowning or critical-zone conduct
rows.plain: The worker wants to avoid being publicly blamed if a sterile-handling problem is linked to their protective clothing or behavior in the most sensitive work area. This protects their reputation and accountability.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation attributed to operator conduct is a blame matter, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost with quality observers after gowning or aseptic conduct is questioned
rows.plain: Avoid losing trust with quality reviewers after people question whether workers dressed properly or followed clean-work practices. This matters because these behaviors help keep the product free from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost after gowning questioned is reputational, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which supervisors or peers second-guess the operator's aseptic setup decisions
rows.plain: Supervisors and coworkers should trust the worker’s decisions when preparing a clean work area instead of repeatedly questioning them. This helps prevent contamination and keeps the setup consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Second-guessing of aseptic setup decisions is a peripheral social/organizational concern with minimal direct bearing on the CFJ of dispensing sterile product; consequences are largely reputational and absorbed. Ranking caps it at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that leadership bypasses the operator for critical aseptic interventions because of doubts about aseptic discipline
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that leaders ignore the worker during critical steps to keep products clean and safe because they do not trust the worker's cleanliness practices. The worker needs to be heard when safety is at risk.
rows.imp_rat: Operator bypassed for interventions due to doubt is a trust/role matter, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a startup deviation is publicly attributed to the setup professional's preparation
rows.plain: A problem when the work starts should not be publicly blamed on the person who prepared the setup. This protects their reputation for getting the setup right the first time.
rows.imp_rat: Startup deviation attributed to setup professional is a blame matter, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost with quality leadership when setup-readiness judgment is challenged
rows.plain: Avoid losing trust with quality leaders when they question whether a setup was truly ready to begin work. This matters because problems at the start can lead to mistakes, delays, or unsafe results.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when setup judgment challenged is reputational, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which production leadership second-guesses the setup professional's release decisions
rows.plain: Production leaders should question the setup expert’s decisions to declare a new setup ready less often. This helps the team start production only when the changeover has been properly completed.
rows.imp_rat: Leadership second-guessing release decisions is a trust matter, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which leadership bypasses the setup professional in high-stakes changeover decisions
rows.plain: Senior leaders should go around the setup expert less often when making important decisions about changing from one production setup to another. This helps prevent rushed or poorly planned changes.
rows.imp_rat: Setup professional bypassed in changeover decisions is a trust/role matter, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which clean initial startups are credited to others during production and quality reviews
rows.plain: When production starts cleanly, the person who prepared the startup should receive proper credit rather than having the success attributed to someone else. This recognizes the work that made the smooth start possible.
rows.imp_rat: Clean startups credited to others is a recognition/fairness matter, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a completed batch waits for required functions to finish release-readiness review
rows.plain: A completed production lot should spend less time waiting for the necessary teams to finish checking whether it is ready for release. This helps approved products move forward sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Batch waiting on release-readiness review is a throughput/delay matter distant from the physical fill; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Review workflows are established but hand-offs between functions make total wait time variable and often extended. This need is directly about how long a completed batch waits for release-readiness review, and multi-function serial reviews make this wait long. The wait depends on availability of qualified reviewers across functions. Batches waiting in queue tie up capital and warehouse space.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to produce requested sterility-assurance evidence
rows.plain: Make it faster to produce proof that the product and its handling were kept free from harmful germs. This helps show that the work follows health and safety rules.
rows.imp_rat: Time to produce sterility-assurance evidence is a documentation-delay issue; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence exists but retrieval speed varies with electronic versus paper systems. The core need is reducing time to produce requested sterility-assurance evidence, often slow with hybrid records. Retrieval needs familiarity with document systems. Retrieval labor is a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required contamination-control readiness evidence that is missing, inconsistent, or outdated at governance review
rows.plain: Keep all required proof that contamination is being controlled complete, consistent, and up to date for oversight reviews. This shows that the controls are still ready and working as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Missing contamination-control readiness evidence at governance review is a documentation gap; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Readiness evidence at governance review is frequently incomplete, inconsistent, or outdated. Governance review is periodic; outdated evidence surfaces then. Judging readiness-evidence currency needs experienced reviewers. Maintaining current evidence is labor-intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of degraded contamination control during utility, facility, or staffing disruptions affecting aseptic filling
rows.plain: Spend less time with weakened protection against contamination when utilities, the building, or staffing are disrupted during sterile filling. This helps protect the medicine from being spoiled or made unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: Degraded contamination control during disruptions is closer to CFJ but ranked below prior batch; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Backup systems and contingency SOPs exist but degraded-control duration during disruptions remains variable. The core loss is prolonged degraded control during utility, facility, or staffing disruptions. Managing disruptions needs experienced response teams. Backup utilities and redundancy carry high capital cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from approved aseptic-condition limits during validation runs
rows.plain: Make sure test production runs stay within the approved limits for handling medicines without introducing germs. This helps show that the process can protect the medicine from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from aseptic-condition limits during validation is closer to the CFJ but ranked below prior batch; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Validation protocols define tight limits and deviations from them are relatively uncommon under controlled conditions. Deviations during validation extend validation timelines. Controlling validation conditions requires skilled execution. Validation deviations cause costly repeat runs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts among acceptance criteria used to judge aseptic validation results
rows.plain: Make sure the rules used to decide whether germ-free testing results are acceptable do not conflict. This helps everyone reach the same conclusion about whether the results pass.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting acceptance criteria in validation is an administrative ambiguity, not central to executing an aseptic fill; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Protocol acceptance criteria are pre-defined, making conflicts relatively uncommon but not absent. Resolving criteria conflicts delays validation decisions. Reconciling acceptance criteria needs validation expertise. Rework of protocols costs labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that the validation decision remains blocked by unresolved deviations
rows.plain: Make it quicker to resolve problems that are preventing the decision about whether clean production conditions were maintained. This helps the validation decision move forward.
rows.imp_rat: Validation blocked by deviations is a schedule/delay issue, not central to dispensing; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation-driven blocks are tracked but resolution timelines vary widely. The core loss is the duration validation decisions stay blocked by unresolved deviations. Deviation resolution needs QA judgement. Blocked validation delays product launch, a cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty while release participants await sign-off
rows.plain: Quickly know what is holding up approval while the people involved wait to sign off. This helps the group make the release decision without unnecessary delay.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty while awaiting sign-off is a release-latency error, not central to the dispensing mechanics; minor consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of losing composure when release participants challenge sterility evidence
rows.plain: They want to stay calm when people deciding whether a product can be released question the evidence that it is free from harmful germs. This helps them explain the evidence clearly and support a sound decision.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when release evidence challenged is emotional, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting clarification of an unexplained sterility-evidence gap
rows.plain: They want a quick explanation for why proof that the medicine is germ-free appears to be missing. This helps the supervisor decide whether the medicine can be released.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting clarification of a sterility-evidence gap is an emotional/informational error rather than a direct dispensing failure; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty after an auditor challenges contamination-control evidence
rows.plain: The team needs to quickly know how to respond after an auditor questions the evidence that contamination was controlled. This helps them give a clear, supported answer.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of visible uncertainty after an auditor challenge is an administrative/emotional error not central to physically dispensing sterile volumes; consequences are minor. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of losing composure when records and explanations conflict under inspection scrutiny
rows.plain: They want to stay calm when inspectors find that the written records do not match the explanations. This helps them answer clearly and show what really happened.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when records conflict under inspection is emotional, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting requested evidence during an inspection
rows.plain: They want to spend less time worrying while waiting for records requested during an inspection. This matters because having the records quickly helps them respond calmly and completely.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting requested inspection evidence is an emotional/documentation error, not central to dispensing sterile volumes; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of self-doubt when an auditor questions contamination-control conclusions
rows.plain: Make sure the reasoning behind the contamination-prevention conclusions is strong enough to explain confidently during an inspection. This helps the person stay composed when questioned.
rows.imp_rat: Self-doubt when auditor questions conclusions is emotional, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of losing composure when unexpected contamination concerns arise during active filling
rows.plain: They want to stay calm when unexpected concerns about harmful contamination arise while products are being filled. This helps the team respond carefully and protect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure during contamination concerns while filling is emotional, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting clarification of ambiguous aseptic-condition evidence during active filling
rows.plain: Reduce the worry caused by unclear proof that sterile conditions were maintained during active filling. Clarifying the evidence helps show whether the product remained protected.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting clarification of ambiguous aseptic-condition evidence is an emotional/informational error, not central to dispensing execution; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about the go/no-go judgment while the filling team awaits direction
rows.plain: The supervisor should quickly know whether the filling work should proceed or stop while the team waits for direction. This prevents the team from moving ahead without a clear decision.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about go/no-go while awaiting direction is a decision-latency error, not central to the machine dispensing job; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that regulators or auditors attribute an inspection finding to weak governance of the aseptic filling unit
rows.plain: The overseer wants to avoid being blamed if inspectors find that the sterile filling unit was not managed properly. Good oversight and records help show that the unit was controlled responsibly.
rows.imp_rat: Auditors attributing findings to weak governance is a reputational framing, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost when the overseer's account conflicts with aseptic filling evidence
rows.plain: They want people to keep trusting the supervisor when the supervisor’s account does not match the evidence from filling the product. This matters because the explanation should agree with what actually happened.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when overseer's account conflicts with evidence is reputational, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which regulators or auditors bypass the overseer on contamination-control judgments
rows.plain: Inspectors and auditors should be less likely to go around the person when making decisions about preventing contamination. This helps keep responsibility clear and shows that the decisions have a trusted owner.
rows.imp_rat: Auditors bypassing overseer is a role/trust matter, distant from dispensing; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of inspection commitments visibly missed before regulators or auditors
rows.plain: Reduce the number of inspection promises that are visibly missed before government reviewers or independent auditors. This helps protect trust in the organization’s work.
rows.imp_rat: Missed inspection commitments before auditors is a reputational/delay matter, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a contaminated-product release is attributed to weak sterility-assurance governance
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a product released with harmful germs is blamed on weak oversight of the steps meant to keep it germ-free. The controls and decisions protecting product cleanliness should be strong and clear.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminated release attributed to weak governance is a reputational/compliance framing, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost with Quality Assurance when aseptic drift is linked to weak oversight
rows.plain: Keep quality staff’s trust when a gradual loss of sterile conditions is linked to weak supervision. Strong oversight should help prevent contamination and catch problems early.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when drift linked to weak oversight is reputational, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the overseer is bypassed in aseptic disposition decisions
rows.plain: The person responsible for protecting cleanliness should be included more often when deciding what to do with a product made under clean conditions. This matters because their input helps prevent unsafe products from being accepted or unnecessarily rejected.
rows.imp_rat: Overseer bypassed in disposition decisions is a role/trust matter, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stakeholders assign blame for failing to escalate sterility-assurance warnings
rows.plain: The overseer wants to avoid being blamed if warnings about a possible loss of sterility are not passed on in time. Important warnings need to reach the right people so they can act before a problem grows.
rows.imp_rat: Blame for failing to escalate warnings is a reputational/process matter, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of public second-guessing of sterility-assurance judgments by site leadership
rows.plain: They want site leaders to stop publicly questioning decisions about how to make sure products stay free from harmful germs. This helps maintain confidence in the safety of the products and the production process.
rows.imp_rat: Public second-guessing of sterility judgments is a reputational matter, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supplier equipment evidence rejected for insufficient relevance or rigor
rows.plain: Make sure evidence from equipment suppliers is useful, detailed, and reliable enough to support the review. This prevents time being wasted on evidence that cannot answer the questions.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected supplier evidence is a procurement-waste issue peripheral to dispensing; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier evidence quality is inconsistent and frequently rejected, so leveraging it remains poorly served. Re-generating rejected supplier evidence delays qualification. Assessing supplier evidence relevance and rigor requires vendor-audit expertise. Rejected supplier evidence forces duplicate testing, a substantial wasted cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of qualification work devoted to capabilities outside the intended sterile fill-finish use
rows.plain: Limit testing and documentation to what is needed for filling medicines into sterile containers. This avoids spending time proving the equipment can do unrelated things.
rows.imp_rat: Qualification work outside intended use is wasted effort but does not affect the fill job itself; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Risk-based qualification (ASTM E2500) reduces scope creep, though over-qualification still occurs where risk assessment is weak. Excess qualification of out-of-scope capabilities wastes execution time. Scoping to intended use requires disciplined risk-based judgement that varies by team. Effort spent on out-of-scope capabilities is direct wasted resource that risk-based approaches partly curb.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification activities duplicating equipment evidence already accepted as relevant and rigorous
rows.plain: Avoid repeating equipment checks that have already been properly completed and accepted. This saves time and money without losing confidence in the equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Duplicated qualification activities are rework waste, peripheral to dispensing; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Despite leveraging concepts, conservative habits drive frequent duplication, keeping this poorly served. Duplicated testing that repeats accepted supplier evidence wastes qualification time. Deciding what can be leveraged versus repeated requires confident risk-based judgement often lacking. Duplication of already-accepted evidence is direct avoidable spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to resolve missing product, container-closure, and dispensing-condition inputs before criteria approval
rows.plain: Spend less time gathering missing information about the product, its packaging, and how it will be dispensed before approving the standards for judging options.
rows.imp_rat: Time to resolve missing inputs before criteria approval is a pre-approval administrative delay, remote from dispensing; low on both.
rows.sat_rat: Stage-gate processes surface missing inputs but resolution is often slow and iterative. Resolving missing product/closure/condition inputs before approval is a recurring bottleneck that delays sign-off. Chasing and validating missing inputs requires knowledgeable coordinators. Delays extend project timelines and carrying costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time inspection evidence remains unavailable after a request
rows.plain: Make inspection records available more quickly after someone asks for them. This helps show that the work was properly documented.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection evidence unavailable after request is a documentation-delay issue; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: EDMS/MES make most evidence readily available, though scattered legacy records still slow some requests. Electronic document and record systems retrieve inspection evidence quickly on request. Retrieval needs only basic system familiarity. Fast retrieval avoids inspection delays and associated costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of visible uncertainty when linking an aseptic requirement to evidence supporting the machine recommendation
rows.plain: Avoid appearing unsure when showing how a sterile-processing need is supported by evidence for the recommended machine. This helps make the recommendation convincing and defensible.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty linking requirement to evidence is a presentation/confidence issue, peripheral to dispensing; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when an inspector probes an undocumented assumption behind the evaluation criteria
rows.plain: The buyer wants to avoid being caught off guard if an inspector asks about an unstated assumption used to decide what would count as acceptable. This matters because the decision rules should be fully explained and supported.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when inspector probes assumptions is emotional/reputational, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when inspection records support conflicting conclusions about the proposed machine's aseptic suitability
rows.plain: Stay calm when inspection records support different views about whether the proposed machine can make the product safely without unwanted contamination. This helps the person give a recommendation that can withstand inspection.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure over conflicting inspection records is emotional, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty when tracing an acceptance criterion to its applicable aseptic or regulatory basis during sign-off
rows.plain: Make it quick and easy to show how each pass-or-fail requirement is supported by the relevant safety or regulatory rule during final approval. This helps reviewers see that the approval is based on the right standards.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty tracing acceptance criteria at sign-off is an administrative ambiguity; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt during sign-off that an applicable aseptic or regulatory requirement has been overlooked
rows.plain: The person influencing the purchase wants to stop doubting whether any required germ-prevention or safety rule was missed during approval. They need confidence that the decision follows all applicable requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt at sign-off about overlooked requirements is an administrative uncertainty; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of disruptions to calm when stakeholders disagree about applicable aseptic or regulatory requirements
rows.plain: Avoid repeatedly disturbing a calm working relationship when people disagree about cleanliness or legal requirements. Clear agreement on the rules helps everyone stay focused and cooperative.
rows.imp_rat: Disruptions to calm during requirement disagreements is emotional, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting regulatory clarification needed to support an acceptance criterion before sign-off
rows.plain: They want to spend less time worrying while waiting for a regulator to explain which requirement applies to the condition for acceptance. This matters because they need a sound basis for approval before signing off.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while awaiting regulatory clarification is an emotional/timing error not central to the mechanics of aseptic dispensing; minor consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of doubt triggered by conflicting sterility-risk findings in the dispensing evidence
rows.plain: Avoid having conflicting signs about germ contamination make people unsure whether the dispensing process is safe. Clear, consistent evidence helps the person buying or approving it trust that the product will not be contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting sterility-risk findings in evidence is an interpretive ambiguity; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting resolution of an aseptic concern about the proposed dispensing approach
rows.plain: They want to spend less time worrying while waiting to resolve a concern that the proposed way of dispensing the product could let in unwanted germs. This matters because they need confidence that the product will not become contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting resolution of aseptic concern is emotional, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to explain whether an intervention creates a contamination pathway
rows.plain: The person wants to explain whether a hands-on change or repair could let harmful contamination enter the process. This matters because even a small opening or mistake could make the product unsafe or unusable.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed unable to explain contamination pathway is emotional, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing credibility with quality leadership or regulators when the recommendation rationale fails cGMP scrutiny
rows.plain: Avoid losing the trust of quality leaders or government inspectors because the reasons for a recommendation do not hold up against strict safety and manufacturing rules. The recommendation should be clearly supported and easy to defend.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when recommendation fails cGMP scrutiny is reputational, distant from dispensing; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which quality leadership bypasses the evaluator in aseptic filling machine decisions due to doubts about regulatory judgment
rows.plain: Make sure quality leaders do not leave out the evaluator when deciding about machines that fill products without exposing them to harmful germs. The evaluator’s regulatory knowledge helps support a safe and lawful choice.
rows.imp_rat: Evaluator bypassed due to doubts about judgment is a trust/role matter, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of public blame for failing to identify a consequential aseptic-compliance concern during machine evaluation
rows.plain: Avoid being publicly blamed for overlooking a serious concern about keeping production sterile while evaluating a machine. Important cleanliness and contamination risks should be found before the machine is approved.
rows.imp_rat: Public blame for missing a compliance concern is reputational, distant from dispensing; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of publicly reversing the machine recommendation under scrutiny from quality leadership or regulators
rows.plain: Avoid having to publicly withdraw a machine recommendation when quality leaders or government inspectors question it. The recommendation should be strong enough to withstand careful review.
rows.imp_rat: Publicly reversing recommendation is a reputational/rework matter, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that site leadership attributes a regulatory observation to an aseptic risk overlooked during machine evaluation
rows.plain: The equipment evaluator wants to avoid site leaders blaming them if inspectors find a cleanliness risk that was missed during the equipment review. They need to identify possible contamination problems before the equipment is approved.
rows.imp_rat: Leadership attributing observation to overlooked risk is a blame framing, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with Quality Assurance and Regulatory Affairs when the contamination-risk rationale fails scrutiny
rows.plain: They want quality and regulatory leaders to keep trusting their judgment when they question the explanation of how contamination could happen. This matters because the safety reasoning must hold up under careful review.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost with QA when rationale fails scrutiny is reputational, distant from dispensing; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the machine-selection committee bypasses the evaluator's contamination-risk criteria in its purchase recommendation
rows.plain: The group choosing a machine should follow the evaluator’s advice about how the machine could introduce dirt or germs. This matters because ignoring those risks could lead to buying equipment that is harder to keep safe.
rows.imp_rat: Committee bypassing contamination-risk criteria is a decision-process matter, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which project sponsors characterize the evaluator as alarmist after contamination concerns are judged overstated
rows.plain: Make sure project sponsors do not dismiss the person warning about contamination as overly worried. Taking those concerns seriously helps prevent contamination problems from being missed.
rows.imp_rat: Being characterized as alarmist is a reputational/perception matter, distant from dispensing; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of perceived independence lost with Quality Assurance and Procurement when the evaluation appears aligned with a preferred supplier
rows.plain: Preserve the appearance and reality of an unbiased evaluation when working with the quality and purchasing teams. The assessment should not seem designed to favor one supplier.
rows.imp_rat: Perceived independence lost with QA/Procurement is a reputational matter, distant; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in acceptance criteria for fault detection, safe response, alarm behavior, and recovery
rows.plain: Make it clear how the system will be judged when it detects a problem, responds safely, gives a warning, and returns to normal. Clear rules make it easier to tell whether the controls work reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in fault-detection acceptance criteria is a specification issue, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguous acceptance criteria are a persistent source of inconsistent pass/fail judgement. Drafting unambiguous acceptance criteria upfront takes negotiation time. Writing clear, testable criteria for detection, response, and recovery is a specialized skill often lacking. Ambiguous criteria cause costly retests but authoring itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of intended recipes, container formats, and operating states absent from fault evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure failure testing covers all planned recipes, container types, and ways the equipment will operate. This helps reveal problems that could occur in real use.
rows.imp_rat: Recipes/formats absent from fault evaluation is a validation-coverage gap, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Practical time limits mean many format and state combinations go unevaluated, leaving real coverage gaps. Covering all recipes, formats, and states in testing greatly extends the test matrix. Deciding the representative coverage set demands seasoned judgement. Broad coverage multiplies test material and machine-time cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of timestamp misalignment across control-event, dispensing, alarm, and audit records
rows.plain: Reduce differences between the recorded times for machine actions, medicine dispensing, warnings, and review records. Matching times makes it easier to understand what happened and whether the system worked properly.
rows.imp_rat: Timestamp misalignment across records is a data-integrity concern, not the physical fill; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Even with time sync, misalignment across control, alarm, and audit systems persists due to differing sources and latencies. Aligning clocks is a one-time setup with little ongoing time. Time synchronization requires standard IT skill. NTP synchronization is essentially free.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in attributing a measured dispensing outcome to control behavior rather than mechanical, pneumatic, or material effects
rows.plain: Make it clear whether a measured amount being dispensed is caused by the control system or by the equipment, air pressure, or material itself. This helps identify the true cause when the result is wrong.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity attributing dispensing outcome to control behavior is a diagnostic ambiguity; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Attribution of dispensing outcomes to control versus physical causes stays ambiguous in many cases. Isolating control-caused effects requires lengthy diagnostic testing. Distinguishing control behavior from mechanical, pneumatic, or material effects demands rare multidisciplinary expertise. Root-cause isolation testing is resource-intensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of repeated fault trials conducted with different initial machine, recipe, or container-flow states
rows.plain: Avoid repeating equipment fault tests under many different starting conditions, such as different settings, mixtures, or material positions. This makes it easier to judge whether the equipment works reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Fault trials with different initial states is a testing-consistency issue, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Repeated trials often start from differing states, weakening comparability of results. Resetting to identical initial states between trials adds setup time. Ensuring consistent initial conditions needs methodical test discipline. Repeated controlled setups add material and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of fault trials lacking evaluation of return to dispensing readiness and the next measured fill
rows.plain: When testing a failure, make sure the review checks whether the equipment can safely return to dispensing and can complete the next measured fill. This shows whether the system works reliably after a problem.
rows.imp_rat: Fault trials lacking return-to-readiness evaluation is a validation-coverage gap; capped low.
