rows.sat_rat: Recovery and subsequent-fill behavior is frequently under-tested, so post-fault readiness remains uncertain. Adding recovery and next-fill checks lengthens each trial. Assessing return-to-readiness needs process expertise. Extended trials consume more machine time and material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recipe governance records are altered or deleted without identification of the responsible person
rows.plain: The adviser wants changes to instructions for making a product to be recorded and traceable to the person who made them. No one should be able to change or erase those records without being identified.
rows.imp_rat: Altered recipe governance records without attribution is a data-integrity issue, ranked below prior batch; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Governance records are usually protected with attributable change history in compliant document systems, so untraceable deletion is uncommon. Tamper-evident governance logging runs continuously without delay. Correct IT configuration rather than user skill governs traceability. Secure logging carries modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical evaluation criteria lacking supporting technical evidence
rows.plain: Make sure the most important points used to judge an option have technical proof behind them. This helps people trust the recommendation.
rows.imp_rat: Evaluation criteria lacking technical evidence is a documentation gap, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Critical criteria frequently rest on assertion rather than traceable evidence, leaving gaps. Gathering supporting evidence for each criterion is time-consuming. Judging evidence sufficiency needs experienced assessors. Evidence generation adds meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evidence represents a machine configuration other than the configuration being recommended
rows.plain: Make sure the supporting information describes the exact machine setup being recommended. Evidence from a different setup could lead to the wrong decision.
rows.imp_rat: Evidence representing wrong configuration is an evaluation-accuracy issue, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence often reflects a configuration different from the one recommended, and this mismatch is easily overlooked. Confirming configuration provenance of evidence adds verification time. Tracking which configuration produced evidence needs configuration-management discipline. Configuration tracking has modest overhead.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of supplier claims about control, recipe, or data-integrity capabilities lacking verifiable support
rows.plain: The person influencing the purchase wants suppliers to make fewer unsupported claims about their ability to control processes, follow recipes, or keep information accurate. This makes the recommendation more trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier claims lacking verifiable support is a procurement-verification issue; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Many supplier capability claims go accepted without verifiable support, a recurring gap. Independently verifying supplier claims lengthens the assessment. Challenging vendor claims requires technical depth many buyers lack. Verification testing or FAT attendance adds cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of broken linkage among evaluation criteria, supporting evidence, and recommendation conclusions
rows.plain: The buyer wants the recommendation to clearly connect the decision standards, the supporting information, and the final conclusion. This matters because others can then see why the recommendation is justified.
rows.imp_rat: Broken linkage among criteria and evidence is a documentation ambiguity; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Broken linkages between evidence and conclusions are a common weakness in assessment reports. Maintaining traceability links is slow when done manually. Building coherent criteria-evidence-conclusion chains needs disciplined analytical skill. Traceability tooling has a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evaluation evidence can be changed without an attributable history
rows.plain: Keep the information used to judge the purchase from being changed without a record showing what was changed and who changed it. This makes the recommendation trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Evidence changeable without attributable history is a data-integrity issue, ranked low here; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Validated document systems give attributable change history for evidence where deployed, but ad hoc files outside them remain vulnerable. Controlled evidence repositories log changes without added time. Proper configuration rather than user skill ensures attributable history. Managed repositories carry a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict between technical records and observed test results
rows.plain: The buyer wants the written technical information to match what the tests actually showed. This matters because differences could make the recommendation unreliable.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between records and test results is a data-consistency issue, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts between documented and observed results recur and often surface late in review. Comparing technical records to observed results is a manual, slow reconciliation. Detecting conflicts needs careful expert review. Reconciliation effort has a recurring labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical technical questions unresolved when the recommendation is due
rows.plain: Resolve more important technical questions before the recommendation is due. This allows the recommendation to be based on a complete understanding of the options.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved technical questions when recommendation due is a delay matter, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Recommendations are frequently issued with unresolved critical questions, undermining confidence. Open critical questions routinely remain when the recommendation deadline arrives, forcing rushed closure. Resolving hard technical questions depends on scarce expert availability. Late unresolved questions drive costly delays or rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that applicable sterile fill-finish and data-integrity requirements are interpreted inconsistently
rows.plain: The advisor wants to avoid different people interpreting the rules for sterile production and trustworthy records in different ways. Consistent interpretation helps the recommendation meet all applicable requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent interpretation of requirements is a compliance ambiguity, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Divergent interpretations across teams and inspectors remain common despite published guidance. Reconciling regulatory interpretations consumes review time. Consistent interpretation of Annex data-integrity guidance depends on scarce regulatory expertise. Misinterpretation causes expensive remediation but analysis is low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that evaluation findings cannot be reproduced under the documented conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the results of the evaluation can be obtained again when people follow the recorded instructions and conditions. This allows others to check whether the conclusion is reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Findings not reproducible under documented conditions is an evaluation-rigor issue, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Findings often fail to reproduce because documented conditions are incomplete, a chronic weakness. Reproducing findings requires repeating full test setups, which is slow. Reproduction depends on precise documentation and skilled execution. Repeat runs consume additional material and machine time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of dispensing-related actions whose recorded timestamps conflict with the execution sequence
rows.plain: Reduce records of dispensing actions whose times do not match the order in which the work actually happened.
rows.imp_rat: Timestamp conflicts on dispensing actions are data-integrity record issues, not the fill execution; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Timestamp-sequence conflicts arise wherever clocks or ordering are not tightly controlled, and remain a common finding. Checking timestamp-versus-sequence consistency is a slow forensic task. Spotting sequence conflicts needs analytical care and system knowledge. The analysis cost is modest but recurring.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required to retrieve and assemble audit evidence for a selected dispensing event
rows.plain: Find and gather the records for a chosen medicine-dispensing event as quickly as possible. This makes it easier to review exactly what happened during that event.
rows.imp_rat: Time to retrieve audit evidence for a dispensing event is a documentation-retrieval error, peripheral to the fill job and low in consequence. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Assembled evidence is usually complete once found, but the effort to compile it remains high. Retrieving and assembling audit evidence for one dispensing event is slow, often spanning multiple systems and manual collation. Knowing where each evidence fragment lives requires system-specific familiarity. The retrieval labor is costly during audits and investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that dispensing audit records become unavailable before the required retention period ends
rows.plain: Keep the records showing that medicines were dispensed available for the full required period. This matters so the organization can later prove what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Audit records unavailable before retention ends is a data-retention issue, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Defined retention and archiving make premature loss uncommon, though media obsolescence and migration risks remain. Retention automation removes ongoing time burden. Retention policy setup needs some records-management knowledge. Long-term storage carries a recurring but manageable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether the aseptic filling machine recommendation can withstand stakeholder scrutiny before budget commitment
rows.plain: Know quickly whether the recommended machine for filling containers without contamination can withstand questions from interested parties before money is committed. This helps the recommender make a well-supported choice.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about whether a recommendation withstands scrutiny is a pre-purchase confidence error, peripheral to the fill job; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when stakeholders challenge the evidence behind the aseptic filling machine recommendation
rows.plain: The recommender needs to stay calm when others question the evidence supporting the choice of a machine that fills products while keeping them free from germs. This helps them explain why the machine is a sound choice.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when challenged on a recommendation is an emotional error, peripheral to the fill job; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of feeling exposed when unable to explain how assessment evidence supports an evaluation criterion
rows.plain: Avoid feeling vulnerable when unable to show how the available proof supports a standard used to compare options. A clear connection between the proof and the standard makes the recommendation easier to defend.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when unable to explain evidence is an emotional/communication error, not central to dispensing; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty when a dispensing fault response supports conflicting contamination-risk interpretations
rows.plain: Quickly resolve disagreement about whether a response to a dispensing mistake could allow contamination. This helps ensure that a possible health or product risk is not overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of uncertainty when fault responses conflict on contamination-risk is an interpretive/timing error, peripheral to executing the fill; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that interactions among control, recipe, and data-integrity behavior were fully considered
rows.plain: The buyer needs confidence that the review considered how the equipment controls, recipe steps, and recorded information affect one another. Missing one of these connections could leave an important risk unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about considering control/recipe interactions is a design-review ambiguity, peripheral; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when the recommendation cannot be defended against a plausible missed-risk scenario
rows.plain: The person wants to show that the recommendation still makes sense even if someone points out a realistic risk that may have been missed. This matters because the decision should account for important dangers rather than relying on false confidence.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed on a missed-risk scenario is an emotional/defensibility error, not central to dispensing execution; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure under an unexpected challenge to the filling system's data integrity
rows.plain: Stay calm if someone unexpectedly questions whether the filling system’s records are accurate and trustworthy. This helps you explain the facts clearly and protect confidence in the process.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure under a data-integrity challenge is an emotional error, not central to the machine's dispensing function; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty when control, recipe, and audit evidence support conflicting conclusions
rows.plain: The team needs to quickly resolve disagreements among the operating information, production instructions, and review records. This helps them reach a decision that can withstand close questioning.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of uncertainty when control/recipe/audit evidence conflict is an evidence-reconciliation error, peripheral to physically dispensing product; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when regulatory scrutiny reveals a gap in the aseptic filling machine evaluation
rows.plain: The person wants a thorough evaluation of the machine that fills products without exposing them to germs, so they can answer questions from inspectors. This matters because a hidden gap could undermine confidence in the machine and the purchase decision.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when scrutiny reveals an evaluation gap is an emotional error, peripheral to physical dispensing; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that costly post-purchase remediation is publicly attributed to this influencer's recommendation by senior stakeholders
rows.plain: The recommender wants to avoid senior leaders publicly blaming them if an expensive fix is needed after the purchase. This matters because the recommendation could affect their credibility and standing.
rows.imp_rat: Public attribution of remediation to a recommendation is a reputational error, not central to dispensing; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost with Quality and Validation when control, recipe, or data-integrity evidence is challenged
rows.plain: Avoid losing trust with the quality team when people question whether the process settings, work instructions, or records are accurate and reliable. This matters because others need to believe the evidence supports the purchase decision.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost with Quality when evidence is challenged is a reputational error, peripheral to the physical fill job; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which purchase decision-makers bypass this influencer's evaluation criteria as insufficiently defensible
rows.plain: Make sure purchasing decision-makers use the evaluator’s safety requirements instead of ignoring them because they seem hard to support with evidence. Clear, well-supported requirements help people choose safer equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Decision-makers bypassing evaluation criteria is a governance/reputational error, not central to sterile dispensing; low consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that Engineering and Quality stakeholders perceive this influencer's supplier recommendations as biased
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that engineering and quality reviewers think the supplier suggestions favor one company unfairly. The recommendations should appear based on facts and risk, not personal preference or hidden interests.
rows.imp_rat: Perceived supplier-recommendation bias is a reputational error with no bearing on sterile dispensing; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that regulatory shortcomings are publicly attributed to this influencer's system recommendation before senior leadership
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that regulators publicly blame this adviser's recommended system for a serious shortcoming in the company's controls. This protects the adviser's standing with senior leaders.
rows.imp_rat: Public attribution of regulatory shortcomings to a recommendation is a reputational error, peripheral to dispensing; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost with quality stakeholders after an endorsed system reveals a batch-data-integrity weakness
rows.plain: Avoid losing trust with quality reviewers after a system that was approved reveals a weakness in the accuracy or reliability of batch information. This matters because decisions about the product depend on trustworthy records.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost after a batch-data-integrity weakness is reputational, peripheral to the physical dispensing job; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which this influencer's reputation for independent judgment is damaged by perceived supplier bias in the evaluation criteria
rows.plain: Protect this person’s reputation for making independent decisions by avoiding evaluation rules that appear to favor a particular supplier. People need to believe the recommendation is based on facts, not favoritism.
rows.imp_rat: Damage to reputation for independent judgment from perceived bias is a personal-reputation error, peripheral to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that senior leadership bypasses this influencer in subsequent aseptic filling machine decisions
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that top leaders stop asking this adviser for input on future machines used to fill products without contamination. This protects the adviser’s credibility.
rows.imp_rat: Leadership bypassing the influencer in future decisions is a reputational error, not central to the CFJ; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of aseptic filling acquisition lifecycle costs omitted from the investment analysis
rows.plain: Include all costs of buying, setting up, using, maintaining, and eventually retiring equipment that fills products under germ-free conditions. This gives a complete picture of the investment cost.
rows.imp_rat: Lifecycle costs omitted from investment analysis is a financial-estimation error; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Inconsistent inclusion of lifecycle costs makes the investment case unreliable across projects. Assembling lifecycle-cost inputs for the investment analysis adds analytical time under deadline pressure. Total-cost-of-ownership modeling for aseptic lines requires specialist financial-technical judgement that is scarce. Lifecycle costs are routinely truncated to acquisition price, understating true spend in the analysis.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which projected cash benefits are overstated by unsupported throughput, yield, labor, and batch-release assumptions
rows.plain: Expected money benefits should not be inflated by unsupported guesses about production speed, usable output, staffing needs, or how quickly batches can be approved for release. More reliable assumptions make the financial plan more credible.
rows.imp_rat: Overstated projected cash benefits is a financial-forecast error unrelated to the physical aseptic fill; low consequence. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Benefit overstatement is a common, hard-to-police bias in investment cases. Validating benefit assumptions extends analysis time but is procedurally known. Distinguishing supportable from optimistic throughput/yield/labor assumptions needs seasoned operational-finance judgement. Overstated benefits misallocate capital, an expensive strategic error.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that forecast sterile fill-finish demand does not support the proposed filling capacity over the investment horizon
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that future demand for sterile filling will be too low to justify the proposed amount of filling equipment over the time the investment is expected to last. This matters because unused capacity could waste a large amount of money.
rows.imp_rat: Demand not supporting capacity over horizon is a capital-forecast error; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Long-range fill-finish demand forecasts are inherently uncertain and often unsupported, making capacity mismatch likely. Building a demand forecast over the investment horizon is slow but a standard planning activity. Long-horizon sterile demand forecasting demands scarce market and clinical-pipeline insight. Overbuilt capacity wastes capital; the forecast error is expensive either way.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which contamination-related batch-rejection losses are understated in the aseptic filling investment analysis
rows.plain: Include a realistic estimate of losses when contamination causes a production batch to be rejected. This prevents the equipment investment from looking less valuable than it really is.
rows.imp_rat: Understated batch-rejection losses in analysis is a financial-estimation error, peripheral to executing the fill; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: These losses are frequently understated or omitted because they are hard to quantify. Quantifying contamination-rejection losses is analytically slow and data-poor. Modeling contamination-related batch-rejection loss requires niche sterility-economics expertise. Understating rejection losses hides real ownership cost from the analysis.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of expected cash-flow erosion from facility-readiness, qualification, and production-ramp delays omitted from the investment analysis
rows.plain: Reduce the expected loss of available money caused by delays in preparing the facility, proving the system works, and starting production when those losses were left out of the investment review. This gives a more realistic picture of the project’s financial impact.
rows.imp_rat: Cash-flow erosion from delays omitted from analysis is a financial-forecast error unrelated to dispensing execution; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Ramp and qualification delays are routinely left out of cash-flow projections. Modeling ramp and readiness-delay cash effects adds analytical time. Estimating delay-driven cash erosion needs combined scheduling and financial skill. Omitting ramp-delay cash effects overstates near-term returns and understates true cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency among financial assumptions used to compare aseptic filling acquisition alternatives
rows.plain: Make sure the financial assumptions used to compare different sterile-filling equipment choices are consistent. This helps leaders see which option is truly the better financial choice.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency in financial assumptions across alternatives is an analysis error unrelated to dispensing execution; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Alternatives are often evaluated with mismatched assumptions, undermining comparability. Harmonizing assumptions across alternatives adds coordination time. Ensuring consistent assumptions requires disciplined financial governance skill. Inconsistent assumptions distort comparisons and may pick the costlier option.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material errors in discounted cash-flow, tax, depreciation, and residual-value calculations
rows.plain: Reduce important mistakes in calculations that account for when money is received or spent, taxes, equipment value, and what remains valuable at the end. This helps show the investment’s true financial value.
rows.imp_rat: Material errors in DCF/tax/depreciation are financial-model errors with no direct effect on dispensing; low relevance. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Finance review catches many errors, so gross mistakes in these calculations are relatively uncommon. Building DCF, tax and depreciation models is time-bounded with standard spreadsheets. Material errors persist because correct DCF, tax, depreciation and residual-value treatment requires specialized finance skill. Calculation errors misstate the case rather than directly waste money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of financial-case approval delay caused by unresolved conflicts among finance, quality, engineering, manufacturing, and procurement
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for approval of the financial case because departments disagree about the money, quality, engineering, production, or purchasing details. This helps the buyer bring the groups to agreement sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Approval delay from unresolved conflicts is a governance-latency error unrelated to dispensing mechanics; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-functional disagreement recurs on most complex aseptic cases, so approval delays are common. Unresolved cross-functional conflicts are a leading cause of financial-case approval delay. Reconciling finance, quality and engineering positions demands facilitation and negotiation skill. Approval delay defers benefits rather than spending money directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage by which aseptic filling capacity utilization falls below the ownership forecast
rows.plain: Keep the use of the sterile medicine filling equipment close to the amount expected over its life. If it is used less than planned, the investment may provide less value than expected.
rows.imp_rat: Utilization falling below forecast is a utilization/financial error unrelated to dispensing execution; low consequence. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Utilization shortfalls are common because demand and scheduling assumptions prove optimistic. Projecting utilization against forecast depends on slow-to-mature demand data. Forecasting capacity utilization needs demand-planning and operations expertise. Utilization below forecast strands fixed capital and raises unit cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of ownership cost attributable to unplanned aseptic filling downtime beyond the lifecycle forecast
rows.plain: Keep ownership costs from rising because sterile filling stops unexpectedly for longer than expected. This helps limit the financial effect of unplanned production interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Ownership cost from unplanned downtime beyond forecast is a cost-estimation error, peripheral to dispensing execution; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Downtime forecasts are optimistic, so actual unplanned downtime routinely overruns. Projecting unplanned downtime cost requires reliability data that takes time to gather. Estimating downtime economics needs reliability-engineering and operations judgement. Downtime beyond forecast is a substantial and commonly underestimated ownership cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of lifecycle cost attributable to sterile product loss and rejected containers beyond the ownership forecast
rows.plain: Include and control the long-term costs of wasted sterile product and rejected containers beyond what was originally expected. This keeps the ownership cost forecast accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Lifecycle cost from product loss beyond forecast is a cost-estimation error, peripheral to dispensing mechanics; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Loss forecasts are uncertain, so actual rejection cost frequently exceeds the ownership estimate. Forecasting product-loss lifecycle cost is analytically time-consuming. Estimating rejected-container loss over ownership needs sterility and yield expertise. Product-loss cost beyond forecast is a real and often understated lifecycle burden.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which maintenance, replacement-part, change-part, and service costs exceed lifecycle forecasts
rows.plain: The costs of upkeep, replacement parts, setup changes, and service should stay close to the long-term estimates. Unexpected extra costs should be kept very small.
rows.imp_rat: Maintenance/service costs exceeding forecast is a cost-overrun error unrelated to dispensing mechanics; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor maintenance estimates are frequently optimistic, so overruns recur. Forecasting maintenance and part costs is a bounded planning exercise. Predicting change-part and service cost trajectories needs OEM lifecycle-cost knowledge. Maintenance and part costs commonly exceed lifecycle forecasts, eroding the business case.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which validation, requalification, and regulatory-support costs exceed lifecycle forecasts
rows.plain: The costs of testing the equipment, checking it again later, and supporting required inspections should stay close to the long-term estimates. Unexpected extra costs should be kept very small.
rows.imp_rat: Validation/requalification costs exceeding forecast is a cost-overrun error unrelated to dispensing execution; low consequence. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Changing regulatory expectations make these costs hard to forecast, so overruns are common. Estimating validation and requalification cost over life adds analysis time. Forecasting regulatory-support and requalification cost needs specialist quality-cost expertise. Requalification and regulatory-support costs regularly exceed lifecycle forecasts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage by which utility and production-consumable spending exceeds ownership assumptions
rows.plain: Keep spending on electricity, other services, and supplies used in production from rising too far above the long-term estimate. This helps control the equipment’s ongoing cost.
rows.imp_rat: Utility/consumable spending exceeding assumptions is a cost-overrun error unrelated to dispensing mechanics; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Consumption estimates are fairly reliable, so deviations are usually modest. Estimating utility and consumable spend is straightforward once specs are known. Utility and consumable forecasting uses standard engineering estimates requiring modest skill. Utility and consumable overruns exist but are a smaller, more predictable lifecycle cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of budget overrun attributable to site preparation, facility integration, commissioning, and startup requirements
rows.plain: Keep costs for preparing the site, connecting the system to the building, testing it, and starting operations from pushing the project over budget. This helps show the true cost of putting the system into use.
rows.imp_rat: Budget overrun from site prep/commissioning is a financial error peripheral to executing sterile dispensing; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Startup overruns recur predictably because these phases are chronically underestimated. Site-prep and commissioning estimation adds planning time that is often rushed. Forecasting commissioning and startup costs needs experienced project-cost engineers. Site-prep, commissioning and startup overruns are among the most common capital-project cost blowouts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the aseptic filling machine becomes obsolete or unsupported before the planned service-life endpoint
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the filling machine becomes outdated or no longer supported by its maker before the planned end of use. This matters because repairs, parts, or help may become difficult to obtain.
rows.imp_rat: Machine obsolescence before service-life endpoint is a lifecycle-planning concern, peripheral to current dispensing; ranked below prior batch, capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors offer support commitments but technology and software obsolescence still frequently outpaces service-life plans. Confirming support-life and obsolescence roadmaps adds modest evaluation time. Reading vendor lifecycle/obsolescence roadmaps needs specialized equipment-lifecycle knowledge. Early obsolescence forces costly upgrades or replacement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between acquired filling capability and forecast container, product, batch-size, throughput, and flexibility requirements
rows.plain: The filling equipment bought should suit the expected containers and products, run the needed production sizes and speeds, and remain flexible enough for future needs. This helps the purchase deliver good value.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between capability and forecast requirements is a procurement-fit error, peripheral to dispensing; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Forecast volatility and cross-functional gaps make capability-to-need mismatch a frequent, poorly-controlled failure. Reconciling forecast container/batch/throughput needs against machine capability takes time given uncertain long-range demand. Translating a fluid product portfolio into filling-flexibility specs demands deep process and marketing knowledge that is hard to assemble. Over- or under-specifying flexibility wastes capital either way, and status quo rarely optimizes this.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the acquired machine fails sterile fill-finish acceptance or qualification criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the purchased machine can pass the checks required for safely filling sterile products. If it cannot, the machine may not be allowed to be used.
rows.imp_rat: Machine failing acceptance criteria is a procurement-quality error, peripheral to the daily fill; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Mature FAT/SAT/PQ protocols and vendor track records make acceptance failure uncommon when standard qualification is followed. Qualification cycles are lengthy but their sequence is well defined by validation practice. Ensuring a machine will pass sterile acceptance relies on specialized validation engineers whose input the purchaser depends on. FAT/SAT and requalification carry cost, yet catching failures early avoids far larger rejection losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of acquisition price paid above comparable compliant supplier offers
rows.plain: Avoid paying more than other suitable suppliers would charge for the same requirement. This helps the buyer get a fair price.
rows.imp_rat: Price paid above comparable offers is a procurement-cost error unrelated to dispensing execution; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Thin supplier pools make true comparability uncertain, so some overpayment risk persists. Benchmarking price against comparable offers takes sourcing time but is standard. Judging comparability of compliant aseptic offers needs procurement market knowledge. Competitive quoting reasonably limits overpayment, though niche aseptic markets have few comparables.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of unplanned spending required to integrate the filling machine with the facility and surrounding production line
rows.plain: Avoid unexpected spending to connect the filling machine to the building and the nearby production equipment. This helps ensure the purchase provides good value.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned integration spending is a facility-cost error with no direct bearing on sterile dispensing; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Integration surprises recur because interfaces are poorly characterized before commitment. Estimating integration effort takes engineering time often under-resourced early. Scoping facility and line integration needs specialist plant-engineering knowledge. Unplanned integration spend is a chronic, frequently underestimated cost overrun.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay between the required production date and qualified availability of the filling capability
rows.plain: Spend less time between the date production is needed and the date the filling equipment is tested and approved for use. This helps make the equipment available when production needs it.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between required date and qualified availability is a schedule issue, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Delivery-date slippage is common as vendor lead times and integration overrun plans. Long lead times and qualification make late availability against required production dates a frequent, hard-to-compress problem. Schedule planning needs project-management skill to align lead times, but the delay itself is structural. Late capability forces expedite fees or lost production revenue.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in supplier scope, acceptance obligations, integration boundaries, and commercial responsibilities at commitment
rows.plain: Make it clear what the supplier will provide, what the buyer must accept, where each side's work begins and ends, and who handles business responsibilities when the deal is made. This reduces unexpected work, costs, and disagreements later.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in supplier scope and commercial responsibilities is a contracting error peripheral to the machine's dispensing function; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scope and boundary ambiguity at commitment is a persistent source of downstream conflict. Nailing down scope and boundaries at commitment lengthens contracting negotiations. Defining integration boundaries and commercial responsibilities demands experienced contract-scoping skill. Ambiguous boundaries drive change orders and disputed costs later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the acquired filling capability becomes unsuitable for the sterile fill-finish portfolio before its planned economic life ends
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the filling equipment no longer fits the site’s range of sterile products before the planned end of its financial life. The purchase should continue to support the products the site makes.
rows.imp_rat: Capability becoming unsuitable before economic life ends is a strategic-portfolio concern, peripheral to the immediate fill job; ranked below prior batch and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Portfolio shifts are unpredictable, so machines becoming unsuitable early is a recurring, poorly-mitigated risk. Judging future portfolio fit over economic life is a slow strategic exercise. Anticipating portfolio evolution against machine flexibility demands scarce strategic-technical foresight. Premature unsuitability strands capital, a large latent cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in contractual criteria for fill accuracy, sterility assurance, throughput, and qualification acceptance
rows.plain: Clearly state in the contract the required filling accuracy, cleanliness, production speed, and standards for accepting the work. This gives both sides the same definition of success.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in contractual acceptance criteria is a procurement-contract matter, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Contracts often still carry vague acceptance language, so ambiguity recurs and surfaces at acceptance disputes. Drafting unambiguous acceptance criteria lengthens contracting but is a known negotiation step. Writing precise fill-accuracy, sterility-assurance and throughput acceptance clauses requires rare combined legal-technical drafting skill. Ambiguity later triggers costly disputes, so upfront specification effort is comparatively cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required machine, integration, qualification, documentation, and training scope omitted from the agreed price
rows.plain: Make sure the agreed price covers the equipment, connections, testing, records, and training that are needed. This prevents essential work from becoming an extra charge later.
rows.imp_rat: Scope omitted from agreed price is a procurement-contract error unrelated to the fill; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Scope-of-supply omissions are a recurring failure because buyers underestimate documentation and training deliverables. Negotiating a fully-scoped price takes iterative vendor rounds that extend contracting. Knowing which qualification, documentation and training items must be in-scope needs experienced procurement of complex equipment. Scope gaps in the agreed price create predictable change orders and unbudgeted spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in responsibility for qualification records, material certificates, validation support, and regulatory documentation
rows.plain: Make clear who is responsible for test records, proof about the materials used, support for showing the equipment works as required, and official paperwork. This helps ensure that no required documents are missing.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in responsibility for qualification records is a documentation-ownership error unrelated to dispensing mechanics; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Responsibility for records and validation support is often ambiguous, causing recurring documentation gaps. Clarifying documentation responsibilities extends contract scoping. Allocating qualification-record and validation-support duties needs quality-contracting expertise. Ambiguity forces rework and duplicated documentation effort rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of payment committed before corresponding contractual acceptance milestones are achieved
rows.plain: Do not pay too much before the supplier has reached the agreed acceptance steps. This protects the buyer if the work or equipment is not yet ready to be accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Payment committed before acceptance milestones is a commercial-control error unrelated to the fill job; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Milestone payment structures are mature and widely enforced, so premature payment exposure is well controlled. Structuring milestone payments is a routine, time-bounded negotiation. Tying payments to acceptance milestones is standard procurement practice requiring modest skill. Milestone-linked payment terms effectively limit money committed ahead of value delivered.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of buyer financial exposure from supplier-caused delay or machine nonconformance left uncovered by contractual remedies
rows.plain: Protect the buyer from financial losses when a supplier causes delays or delivers equipment that does not meet requirements. The contract should provide a clear remedy for these problems.
rows.imp_rat: Uncovered buyer exposure from supplier delay is a contractual-risk error unrelated to dispensing execution; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers resist strong liability caps, so meaningful remedy coverage is often incomplete. Negotiating remedy and liability clauses lengthens contracting. Crafting enforceable delay and nonconformance remedies requires strong legal-commercial skill. Uncovered exposure leaves the buyer bearing delay and defect costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between the contractual completion date and the required production-readiness date
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier's promised finish date allows the plant to be ready to make products when needed. This helps prevent a late delivery from delaying production.
rows.imp_rat: Contract-date vs readiness-date mismatch is a scheduling error, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Contractual dates frequently diverge from real readiness because upstream approvals and facility work slip. Aligning contractual completion to the production-readiness date is often unachievable given supplier backlogs. Negotiating enforceable dates requires scheduling and contracting expertise but the constraint is external capacity. Date mismatch drives penalty clauses or idle downstream investment cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of service, replacement-part, change-part, software, and travel expense left unbounded by the commercial terms
rows.plain: Set clear limits in the contract for service, replacement parts, setup parts, computer programs, and travel costs. This prevents these expenses from growing without control.
rows.imp_rat: Unbounded service/parts expense in terms is a commercial-cost error unrelated to sterile dispensing; low consequence. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Aftermarket cost terms are often left open, so this exposure recurs. Bounding service and parts terms extends contract negotiation. Capping change-part, software and travel costs needs foresight into aftermarket pricing tactics. Unbounded service and change-part expense is a frequent, significant lifecycle cost leak.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of final acquisition price exposed to currency fluctuation, tariffs, escalation, or supplier input-cost changes
rows.plain: Avoid having the final purchase price rise because of changing exchange rates, import charges, general price increases, or higher supplier costs. This makes the price more predictable.
rows.imp_rat: Price exposure to currency/tariffs is a commercial-financial error unrelated to the fill job; low consequence. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard hedging and fixed-price clauses cover much exposure but long lead times leave residual risk. Negotiating currency and escalation terms adds contracting time. Structuring FX, tariff and escalation protection needs specialized commercial-finance skill. Unhedged currency, tariff and escalation exposure can materially inflate final price.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which the requested aseptic filling acquisition budget exceeds the available capital allocation
rows.plain: The requested budget for equipment that fills products without exposing them to germs should not exceed the available investment funds. Any excess should be kept very small.
rows.imp_rat: Acquisition budget exceeding capital allocation is a procurement/financial error, remote from the dispensing CFJ; low on both.
rows.sat_rat: Overruns against allocation remain common because early estimates omit integration and qualification, so the mismatch recurs predictably. Capital allocation figures are known early, so aligning a request to them takes little elapsed time when budgets are set. Estimating equipment cost against a capital ceiling is routine finance work but demands vendor-quote experience to avoid lowballing aseptic complexity. Vendor budgetary quotes and prior-project benchmarks let executors bracket cost reasonably, though aseptic add-ons routinely push requests over allocation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in the financial benefits attributed to the aseptic filling machine acquisition
rows.plain: Make the expected financial gains from buying the sterile-product filling machine as certain as possible. This helps leaders decide whether the machine is worth the investment.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in attributed financial benefits is a financial-forecast error, peripheral to the fill job; low consequence. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Benefit figures are typically point estimates with poorly characterized uncertainty. Reducing benefit uncertainty requires sensitivity analysis that adds time. Quantifying uncertainty in attributed benefits needs advanced financial-modeling skill. Uncertain benefits risk misvalued capital allocation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required financial, technical, quality, and implementation elements missing from the capital request at submission
rows.plain: Include all required cost, design, quality, and implementation details in the capital funding request. This helps reviewers understand what will be bought, how it will work, and how it will be put in place.
rows.imp_rat: Missing elements in capital request is a submission-completeness error unrelated to the fill; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Capital-request checklists exist, yet submissions still arrive with missing quality or implementation elements, triggering rejection loops. Completing all request elements before submission adds preparation time against authorization windows. Assembling financial, technical, quality and implementation content requires coordinating multiple expert contributors. Missing elements cause rework cycles but not direct large spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of total acquisition and lifecycle costs omitted or underestimated in the capital request
rows.plain: Include realistic estimates for all costs of buying, using, maintaining, and eventually replacing the equipment. This prevents the funding request from being too low.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted lifecycle costs in capital request is a financial-estimation error; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Standard capital templates catch major items but consistently miss integration, change-parts and requalification costs. Building a full lifecycle cost view is time-consuming and often compressed by approval deadlines. Capturing all lifecycle cost elements demands experienced cost engineers who know hidden aseptic ownership costs. Omitted or underestimated costs are a chronic problem that distorts the capital request and creates later overruns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved quality, sterility-assurance, data-integrity, and regulatory concerns cited during authorization review
rows.plain: Resolve quality, cleanliness, record-accuracy, and legal concerns before the request for funding is reviewed. This helps reviewers approve the investment with confidence.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved concerns cited during authorization review are governance-administrative errors peripheral to physical sterile dispensing; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Concerns are often carried into review unresolved, so this is a recurring authorization friction point. Resolving quality and regulatory concerns before authorization can prolong review substantially. Closing sterility, data-integrity and regulatory concerns requires cross-functional expert judgement. Unresolved concerns delay approval more than they cost money directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in facility modifications, utilities, shutdown needs, and production disruption associated with the acquisition
rows.plain: Reduce uncertainty about changes to the building, supporting services, production stoppages, and other disruptions needed to install the equipment. This helps estimate the full effect and cost of buying it.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in facility modifications and disruption is a planning error unrelated to the machine's dispensing function; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Facility and shutdown impacts are routinely under-characterized, leaving significant uncertainty. Assessing facility modifications, utilities and shutdown needs is a lengthy pre-planning task. Predicting production disruption requires cross-disciplinary facilities and operations expertise. Uncertain modification needs translate into contingency-heavy budgets or overruns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of acquisition-scope changes requiring capital-request revision during authorization review
rows.plain: Avoid changing what is being bought while the funding request is being reviewed. Stable plans make it easier to get the needed capital approved.
rows.imp_rat: Scope changes requiring capital-request revision is a governance-process error unrelated to dispensing execution; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Late scope changes are frequent because requirements are immature at submission. Scope changes during review cause repeated capital-request revisions that delay authorization. Freezing scope before submission requires disciplined requirements-management skill. Revision churn consumes staff effort more than direct money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the aseptic filling capital request misses the applicable budget authorization window
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the request for money to buy the filling equipment misses the period when it can be approved. This matters because the project could be delayed until the next funding cycle.
rows.imp_rat: Missing the budget authorization window is a timing/administrative error unrelated to dispensing execution; low consequence. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Known budget cycles make window-timing fairly manageable, though late-maturing requests still occasionally slip. Hitting the budget authorization window is largely about calendar discipline and is often achievable. Timing a request to the budget cycle needs planning skill but is well understood. Missing a window delays funding and can raise costs but is not itself a spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a selected supplier cannot satisfy sterile fill-finish quality, data-integrity, and regulatory requirements
rows.plain: The chosen supplier should be able to make sterile products, keep accurate and trustworthy records, and meet all required rules. Choosing one that cannot do these things could put the whole project at risk.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier inability to meet quality requirements matters but sits in procurement, ranked below prior batch; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Established supplier-qualification and audit programs materially reduce, but do not eliminate, the chance a chosen supplier falls short on sterility or data integrity. Vendor qualification and audits stretch schedules, but standard supplier-assessment cycles are established. Judging a supplier's fill-finish, data-integrity and regulatory competence requires scarce quality-engineering expertise the purchaser must borrow. Audits and pre-qualification carry cost but are far cheaper than a failed sterile supplier.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required supplier qualification evidence that is incomplete, outdated, or unverifiable
rows.plain: Make sure the evidence that a supplier is suitable is complete, current, and possible to verify. This helps the organization choose suppliers it can rely on.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete supplier qualification evidence is a procurement-documentation gap; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier qualification dossiers are often outdated or partial, leaving unverifiable gaps. Chasing complete, current qualification evidence lengthens due diligence. Verifying that supplier evidence is valid and current needs quality-audit expertise. Incomplete evidence rarely costs money directly but risks expensive downstream failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material supplier capability claims that conflict with aseptic filling acquisition requirements
rows.plain: Make sure suppliers' claims about what they can provide match the requirements for buying equipment that fills products under germ-free conditions. This helps identify suppliers that can truly meet the need.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier claims conflicting with requirements is a procurement-verification issue; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier claims are frequently taken at face value, so conflicts with requirements surface late. Cross-checking supplier claims against requirements adds evaluation time but is bounded. Detecting inflated capability claims requires technical due-diligence skill the purchaser often lacks alone. Unverified claims cause costly post-award surprises rather than immediate spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that relevant contamination-control, data-integrity, or regulatory incidents remain undisclosed during supplier qualification
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that important incidents involving contamination, unreliable records, or regulatory compliance are hidden during supplier checks. The company needs the full history before choosing a supplier.
rows.imp_rat: Undisclosed supplier incidents during qualification is a procurement-transparency issue; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers may not disclose adverse incidents and audits cannot guarantee detection, so this remains poorly assured. Uncovering undisclosed incidents during qualification can be slow and open-ended. Probing for hidden contamination or data-integrity history requires investigative audit expertise. Undisclosed incidents cost little to check but a lot if they surface post-award.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a qualified supplier lacks engineering or manufacturing capacity for the required delivery schedule
rows.plain: A supplier may meet the required standards but still need enough design and production ability to deliver on time. The buyer needs to avoid choosing a supplier that cannot meet the schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier lacking capacity for delivery schedule is a procurement-timing issue; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier capacity claims are hard to verify and backlog surprises make schedule shortfalls fairly common. Verifying a supplier's capacity for the schedule adds assessment time but is feasible. Assessing engineering and manufacturing capacity requires operational insight into the vendor's order book. Capacity shortfalls cause expensive delay rather than direct purchase cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that supplier financial instability disrupts fulfillment of the aseptic filling machine acquisition
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a supplier’s money problems interrupt delivery of the machine. The equipment should still arrive as promised.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier financial instability disrupting acquisition is an upstream procurement risk, peripheral to executing the aseptic fill itself; ranked below the prior batch and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard financial due-diligence gives decent early warning, so instability disrupting fulfillment is moderately guarded against. Financial-stability checks are quick with standard credit and reporting tools. Interpreting supplier financial health needs basic financial-analysis skill widely available. Credit reports are inexpensive relative to disruption risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which a supplier cannot support qualification, maintenance, technical service, and replacement-part needs
rows.plain: Choose suppliers that can help prove the equipment meets requirements, keep it working, provide technical help, and supply replacement parts. This reduces the risk of being left without support after purchase.
rows.imp_rat: Supplier unable to support service needs is a procurement-fit issue, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Established OEMs provide fairly dependable service and parts support, so this need is moderately well covered. Assessing lifetime service and parts support extends supplier evaluation modestly. Judging aftermarket service capability requires knowledge of the supplier's field network and part availability. Weak support translates into higher lifecycle service and downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical design, fabrication, software, or qualification work is assigned to unqualified or undisclosed subcontractors
rows.plain: The buyer wants important design, building, software, and testing work to be done only by capable companies whose involvement is known and approved. This matters so hidden or unqualified workers do not create serious problems.
rows.imp_rat: Critical work assigned to undisclosed subcontractors is a procurement-quality risk, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Subcontractor visibility is typically weak, so unqualified hidden subcontractors are a real, under-detected risk. Mapping subcontractor assignments takes extra diligence time often skipped under pressure. Identifying critical outsourced design/software work needs deep supply-chain scrutiny skill. Undisclosed subcontracting rarely shows in price but risks costly quality failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether negotiated scope, delivery, acceptance, and warranty obligations remain enforceable when execution issues arise
rows.plain: Know quickly whether the agreed work, delivery, approval checks, and repair promises can still be enforced when problems occur. This helps protect the buyer if the project does not go as planned.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about enforceability of obligations is a contractual-risk error unrelated to dispensing execution; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt about buyer and supplier accountability under exceptions, exclusions, and qualifiers in the negotiated terms
rows.plain: The agreement should make clear who is responsible when unusual situations, exceptions, or limits arise. This helps both the buyer and supplier be held to their promises.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about accountability under contract exclusions is a contracting-clarity error unrelated to dispensing execution; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of alarm when delivery, acceptance, or warranty commitments lack adequate contractual recourse
rows.plain: Avoid worry when promises about delivery, acceptance, or repairs are not backed by enforceable contract terms. Strong agreements provide options if the other party does not deliver as promised.
rows.imp_rat: Alarm when commitments lack recourse is an emotional/contractual error unrelated to dispensing mechanics; low consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty that the selected supplier can satisfy GMP, data-integrity, and regulatory expectations during the procurement decision
rows.plain: Quickly confirm that the chosen supplier can meet required quality, recordkeeping, and legal standards before the purchase is made. This helps avoid choosing a supplier that could create compliance problems.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about supplier GMP fitness during procurement is peripheral to the fill act; capped low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when quality or regulatory stakeholders challenge the evidence supporting supplier selection
rows.plain: The person wants clear evidence for why a supplier was chosen when quality or government-compliance reviewers question the decision. This matters because weak or missing evidence could make the choice appear unsafe or improper.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when challenged on supplier-selection evidence is an emotional error, peripheral to the fill job; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety when supplier claims conflict with available GMP, data-integrity, or regulatory evidence
rows.plain: They want fewer sudden worries when a supplier’s claims do not match available evidence about required manufacturing, trustworthy records, or legal compliance. This matters because choosing the supplier could otherwise create serious compliance problems.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety when supplier claims conflict with GMP evidence is an emotional/informational error unrelated to dispensing execution; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether the required filling capability can be acquired within the approved capital envelope
rows.plain: Know quickly whether the needed machine for filling containers can be bought within the approved investment budget. This helps prevent the purchase from exceeding the available funds.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about capital acquisition is a procurement-phase concern well removed from dispensing; capped low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of losing composure when leadership challenges the cost, contingency, or benefit assumptions behind the capital commitment
rows.plain: Make it easier to stay calm when leaders question the expected cost, backup plans, or benefits behind a major purchase. This helps the buyer defend the request with sound reasoning.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when challenged on capital assumptions is an emotional error unrelated to dispensing mechanics; low consequence. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when supplier, engineering, and finance estimates cannot be reconciled before capital commitment
rows.plain: The person wants the supplier’s, technical team’s, and finance team’s cost estimates to agree before committing the money. This matters because conflicting estimates could lead to overspending or an unreliable purchase decision.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when estimates cannot be reconciled is an emotional/analysis error unrelated to dispensing execution; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that senior leadership attributes a surprise funding request to the purchase executor's inadequate capital diligence
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that top leaders blame the buyer if an unexpected request for more money comes later. This means checking the full cost carefully before approving the purchase.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of a surprise funding request to inadequate diligence is a reputational/administrative error unrelated to dispensing mechanics; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility loss with senior leadership when a filling-capability shortfall is attributed to the purchase executor's procurement decision
rows.plain: Protect trust with senior leaders by making sure a decision to buy equipment does not leave the site unable to fill products in the needed amount. The purchase should support the promised production ability.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss from a capability shortfall attributed to procurement is a reputational error unrelated to dispensing execution; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which finance or leadership bypasses the purchase executor when acquisition assumptions are disputed
rows.plain: Finance or senior leaders should go around the person responsible for purchases less often when they disagree about the assumptions behind a buying decision. This helps keep investment decisions consistent and accountable.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed when assumptions are disputed is a reputational error unrelated to dispensing execution; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quality, regulatory, or manufacturing stakeholders attribute supplier compliance exposure to the purchase executor
rows.plain: The buyer wants to avoid being held responsible if a supplier fails to meet required quality or legal standards. Clear decisions and records help show that the supplier's responsibilities were understood.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of supplier compliance exposure to the executor is a reputational error, peripheral to the fill job; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility loss when the purchase executor's supplier-compliance assurances fail quality or regulatory scrutiny
rows.plain: Protect the buyer's reputation by making sure promises about the supplier's compliance with rules can withstand quality and regulatory review. If those promises fail, people may no longer trust the buyer's judgment.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss when compliance assurances fail scrutiny is a reputational error unrelated to dispensing mechanics; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the purchase executor is bypassed in aseptic equipment sourcing because stakeholders distrust the buyer's compliance judgment
rows.plain: The buyer should be included more often when choosing equipment for work that must stay free from germs. This matters because trusting the buyer’s judgment can help ensure the purchase meets safety and rule requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed due to distrust of compliance judgment is a reputational error unrelated to dispensing execution; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of divergence between the investment-case demand forecast and the site's long-term capacity assumptions
rows.plain: The expected need used to justify the investment should fit with the site's long-term ability to produce enough. This helps ensure the investment supports future plans instead of working against them.
rows.imp_rat: Divergence between demand forecast and capacity assumptions is a planning error unrelated to the fill job; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Case-versus-site-plan divergence occurs regularly and is only partially reconciled. Reconciling investment-case and site capacity assumptions is a planning-stage step. Aligning demand forecasts with site capacity plans needs cross-functional analytical judgement. Divergent assumptions risk mis-sized investment and stranded or short capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of the planned sterile-product and container portfolio that the proposed filling capability cannot accommodate
rows.plain: Make sure the proposed filling equipment can handle the planned range of germ-free products and containers. This prevents buying equipment that cannot support the intended production needs.
rows.imp_rat: Portfolio the capability cannot accommodate touches fitness but is framed as a coverage-defect peripheral to executing the current fill; capped below prior batch at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Format/container compatibility is usually assessed but edge-case portfolio items still get missed. Checking portfolio-fit against filling capability is done during specification, not urgent. Assessing product/container compatibility needs fill-finish engineering expertise typically consulted. Uncovered portfolio items strand capacity or force additional investment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of forecast sterile fill-finish demand exceeding the usable capacity funded by the investment
rows.plain: Avoid planning to make more sterile medicines than the investment will actually allow the facility to produce. The funded equipment should be able to handle the expected demand.
rows.imp_rat: Demand exceeding funded capacity is a capital-sizing error unrelated to the fill; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Usable-capacity estimation is imperfect and demand overshoot detection is only moderately reliable pre-authorization. Comparing forecast demand to usable funded capacity is a planning activity with modest time cost. Distinguishing usable from nameplate capacity against demand needs analytical judgement often provided by planning teams. Demand exceeding funded usable capacity forfeits saleable revenue the case counted on.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that planned throughput cannot be sustained without compromising aseptic conditions
rows.plain: The buyer wants the planned production level to be achievable without losing the conditions needed to keep the work sterile. This matters because producing more than the process can safely handle could threaten product quality.
rows.imp_rat: Throughput unsustainable without compromising asepsis is closer to CFJ but ranked below prior batch; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Nameplate throughput often overstates aseptically sustainable output, and this gap is only partially probed before authorization. Throughput-versus-sterility trade-offs emerge during ramp, not at the fast decision moment. Judging whether rated throughput is sustainable under aseptic constraints needs process-engineering expertise beyond the executor's scope. Under-delivered sustainable throughput undermines the volume assumptions the return depends on.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the gap between required production ramp-up and availability of qualified filling capacity
rows.plain: The buyer wants filling equipment that has passed all required checks to be available sooner after production needs increase. This helps the site reach higher production levels without waiting for equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Gap between ramp-up need and qualified capacity is a scheduling error, peripheral to the machine's dispensing function; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Qualified-capacity timing frequently lags demand ramp, and mitigation is inconsistent. The gap between ramp-up need and qualified-capacity availability is a common and painful schedule slip. Aligning capacity readiness to ramp needs project-scheduling expertise that is available but strained. A readiness gap delays revenue and may force costly interim measures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that site utilities, cleanroom space, or staffing cannot support planned filling capacity
rows.plain: The purchase executor wants to avoid planning for more production than the site’s utilities, clean areas, or available workers can support. Investments should match what the site can realistically produce.
rows.imp_rat: Utilities/staffing unable to support capacity is an infrastructure-fit error, peripheral; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Site-readiness assessment is a fairly mature practice, though staffing and utility constraints still occasionally get underestimated. Utility, cleanroom, and staffing readiness checks are part of standard site assessment and add manageable time. Confirming site support requires facilities-engineering expertise that established site-readiness reviews usually mobilize. Unsupported capacity wastes the machine investment and forces unplanned enabling spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of funded filling capacity expected to remain idle over the strategic planning horizon
rows.plain: Make sure the filling capacity paid for is not expected to sit unused during the long-term planning period. This helps match the investment to actual production needs.
rows.imp_rat: Idle funded capacity over the horizon is a strategic-utilization error, peripheral to the fill job; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Long-horizon idle-capacity forecasts are unreliable, so this risk is weakly controlled. Assessing long-horizon idle capacity is a strategic planning task, not time-critical. Projecting strategic-horizon utilization needs long-range forecasting expertise that is inherently uncertain. Persistently idle funded capacity locks up capital unproductively.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that manufacturing-network reallocations make funded filling capacity redundant
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that moving production work between sites makes newly funded filling equipment unnecessary. Investment should support the amount of production the network will actually need.
rows.imp_rat: Network reallocations making capacity redundant is a strategic error, peripheral to the fill job; low stakes. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Network-driven redundancy is a real strategic risk that is inconsistently factored into cases. Network reallocation effects emerge over strategic horizons, not immediately. Anticipating manufacturing-network changes needs enterprise-level strategic insight. Redundant capacity from reallocation wastes the investment entirely.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between available capacity expansion increments and the site's staged growth plan
rows.plain: Make sure the planned increases in production ability match the site's growth schedule, so the company does not spend too much too soon or fall behind demand.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between expansion increments and growth plan is a capacity-planning error, peripheral to the fill job; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Increment-to-growth mismatch is a recognized but inconsistently managed risk. Matching expansion increments to staged growth is a planning consideration, not a timeline risk. Aligning capacity increments to growth stages needs capacity-planning expertise of mixed availability. Mismatched increments force premature over-investment or later expensive re-buys.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of facility, integration, qualification, and contingency spending excluded from the total capital requirement
rows.plain: Avoid leaving out the costs of preparing the facility, connecting the equipment, proving that it works properly, and handling unexpected problems. This helps show how much money the project will actually require.
rows.imp_rat: Excluded facility/qualification spending is a capital-estimation error unrelated to dispensing; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: These 'soft' and enabling costs are systematically excluded or under-scoped in early cases, making this a chronic and predictable understatement. Assembling facility, integration, qualification, and contingency costs takes time and often lags the headline machine quote. Knowing which ancillary and integration costs to include for aseptic installations demands specialist estimating experience frequently underused. Omitted facility, integration, qualification, and contingency spend is a classic driver of capital blowouts the executor ends up absorbing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation between assumed aseptic filling utilization and supportable production demand
rows.plain: Keep the expected use of sterile filling equipment close to the amount of production demand that can actually be supported. This matters because financial plans should be based on realistic production needs, not optimistic assumptions.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between assumed utilization and demand is a forecasting error, peripheral to dispensing execution; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Utilization assumptions often deviate from real supportable demand, moderately controlled. Reconciling assumed utilization with supportable demand is a planning task, not a delay driver. Judging supportable utilization needs demand and operations expertise shared across functions. Overstated utilization overstates returns and risks funding excess capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of projected financial benefit lacking evidence or counted more than once
rows.plain: Base expected financial benefits on supporting evidence and count each benefit only once. This makes the business case more credible.
rows.imp_rat: Benefit lacking evidence or double-counted is a financial-analysis error; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Benefit inflation and double-counting are common and only partially caught by standard case review. Validating and de-duplicating projected benefits adds review time before sign-off. Detecting double-counted or unevidenced benefits requires financial scrutiny skill that varies by reviewer. Overstated benefits inflate the case and risk funding a value-negative project.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of aseptic filling lifecycle operating costs omitted from the financial case
rows.plain: Include all ongoing costs of running equipment that fills products under germ-free conditions in the financial case. This helps avoid understating the cost of operating it.
rows.imp_rat: Lifecycle operating costs omitted from the financial case is an accounting-completeness error, peripheral to the fill job; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Operating-cost omissions are common in financial cases, making this weakly served. Including full lifecycle operating cost lengthens case preparation. Comprehensive lifecycle operating cost modeling requires specialized TCO skill often missing. Omitted operating costs understate lifetime spend and inflate apparent returns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of misstatement of financial exposure from contamination events, rejected batches, and regulatory interruption
rows.plain: Make sure the financial risk from contamination, thrown-away batches, or a shutdown ordered by regulators is estimated accurately. This helps leaders understand the true cost of the decision.
rows.imp_rat: Misstatement of financial exposure from contamination events is a financial-accounting error, not a direct dispensing control; low relevance to the CFJ. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: These exposures are notoriously hard to quantify and are routinely misstated, a chronic weakness. Estimating contamination, batch-loss, and regulatory-interruption exposure is slow and often omitted for expediency. Accurately stating this exposure requires scarce combined sterile-quality and financial-risk expertise. Misstated exposure hides large potential losses that fall on the business.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of understatement of the gap between capital commitment and validated aseptic production
